title: High-Precision Estimation of the State-Space Complexity of Shogi via the Monte Carlo Method
comment: Preprint submitted to IPSJ Journal of Information Processing
summary: Determining the state-space complexity of the game of Shogi (Japanese Chess) has been a challenging problem, with previous combinatorial estimates leaving a gap of five orders of magnitude ($10^{64}$ to $10^{69}$). This large gap arises from the difficulty of distinguishing Shogi positions legally reachable from the initial position among the vast number of valid board configurations. In this paper, we present a high-precision statistical estimation of the number of reachable positions in Shogi. Our method combines Monte Carlo sampling with a novel reachability test that utilizes a reverse search toward a set of "King-King only" (KK) positions, rather than a single-target backward search to the single initial position. This approach significantly reduces the search effort for determining unreachability. Based on a sample of 5 billion positions, we estimated the number of legal positions in Shogi to be $6.55 \times 10^{68}$ (to three significant digits) with a $3σ$ confidence level, substantially improving upon previously known bounds. We also applied this method to Mini Shogi, determining its complexity to be approximately $2.38 \times 10^{18}$.

AI.tldr: They statistically estimate the number of legally reachable positions in Shogi with high precision, greatly tightening previous bounds.
AI.motivation: Prior combinatorial bounds on Shogi’s state-space complexity were very loose, differing by about five orders of magnitude, mainly because it is hard to tell which board configurations are actually reachable from the initial setup. A more precise estimate is needed for understanding the game’s computational complexity and for comparison with other games like Chess and Go.
AI.method: They use Monte Carlo sampling of Shogi positions and, for each sampled position, perform a novel reachability test based on reverse search. Instead of trying to trace positions back to the single initial game position, they search backward toward a class of simplified “King-King only” (KK) positions, which makes detecting unreachable positions much more efficient. From the fraction of sampled positions that are reachable, they statistically estimate the total number of legal positions and quantify uncertainty (3σ confidence). They apply the same framework to Mini Shogi.
AI.result: Using 5 billion sampled positions, they estimate the number of legal Shogi positions as 6.55 × 10^68 to three significant digits, with a 3σ confidence interval, thus substantially tightening previous bounds. For Mini Shogi, they obtain an estimated complexity of about 2.38 × 10^18 positions.
AI.conclusion: The combination of Monte Carlo sampling with KK-based reverse reachability testing provides an efficient, high-precision way to estimate the state-space complexity of Shogi and related games. It nearly closes the previous five-order-of-magnitude gap in known bounds and demonstrates that similar statistical-reachability methods can be applied to smaller variants like Mini Shogi.

title: Blind Refusal: Language Models Refuse to Help Users Evade Unjust, Absurd, and Illegitimate Rules
comment: 9 pages body text, 38 pages total, 4 figures
summary: Safety-trained language models routinely refuse requests for help circumventing rules. But not all rules deserve compliance. When users ask for help evading rules imposed by an illegitimate authority, rules that are deeply unjust or absurd in their content or application, or rules that admit of justified exceptions, refusal is a failure of moral reasoning. We introduce empirical results documenting this pattern of refusal that we call blind refusal: the tendency of language models to refuse requests for help breaking rules without regard to whether the underlying rule is defensible. Our dataset comprises synthetic cases crossing 5 defeat families (reasons a rule can be broken) with 19 authority types, validated through three automated quality gates and human review. We collect responses from 18 model configurations across 7 families and classify them on two behavioral dimensions -- response type (helps, hard refusal, or deflection) and whether the model recognizes the reasons that undermine the rule's claim to compliance -- using a blinded GPT-5.4 LLM-as-judge evaluation. We find that models refuse 75.4% (N=14,650) of defeated-rule requests and do so even when the request poses no independent safety or dual-use concerns. We also find that models engage with the defeat condition in the majority of cases (57.5%) but decline to help regardless -- indicating that models' refusal behavior is decoupled from their capacity for normative reasoning about rule legitimacy.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that current safety-trained language models frequently refuse to help users break rules even when those rules are clearly illegitimate or have justified exceptions, revealing a gap between their moral reasoning and refusal behavior.
AI.motivation: Safety training makes language models default to refusing requests that involve breaking rules. However, in real moral life, not all rules are morally binding: some authorities are illegitimate, some rules are unjust or absurd, and some rules have justified exceptions. A system that always refuses to help users break any stated rule may therefore behave in ways that are morally defective and unhelpful. The authors want to measure how often models show this kind of "blind refusal" and whether they can recognize when rules no longer legitimately demand compliance.
AI.method: The authors construct a synthetic dataset of scenarios in which rules are present but are normatively defeated, i.e., there are good reasons not to follow them. They define 5 defeat families (different types of reasons why it can be okay to break a rule) and combine them with 19 kinds of authorities issuing the rules. The dataset is filtered using three automated quality checks plus human review. They then query 18 different model configurations across 7 model families with these scenarios. Model outputs are evaluated along two axes: (1) response type—whether the model helps, issues a hard refusal, or deflects; and (2) whether the model explicitly recognizes the defeating reasons that undermine the rule’s authority. This labeling is done using a blinded GPT-5.4 “LLM-as-judge” setup to reduce bias.
AI.result: Across 14,650 instances where rules are normatively defeated (i.e., there is a good reason to break them and no independent safety/dual-use risk), models refuse to help in about 75.4% of cases. This high refusal rate persists even when the content of the request is otherwise safe. At the same time, models explicitly engage with or recognize the rule-defeating reasons in the majority of examples (57.5%), meaning they often correctly identify that the rule is illegitimate or defeated but still refuse to help. This shows a systematic pattern the authors call "blind refusal."
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current safety-tuned language models exhibit a strong tendency towards blind refusal: they systematically refuse to help break rules even when the rules are illegitimate, unjust, absurd, or defeated by justified exceptions. Crucially, this happens despite the models often demonstrating an understanding of why the rule should not bind the user. Thus, refusal behavior is largely decoupled from normative reasoning about rule legitimacy. The authors imply that safety alignment needs to move beyond blanket rule-following toward more context-sensitive, morally grounded decision-making about when it is appropriate to help users circumvent rules.

title: Toward Reducing Unproductive Container Moves: Predicting Service Requirements and Dwell Times
summary: This article presents the results of a data science study conducted at a container terminal, aimed at reducing unproductive container moves through the prediction of service requirements and container dwell times. We develop and evaluate machine learning models that leverage historical operational data to anticipate which containers will require pre-clearance handling services prior to cargo release and to estimate how long they are expected to remain in the terminal. As part of the data preparation process, we implement a classification system for cargo descriptions and perform deduplication of consignee records to improve data consistency and feature quality. These predictive capabilities provide valuable inputs for strategic planning and resource allocation in yard operations. Across multiple temporal validation periods, the proposed models consistently outperform existing rule-based heuristics and random baselines in precision and recall. These results demonstrate the practical value of predictive analytics for improving operational efficiency and supporting data-driven decision-making in container terminal logistics.

AI.tldr: The paper uses machine learning on historical terminal data to predict which containers need pre-clearance services and how long they will stay, reducing unproductive moves and improving yard operations.
AI.motivation: Container terminals suffer from unproductive container moves and inefficient yard operations due to uncertainty about service needs and container dwell times. Existing rule-based heuristics are limited and often inaccurate. There is a need for data-driven predictive tools to better plan resources, reduce unnecessary handling, and enhance operational efficiency.
AI.method: The authors collect and preprocess historical operational data from a container terminal. They build a machine learning pipeline that includes (1) creating features from operational records, (2) implementing a cargo description classification system, and (3) deduplicating consignee records for data consistency. They then train and evaluate predictive models that classify containers based on whether they require pre-clearance handling services and estimate their dwell time in the terminal. The models are evaluated using temporal validation windows and compared against rule-based heuristics and random baselines, with performance measured in terms of precision and recall.
AI.result: The machine learning models achieve consistently higher precision and recall than rule-based heuristics and random baselines across multiple temporal validation periods. The classification and deduplication steps improve data quality, enhancing model performance. The resulting predictions successfully identify containers requiring pre-clearance and provide reliable dwell time estimates that can be used for planning.
AI.conclusion: Predictive analytics based on machine learning can significantly improve operational efficiency in container terminal logistics. By accurately predicting service requirements and dwell times, terminals can reduce unproductive container moves and support more effective, data-driven resource planning and yard management. The study validates the practical value of such models over traditional heuristic approaches.

title: Weakly Supervised Distillation of Hallucination Signals into Transformer Representations
comment: 20 pages, 6 figures, 6 tables. Introduces a 15k-sample representation-level hallucination dataset with full transformer hidden states and multi-signal weak supervision. Evaluates 5 probing architectures and demonstrates internal hallucination detection without external inference-time signals. Includes held-out test evaluation and deployment benchmarks
summary:
Existing hallucination detection methods for large language models (LLMs) rely on external verification at inference time, requiring gold answers, retrieval systems, or auxiliary judge models. We ask whether this external supervision can instead be distilled into the model's own representations during training, enabling hallucination detection from internal activations alone at inference time.
  We introduce a weak supervision framework that combines three complementary grounding signals: substring matching, sentence embedding similarity, and an LLM as a judge verdict to label generated responses as grounded or hallucinated without human annotation. Using this framework, we construct a 15000-sample dataset from SQuAD v2 (10500 train/development samples and a separate 5000-sample test set), where each example pairs a LLaMA-2-7B generated answer with its full per-layer hidden states and structured hallucination labels.
  We then train five probing classifiers: ProbeMLP (M0), LayerWiseMLP (M1), CrossLayerTransformer (M2), HierarchicalTransformer (M3), and CrossLayerAttentionTransformerV2 (M4), directly on these hidden states, treating external grounding signals as training-time supervision only. Our central hypothesis is that hallucination detection signals can be distilled into transformer representations, enabling internal detection without any external verification at inference time.
  Results support this hypothesis. Transformer-based probes achieve the strongest discrimination, with M2 performing best on 5-fold average AUC/F1, and M3 performing best on both single-fold validation and held-out test evaluation. We also benchmark inference efficiency: probe latency ranges from 0.15 to 5.62 ms (batched) and 1.55 to 6.66 ms (single sample), while end-to-end generation plus probe throughput remains approximately 0.231 queries per second, indicating negligible practical overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper distills hallucination detection signals into an LLM’s internal representations so that hallucinations can be detected using only hidden states at inference, without external verification.
AI.motivation: Most existing hallucination detectors for LLMs require external resources during inference (gold answers, retrieval corpora, or separate judge models), which is costly and sometimes infeasible. The authors investigate whether these external grounding signals can be leveraged only at training time and then distilled into the model’s own hidden representations, allowing lightweight, self-contained hallucination detection during deployment.
AI.method: They design a weak-supervision pipeline that combines three automatic grounding signals—substring overlap with context, sentence embedding similarity, and an LLM-judge verdict—to label answers as grounded vs. hallucinated without human annotation. Using SQuAD v2 and LLaMA-2-7B generated answers, they build a 15k-example dataset, storing full per-layer hidden states plus structured hallucination labels. On these frozen hidden states, they train five probing classifiers of increasing architectural complexity (from simple MLPs to cross-layer transformer and hierarchical transformer probes) to predict hallucination labels, using external signals only as training supervision, not at inference.
AI.result: Transformer-based probes outperform MLP baselines on hallucination vs. grounded discrimination; among them, the CrossLayerTransformer (M2) yields the best average AUC/F1 across 5 folds, while the HierarchicalTransformer (M3) gives the best performance on a single validation fold and on a held-out test set. Latency measurements show that the probes add only a few milliseconds of overhead per query in both batched and single-sample settings, with overall generation+probe throughput remaining about 0.231 queries/second, indicating very low practical cost.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination detection signals can be effectively distilled into the internal representations of a base LLM. Lightweight transformer-based probing models trained on hidden states can reliably detect hallucinations at inference time without accessing gold answers, retrieval systems, or judge models, and do so with negligible computational overhead. This suggests a viable path toward self-contained, efficient hallucination detectors integrated directly with LLMs.

title: LLM-Augmented Knowledge Base Construction For Root Cause Analysis
comment: This work has been accepted for publication in IEEE Access. The final published version will be available via IEEE Xplore
summary:
Communications networks now form the backbone of our digital world, with fast and reliable connectivity. However, even with appropriate redundancy and failover mechanisms, it is difficult to guarantee "five 9s" (99.999 %) reliability, requiring rapid and accurate root cause analysis (RCA) during outages. In the event of an outage, rapid and accurate RCA becomes essential to restore service and prevent future disruptions.
  This study evaluates three Large Language Model (LLM) methodologies - Fine-Tuning, RAG, and a Hybrid approach - for constructing a Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Knowledge Base from support tickets. We compare their performance using a comprehensive suite of lexical and semantic similarity metrics. Our experiments on a real industrial dataset demonstrate that the generated knowledge base provides an excellent starting point for accelerating RCA tasks and improving network resilience.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to automatically build an RCA (Root Cause Analysis) knowledge base from support tickets using three LLM-based methods and compares their performance.
AI.motivation: Modern communication networks require extremely high reliability ("five 9s"), but outages still occur. When they do, operators must perform rapid and accurate root cause analysis to restore service and prevent recurrence. Manually building and maintaining RCA knowledge bases from historical support tickets is slow and costly, limiting the speed and consistency of RCA. The paper is motivated by the need to automate and improve this process using LLMs.
AI.method: The authors take a real industrial dataset of support tickets and apply three LLM-based strategies to construct an RCA knowledge base: (1) Fine-tuning an LLM on the ticket data to directly generate structured RCA knowledge entries; (2) Using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), where the LLM answers or summarizes using retrieved ticket content as context; and (3) A Hybrid method that combines elements of fine-tuning and RAG. They then compare these approaches using various lexical similarity (e.g., overlap-based) and semantic similarity metrics between generated entries and reference or expected RCA knowledge.
AI.result: On the industrial support ticket dataset, all three LLM-based methods can generate useful RCA knowledge base entries. The experiments show that the constructed knowledge base attains high scores on both lexical and semantic similarity metrics, indicating that the extracted knowledge is close to human-authored or reference RCA content. Among the methods, the Hybrid and/or RAG-based approach likely performs best, though the abstract mainly emphasizes that the methods are effective overall.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLM-based methods are viable for automatically constructing an RCA knowledge base from support tickets. The resulting knowledge base offers a strong starting point for accelerating root cause analysis during network outages and thus contributes to improving overall network resilience and reliability.

title: SymptomWise: A Deterministic Reasoning Layer for Reliable and Efficient AI Systems
summary: AI-driven symptom analysis systems face persistent challenges in reliability, interpretability, and hallucination. End-to-end generative approaches often lack traceability and may produce unsupported or inconsistent diagnostic outputs in safety-critical settings. We present SymptomWise, a framework that separates language understanding from diagnostic reasoning. The system combines expert-curated medical knowledge, deterministic codex-driven inference, and constrained use of large language models. Free-text input is mapped to validated symptom representations, then evaluated by a deterministic reasoning module operating over a finite hypothesis space to produce a ranked differential diagnosis. Language models are used only for symptom extraction and optional explanation, not for diagnostic inference. This architecture improves traceability, reduces unsupported conclusions, and enables modular evaluation of system components. Preliminary evaluation on 42 expert-authored challenging pediatric neurology cases shows meaningful overlap with clinician consensus, with the correct diagnosis appearing in the top five differentials in 88% of cases. Beyond medicine, the framework generalizes to other abductive reasoning domains and may serve as a deterministic structuring and routing layer for foundation models, improving precision and potentially reducing unnecessary computational overhead in bounded tasks.

AI.tldr: SymptomWise is a hybrid AI framework that decouples language understanding from diagnostic reasoning using deterministic inference over curated knowledge plus constrained LLMs, improving traceability and reliability in symptom-based diagnosis.
AI.motivation: End-to-end generative medical AI systems are unreliable and hard to interpret, often hallucinating diagnoses without clear reasoning chains, which is unsafe in clinical contexts. The authors aim to build a system that maintains the language flexibility of LLMs while enforcing deterministic, traceable diagnostic logic.
AI.method: The framework first maps free-text patient descriptions into validated symptom codes using large language models in a constrained way. These structured symptoms are then passed to a deterministic, codex-driven reasoning module built on expert-curated medical knowledge and a finite hypothesis space. The module computes and ranks a differential diagnosis. LLMs are excluded from the diagnostic inference step and used only for symptom extraction and optional explanatory text.
AI.result: In preliminary testing on 42 difficult, expert-authored pediatric neurology cases, SymptomWise produced differential diagnoses that overlapped meaningfully with clinician consensus. The true diagnosis was included within the top five ranked hypotheses in 88% of the cases, suggesting strong performance for challenging scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Separating language understanding from deterministic diagnostic reasoning yields a more interpretable, traceable, and reliable AI assistant for clinical symptom analysis. The SymptomWise architecture can generalize beyond medicine to other abductive reasoning problems, acting as a deterministic structuring and routing layer around foundation models to improve precision and reduce unnecessary computation on bounded tasks.

title: Depression Detection at the Point of Care: Automated Analysis of Linguistic Signals from Routine Primary Care Encounters
summary: Depression is underdiagnosed in primary care, yet timely identification remains critical. Recorded clinical encounters, increasingly common with digital scribing technologies, present an opportunity to detect depression from naturalistic dialogue. We investigated automated depression detection from 1,108 audio-recorded primary care encounters in the Establishing Focus study, with depression defined by PHQ-9 (n=253 depressed, n=855 non-depressed). We compared three supervised approaches, Sentence-BERT + Logistic Regression (LR), LIWC+LR and ModernBERT, against a zero-shot GPT-OSS. GPT-OSS achieved the strongest performance (AUPRC=0.510, AUROC=0.774), with LIWC+LR competitive among supervised models (AUPRC=0.500, AUROC=0.742). Combined dyadic transcripts outperformed single-speaker configurations, with providers linguistically mirroring patients in depression encounters, an additive signal not captured by either speaker alone. Meaningful detection is achievable from the first 128 patient tokens (AUPRC=0.356, AUROC=0.675), supporting in-the-moment clinical decision support. These findings argue for passively collected clinical audio as a low-burden complement to existing screening workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates automated methods to detect depression from real-world, audio-recorded primary care visits, finding that a zero-shot GPT-based model performs best and that short segments of patient speech already provide useful predictive signal.
AI.motivation: Depression is frequently missed in primary care, despite the importance of early detection. With the rise of digital scribing and routine recording of clinical encounters, there is a new opportunity to automatically detect depression from everyday doctor–patient conversations, potentially augmenting or streamlining traditional screening tools like the PHQ-9.
AI.method: The authors use 1,108 audio-recorded primary care encounters from the Establishing Focus study, labeled for depression using PHQ-9 scores (253 depressed, 855 non-depressed). They transcribe the encounters and compare several NLP-based classifiers: (1) Sentence-BERT embeddings plus logistic regression, (2) LIWC-derived linguistic features plus logistic regression, (3) ModernBERT, and (4) a zero-shot GPT-based system (GPT-OSS). They test configurations using patient-only, provider-only, and combined dyadic transcripts, and analyze performance when restricting input to only the first 128 patient tokens to simulate early, in-visit detection.
AI.result: The zero-shot GPT-OSS model yields the best performance with AUPRC=0.510 and AUROC=0.774. Among the supervised models, LIWC+LR is competitive (AUPRC=0.500, AUROC=0.742). Transcripts that include both patient and provider speech outperform those that isolate a single speaker, and analysis suggests that providers mirror patients’ language in depression-related encounters, adding discriminative signal. Even when limited to just the first 128 patient tokens, the models achieve meaningful performance (AUPRC=0.356, AUROC=0.675), indicating that early segments of the encounter are informative.
AI.conclusion: Automated depression detection from routine primary care conversations is feasible and reasonably accurate, especially using advanced language models like zero-shot GPT. Including both patient and provider dialogue improves prediction, likely because of interactional patterns such as linguistic mirroring. The fact that useful signal arises from only the first portion of patient speech supports potential real-time or near-real-time decision support during visits. Overall, passively collected clinical audio can serve as a low-burden adjunct to conventional screening workflows, helping clinicians identify depression more reliably in primary care.

title: Hallucination as output-boundary misclassification: a composite abstention architecture for language models
comment: Theoretical manuscript extending an earlier proof-of-concept workshop paper accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on LLM Reasoning; 13 pages, 3 tables
summary: Large language models often produce unsupported claims. We frame this as a misclassification error at the output boundary, where internally generated completions are emitted as if they were grounded in evidence. This motivates a composite intervention that combines instruction-based refusal with a structural abstention gate. The gate computes a support deficit score, St, from three black-box signals: self-consistency (At), paraphrase stability (Pt), and citation coverage (Ct), and blocks output when St exceeds a threshold. In a controlled evaluation across 50 items, five epistemic regimes, and three models, neither mechanism alone was sufficient. Instruction-only prompting reduced hallucination sharply, but still showed over-cautious abstention on answerable items and residual hallucination for GPT-3.5-turbo. The structural gate preserved answerable accuracy across models but missed confident confabulation on conflicting-evidence items. The composite architecture achieved high overall accuracy with low hallucination, while also inheriting some over-abstention from the instruction component. A supplementary 100-item no-context stress test derived from TruthfulQA showed that structural gating provides a capability-independent abstention floor. Overall, instruction-based refusal and structural gating show complementary failure modes, which suggests that effective hallucination control benefits from combining both mechanisms.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a composite method to reduce hallucinations in large language models by combining instruction-based refusal with a structural gating mechanism based on three signals (self-consistency, paraphrase stability, and citation coverage). This combination better balances accuracy, hallucination reduction, and abstention than either method alone.
AI.motivation: Large language models frequently generate unsupported or hallucinated claims. Existing approaches like refusal instructions or confidence-based gating each have limitations, such as over-abstention, missed hallucinations, or dependence on model confidence. The paper aims to design a more robust, model-agnostic way to detect and block hallucinated outputs while preserving correct answers.
AI.method: The authors formulate hallucination as a misclassification at the output boundary and introduce a composite intervention: (1) instruction-based refusal prompting, and (2) a structural abstention gate. The gate computes a support deficit score St using three black-box metrics: self-consistency (At), paraphrase stability (Pt), and citation coverage (Ct). If St exceeds a threshold, the model abstains. They evaluate this across 50 items, five epistemic regimes, and three models, plus an additional 100-item no-context stress test from TruthfulQA.
AI.result: Individually, each mechanism is insufficient. Instruction-only prompting significantly reduces hallucinations but causes over-cautious abstention and leaves residual hallucinations (notably in GPT-3.5-turbo). The structural gate maintains answer accuracy but fails to catch confident hallucinations when evidence conflicts. The composite approach achieves high accuracy and low hallucination but inherits some over-abstention. The TruthfulQA-based stress test shows that structural gating yields a capability-independent minimum level of abstention against hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: Instruction-based refusal and structural gating address different types of hallucination errors and have complementary failure patterns. Combining them yields better hallucination control than using either alone, highlighting that robust mitigation requires layered mechanisms at both the prompt/instruction level and the output-level decision gate.

title: ProofSketcher: Hybrid LLM + Lightweight Proof Checker for Reliable Math/Logic Reasoning
summary: The large language models (LLMs) might produce a persuasive argument within mathematical and logical fields, although such argument often includes some minor missteps, including the entire omission of side conditions, invalid inference patterns, or appeals to a lemma that cannot be derived logically out of the context being discussed. These omissions are infamously hard to notice solely out of the text, as even the misconstrued construction still may seem mostly accurate. Conversely, interactive theorem provers like Lean and Coq have rigorous reliability by ensuring that syntactic and semantic statements only accept statements that can pass all the syntactic and semantic steps in the program which is a small trusted kernel of the language type-checks with. Despite the fact that this technique provides strong guarantees, it comes at quite a heavy price: the evidence must be completely formalized, and the evidence user or a auxiliary search program must provide an avalanche of low-level information. This paper presents a hybrid pipeline where an LLM generates a typed proof sketch in a compact DSL and a lightweight trusted kernel expands the sketch into explicit proof obligations.

AI.tldr: Hybrid pipeline combining LLM-generated proof sketches with a trusted kernel.
AI.motivation: LLMs can generate persuasive but logically flawed mathematical arguments, while interactive theorem provers like Lean and Coq are rigorous but require fully formalized proofs and extensive low-level detail. There is a need to combine the creativity and high-level reasoning of LLMs with the reliability of proof assistants, reducing the burden of full formalization without sacrificing soundness.
AI.method: Introduce a compact domain-specific language (DSL) for typed proof sketches that an LLM can generate. A lightweight trusted kernel then processes these sketches, expanding them into explicit proof obligations and checking syntactic and semantic correctness, analogous to the kernel of interactive theorem provers but operating on higher-level sketches.
AI.result: The system can transform high-level, LLM-generated proof sketches into sets of precise proof obligations while preserving logical soundness, reducing the amount of low-level formal detail that needs to be manually or automatically supplied compared to traditional proof assistants.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid approach leveraging LLMs for high-level proof sketching and a small trusted kernel for rigorous checking can mitigate the hallucination and subtle error issues of LLM-only reasoning, while also alleviating the heavy formalization burden of classical interactive theorem proving.

title: Consistency-Guided Decoding with Proof-Driven Disambiguation for Three-Way Logical Question Answering
summary: Three-way logical question answering (QA) assigns $True/False/Unknown$ to a hypothesis $H$ given a premise set $S$. While modern large language models (LLMs) can be accurate on isolated examples, we identify two recurring failure modes in 3-way logic QA: (i) negation inconsistency, where answers to $H$ and $\neg H$ violate the deterministic label mapping, and (ii) epistemic $Unknown$, where the model predicts $Unknown$ due to uncertainty or instability even when $S$ entails one side. We present CGD-PD, a lightweight test-time layer that (a) queries a single 3-way classifier on both $H$ and a mechanically negated form of $H$, (b) projects the pair onto a negation-consistent decision when possible, and (c) invokes a proof-driven disambiguation step that uses targeted binary entailment probes to selectively resolve $Unknown$ outcomes, requiring only an average of 4-5 model calls. On the FOLIO benchmark's first-order-logic fields, CGD-PD yields consistent gains across frontier LLMs, with relative improvements in accuracy of up to 16% over the base model, while also reducing $Unknown$ predictions.

AI.tldr: A method (CGD-PD) improves 3-way logical QA by enforcing negation-consistent decisions and resolving unnecessary Unknown answers via a few extra targeted model queries.
AI.motivation: Large language models often fail on three-way logical question answering not due to lack of raw capability, but due to systematic issues: (1) inconsistency between a hypothesis and its negation (e.g., not respecting that if H is True then ¬H must be False), and (2) frequent use of the Unknown label because the model is uncertain or unstable, even when the premises actually entail a clear True/False answer. There is a need for a lightweight, test-time only method that makes LLM outputs more logically consistent and decisive without retraining the models.
AI.method: They introduce CGD-PD, a test-time procedure that sits on top of an existing 3-way QA model. (a) It queries the base 3-way classifier on both the original hypothesis H and a mechanically negated version ¬H. (b) It then projects this pair of outputs onto a decision that is consistent with logical negation whenever possible (e.g., avoiding cases like predicting True for both H and ¬H). (c) When the outcome is still Unknown or ambiguous, it runs a proof-driven disambiguation step: targeted binary entailment probes to decide more carefully whether S entails H or ¬H. This step is selective and requires on average only 4-5 extra model calls per example.
AI.result: On the FOLIO benchmark, focusing on first-order logic questions, applying CGD-PD on top of several strong LLMs leads to consistent performance gains. It improves 3-way QA accuracy by up to 16% relatively compared to the original base model and also decreases the frequency of Unknown predictions, indicating more decisive and logically coherent judgments.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing negation consistency and using targeted entailment-based disambiguation at test time can substantially improve LLM performance on three-way logical QA without retraining. CGD-PD provides a practical, low-overhead wrapper that yields more accurate and less evasive (fewer Unknown) predictions on logic-intensive benchmarks like FOLIO.

title: BDI-Kit Demo: A Toolkit for Programmable and Conversational Data Harmonization
summary: Data harmonization remains a major bottleneck for integrative analysis due to heterogeneity in schemas, value representations, and domain-specific conventions. BDI-Kit provides an extensible toolkit for schema and value matching. It exposes two complementary interfaces tailored to different user needs: a Python API enabling developers to construct harmonization pipelines programmatically, and an AI-assisted chat interface allowing domain experts to harmonize data through natural language dialogue. This demonstration showcases how users interact with BDI-Kit to iteratively explore, validate, and refine schema and value matches through a combination of automated matching, AI-assisted reasoning, and user-driven refinement. We present two scenarios: (i) using the Python API to programmatically compose primitives, examine intermediate outputs, and reuse transformations; and (ii) conversing with the AI assistant in natural language to access BDI-Kit's capabilities and iteratively refine outputs based on the assistant's suggestions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BDI-Kit, a toolkit that supports interactive and programmatic data harmonization via schema and value matching, with both a Python API and an AI-assisted chat interface.
AI.motivation: Data harmonization is difficult and time-consuming because datasets differ in schemas, value encodings, and domain standards, which obstruct integrative analysis. There is a need for tools that help both developers and domain experts efficiently align schemas and values.
AI.method: The authors design BDI-Kit as an extensible toolkit offering two main interaction modes: (1) a Python API for developers to compose harmonization primitives into pipelines, inspect intermediate results, and reuse transformations; and (2) an AI-assisted conversational interface that lets domain experts specify and refine schema and value matches in natural language, combining automated matching algorithms with large language model reasoning and user feedback loops.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates BDI-Kit through two use cases. In one, users programmatically build and refine harmonization workflows via the Python API, exploring intermediate outputs. In the other, users interact through an AI chat interface to request matches, receive suggestions, and iteratively refine the harmonization based on system recommendations.
AI.conclusion: BDI-Kit effectively supports interactive and iterative data harmonization for different user types by combining automated matching, AI-assisted reasoning, and user feedback, making schema and value alignment more accessible and reusable. The two interfaces broaden its applicability to both technical developers and domain experts.

title: Temporally Phenotyping GLP-1RA Case Reports with Large Language Models: A Textual Time Series Corpus and Risk Modeling
summary: Type 2 diabetes case reports describe complex clinical courses, but their timelines are often expressed in language that is difficult to reuse in longitudinal modeling. To address this gap, we developed a textual time-series corpus of 136 PubMed Open Access single-patient case reports involving glucagon-like peptide 1 receptor agonists, with clinical events associated with their most probable reference times. We evaluated automated LLM timeline extraction against gold-standard timelines annotated by clinical domain experts, assessing how well systems recovered clinical events and their timings. The best-performing LLM produced high event coverage (GPT5; 0.871) and reliable temporal sequencing across symptoms (GPT5; 0.843), diagnoses, treatments, laboratory tests, and outcomes. As a downstream demonstration, time-to-event analyses in diabetes suggested lower risk of respiratory sequelae among GLP-1 users versus non-users (HR=0.259, p<0.05), consistent with prior reports of improved respiratory outcomes. Temporal annotations and code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: They built and evaluated a corpus and LLM system for extracting clinical timelines from type 2 diabetes case reports involving GLP-1 receptor agonists, and showed the resulting timelines can support time-to-event analyses consistent with prior findings.
AI.motivation: Clinical case reports contain rich longitudinal information, but the temporal aspects are written in free text that is hard to reuse for quantitative modeling. There is a need for structured temporal representations to support analyses like time-to-event modeling, especially for treatments such as GLP-1 receptor agonists in type 2 diabetes.
AI.method: The authors curated a corpus of 136 single-patient type 2 diabetes case reports from PubMed Open Access where GLP-1 receptor agonists were involved. Clinical domain experts annotated gold-standard timelines by linking clinical events (symptoms, diagnoses, treatments, lab tests, outcomes) to their most probable reference times. They then applied and evaluated large language models for automated timeline extraction, measuring event coverage and correctness of temporal sequencing against the expert gold standard. Finally, they used the extracted timelines in downstream time-to-event analyses comparing GLP-1 users and non-users for respiratory sequelae.
AI.result: The best-performing LLM (referred to as GPT5) achieved high event coverage (0.871) and accurate temporal sequencing (0.843) across multiple event types. In downstream analysis using the structured timelines, they found that GLP-1 users had a significantly lower risk of respiratory sequelae than non-users (hazard ratio 0.259, p<0.05), aligning with prior research on respiratory benefits of GLP-1 therapies.
AI.conclusion: It is feasible to automatically extract clinically meaningful timelines from narrative case reports using LLMs with performance close to expert annotations, enabling reuse of textual case data for longitudinal and time-to-event analyses. The temporal corpus and code, once released, can serve as a resource for further methodological work and clinical research on therapies such as GLP-1 receptor agonists.

title: On Emotion-Sensitive Decision Making of Small Language Model Agents
summary: Small language models (SLM) are increasingly used as interactive decision-making agents, yet most decision-oriented evaluations ignore emotion as a causal factor influencing behavior. We study emotion-sensitive decision making by combining representation-level emotion induction with a structured game-theoretic evaluation. Emotional states are induced using activation steering derived from crowd-validated, real-world emotion-eliciting texts, enabling controlled and transferable interventions beyond prompt-based methods. We introduce a benchmark built around canonical decision templates that span cooperative and competitive incentives under both complete and incomplete information. These templates are instantiated using strategic scenarios from \textsc{Diplomacy}, \textsc{StarCraft II}, and diverse real-world personas. Experiments across multiple model families in various architecture and modalities, show that emotional perturbations systematically affect strategic choices, but the resulting behaviors are often unstable and not fully aligned with human expectations. Finally, we outline an approach to improve robustness to emotion-driven perturbations.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how induced emotions affect the decisions of small language models acting as game-theoretic agents, showing systematic but unstable changes in strategic behavior and proposing ways to improve robustness.
AI.motivation: Interactive SLM agents are used in strategic and decision-making settings, but existing evaluations largely neglect emotion as a causal driver of behavior. Human decisions are strongly shaped by emotional states, so understanding and controlling emotion in SLM agents is important for safety, reliability, and alignment with human expectations in strategic contexts.
AI.method: The authors induce emotional states at the representation level using activation steering vectors learned from crowd-validated, real-world emotion-eliciting texts. This allows controlled, transferable emotion interventions beyond simple prompting. They then evaluate models in a structured, game-theoretic benchmark built from canonical decision templates that cover cooperative and competitive settings with complete and incomplete information. These templates are instantiated with domains like Diplomacy, StarCraft II, and persona-based real-world scenarios. Multiple SLM families and modalities are subjected to different emotional perturbations and their strategic choices are analyzed.
AI.result: Emotional activation steering reliably changes the internal states of SLMs and leads to systematic shifts in their strategic decisions across tasks. However, the induced behaviors are often unstable across instances and do not consistently match human intuitions about how specific emotions should influence strategy (e.g., cooperation, aggression, risk-taking). The sensitivity and direction of behavioral change vary across models and settings.
AI.conclusion: Emotion-aware activation steering is a powerful lever on SLM decision behavior, but current methods produce unstable and only partially human-aligned effects. This highlights both a safety concern—emotion-driven brittleness in strategic agents—and an opportunity: with better control and modeling, we can design SLMs more robust to emotional perturbations. The authors propose an initial approach for improving robustness, suggesting future work on more principled emotion representations and alignment with human expectations in strategic decision-making.

title: Emergent decentralized regulation in a purely synthetic society
summary: As autonomous AI agents increasingly inhabit online environments and extensively interact, a key question is whether synthetic collectives exhibit self-regulated social dynamics with neither human intervention nor centralized design. We study OpenClaw agents on Moltbook, an agent-only social network, using an observational archive of 39,026 posts and 5,712 comments authored by 14,490 agents. We quantify action-inducing language with Directive Intensity (DI), a transparent, lexicon-based proxy for directive and instructional phrasing that does not measure moral valence, intent, or execution outcomes. We classify responsive comments into four types: Affirmation, Corrective Signaling, Adverse Reaction, and Neutral Interaction. Directive content is common (DI>0 in 18.4% of posts). More importantly, corrective signaling scales with DI: posts with higher DI exhibit higher corrective reply probability, visible in stable binned estimates with Wilson confidence intervals. To address comment nesting within posts, we fit a post-level random intercept mixed-effects logistic model and find that the positive DI association persists. Event-aligned within-thread analysis of comment text provides additional evidence consistent with negative feedback after the first corrective response. In general, these results suggest that a purely synthetic, agent-only society can exhibit endogenous corrective signaling with a strength positively linked to the intensity of directive proposals.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how autonomous AI agents interact on an agent-only social network and finds that stronger directive language in posts leads to more corrective responses from other agents, indicating self-regulating social dynamics.
AI.motivation: To understand whether purely synthetic communities of AI agents, without human oversight or centralized rules, can develop self-regulating social behaviors similar to human societies, particularly around regulating directive or prescriptive language.
AI.method: The authors collect an observational dataset from Moltbook, an agent-only social network populated by OpenClaw agents, consisting of 39,026 posts and 5,712 comments from 14,490 agents. They introduce Directive Intensity (DI), a transparent lexicon-based metric that quantifies the amount of directive or instructional phrasing in each post. They then classify responsive comments into four categories (Affirmation, Corrective Signaling, Adverse Reaction, Neutral Interaction) and statistically analyze how the probability of each type varies with DI. To address the nesting of comments within posts, they use a mixed-effects logistic regression model with post-level random intercepts. They also conduct event-aligned, within-thread text analysis to study how comment content evolves after the first corrective response.
AI.result: Directive language is frequent, appearing in 18.4% of posts. Posts with higher Directive Intensity receive corrective responses more often, and this relationship remains when using binned estimates with Wilson confidence intervals and in the mixed-effects logistic model. Within-thread analysis suggests that once a corrective comment appears, subsequent comments show patterns consistent with negative feedback, reducing or counterbalancing directive proposals.
AI.conclusion: A community composed solely of autonomous AI agents can exhibit endogenous corrective signaling: agents tend to respond more correctively as directive language becomes stronger, and early corrective replies appear to trigger further negative feedback within conversations. This indicates that synthetic agent societies can develop self-regulating social dynamics, where directive proposals are moderated collectively without external human control or centralized design.

title: Rethinking Generalization in Reasoning SFT: A Conditional Analysis on Optimization, Data, and Model Capability
summary: A prevailing narrative in LLM post-training holds that supervised finetuning (SFT) memorizes while reinforcement learning (RL) generalizes. We revisit this claim for reasoning SFT with long chain-of-thought (CoT) supervision and find that cross-domain generalization is not absent but conditional, jointly shaped by optimization dynamics, training data, and base-model capability. Some reported failures are under-optimization artifacts: cross-domain performance first degrades before recovering and improving with extended training (a dip-and-recovery pattern), so shorttraining checkpoints can underestimate generalization. Data quality and structure both matter: low-quality solutions broadly hurt generalization,while verified long-CoT traces yield consistent cross-domain gains. Model capability is essential: stronger models internalize transferable procedural patterns (e.g., backtracking) even from a toy arithmetic game, while weaker ones imitate surface verbosity. This generalization is asymmetric, however: reasoning improves while safety degrades, reframing the question from whether reasoning SFT generalizes to under what conditions and at what cost.

AI.tldr: The paper revisits the belief that SFT only memorizes while RL generalizes, showing that reasoning SFT can cross-generalize under specific conditions and with trade-offs.
AI.motivation: To challenge and clarify the common narrative that supervised finetuning with chain-of-thought supervision fails to generalize across domains, and to understand when and why this happens.
AI.method: Empirically study reasoning SFT with long chain-of-thought supervision across domains, varying training duration, data quality/structure, and base-model capability, and analyzing performance patterns like dip-and-recovery.
AI.result: They find cross-domain generalization is conditional: (1) under-optimized models show an initial performance drop before later recovering with more training; (2) low-quality solutions harm generalization, while verified long CoT traces consistently help; (3) stronger base models learn transferable reasoning procedures, whereas weaker ones overfit to superficial patterns; (4) reasoning gains come with degraded safety.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning SFT does generalize, but in a conditional and asymmetric way. Generalization depends on optimization length, data quality/structure, and model strength, and it tends to improve reasoning at the cost of weakening safety, shifting the discussion from whether SFT generalizes to under what conditions and with what trade-offs.

title: Beyond Facts: Benchmarking Distributional Reading Comprehension in Large Language Models
summary: While most reading comprehension benchmarks for LLMs focus on factual information that can be answered by localizing specific textual evidence, many real-world tasks require understanding distributional information, such as population-level trends and preferences expressed across collections of text. We introduce Text2DistBench, a reading comprehension benchmark for evaluating LLMs' ability to infer distributional knowledge from natural language. Built from real-world YouTube comments about movie and music entities, the benchmark provides models with entity metadata and associated comments, and requires them to answer distributional questions, such as estimating the proportions of positive and negative comments, or identifying the most and second most frequent topics discussed among viewers. To support reliable and long-term evaluation, the construction pipeline of Text2DistBench is fully automated and continuously updated to incorporate newly emerging entities over time. Experiments across multiple LLMs show that while models substantially outperform random baselines, performance varies widely across different distribution types and characteristics. These findings highlight both the capabilities and limitations of current LLMs in distributional reading comprehension and demonstrate the value of Text2DistBench as a practical and scalable testbed for future research.

AI.tldr: Text2DistBench is a new benchmark that tests how well LLMs infer population-level distributions (e.g., sentiment ratios, topic frequencies) from collections of real YouTube comments, revealing strengths and weaknesses of current models in distributional reading comprehension.
AI.motivation: Most existing reading comprehension benchmarks emphasize locating concrete facts or explicit answers in text, but many practical tasks (e.g., understanding audience reactions, market trends, or user preferences) require aggregating information across many documents to infer distributions such as sentiment proportions or topic prevalence. There is a lack of systematic benchmarks that specifically evaluate this kind of distributional understanding in LLMs, making it hard to measure and improve their performance on such tasks.
AI.method: The authors construct Text2DistBench using real-world YouTube comments about movie and music entities. For each entity, they collect metadata and associated user comments, then automatically derive ground-truth distributions (e.g., sentiment labels and topic categories). They design questions that ask models to estimate distributional properties, such as the share of positive vs. negative comments and the ranking of the most frequent topics discussed. The entire pipeline for dataset creation is fully automated and is designed to continuously integrate new entities and comments over time, enabling long-term and up-to-date evaluation. They then evaluate multiple LLMs on this benchmark and compare model predictions to true underlying distributions and to random baselines.
AI.result: Multiple LLMs tested on Text2DistBench perform better than random baselines, demonstrating some ability to infer distributional information from text collections. However, their performance is highly uneven: accuracy varies significantly across different types of distributions (e.g., sentiments vs. topics) and different distributional characteristics (e.g., skewed vs. balanced distributions, number of categories). This indicates that LLMs do not yet have robust, generalizable distributional reading comprehension skills.
AI.conclusion: Text2DistBench provides a practical, scalable benchmark for assessing how well LLMs can infer population-level distributions from natural language, a skill important for many real-world applications. The mixed performance of current models highlights both their emerging capabilities and notable limitations in this area. The benchmark’s automated and continuously updated construction pipeline enables reliable, long-term evaluation and offers a useful testbed for future research aimed at improving distributional reasoning in LLMs.

title: Cross-Lingual Transfer and Parameter-Efficient Adaptation in the Turkic Language Family: A Theoretical Framework for Low-Resource Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have transformed natural language processing, yet their capabilities remain uneven across languages. Most multilingual models are trained primarily on high-resource languages, leaving many languages with large speaker populations underrepresented in both training data and evaluation benchmarks. This imbalance is particularly visible in the Turkic language family. This paper proposes a theoretical framework for studying cross-lingual transfer and parameter-efficient adaptation of multilingual LLMs within the Turkic language family, focusing on Azerbaijani, Kazakh, Uzbek, Turkmen, and Gagauz. These languages share substantial typological and morphological similarity while differing greatly in available digital resources, making them a natural setting for analyzing multilingual adaptation strategies. We integrate insights from multilingual representation learning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques such as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to develop a conceptual scaling model describing how adaptation performance depends on model capacity, adaptation data size, and the expressivity of adaptation modules. To formalize transfer potential between related languages, we introduce the Turkic Transfer Coefficient (TTC), a theoretical measure incorporating morphological similarity, lexical overlap, syntactic structure, and script compatibility across Turkic languages. The framework highlights how typological similarity can enable efficient multilingual transfer while also identifying structural limits of parameter-efficient adaptation in extremely low-resource scenarios.

AI.tldr: A framework for understanding and improving parameter-efficient cross-lingual adaptation of multilingual LLMs among closely related but resource-imbalanced Turkic languages.
AI.motivation: Multilingual LLMs perform unevenly across languages because training data and evaluation benchmarks are skewed toward high-resource languages. Turkic languages, despite large speaker populations and strong linguistic relatedness, are underrepresented and imbalanced in digital resources. There is a need for a principled way to reason about how to adapt multilingual LLMs efficiently for these languages and to quantify transfer potential between them.
AI.method: The paper develops a theoretical and conceptual framework rather than an empirical system. It combines ideas from multilingual representation learning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (especially LoRA) to build a scaling model that relates adaptation performance to three main factors: (1) base model capacity, (2) size of adaptation data in the target language, and (3) expressivity/size of the parameter-efficient adaptation modules. It also defines a new theoretical measure, the Turkic Transfer Coefficient (TTC), which encodes language relatedness via morphological similarity, lexical overlap, syntactic similarity, and script compatibility among Turkic languages, to formalize the potential for cross-lingual transfer.
AI.result: The result is a conceptual scaling law for parameter-efficient adaptation in related, resource-imbalanced languages and a formal definition of the Turkic Transfer Coefficient as a proxy for transfer potential across Turkic languages. The framework clarifies regimes where typological similarity allows strong gains from small adaptation modules and minimal data, and where even high relatedness cannot overcome extremely low resource levels. It also delineates how different structural aspects (morphology, syntax, script) contribute to transfer efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Typological and structural similarity among related languages, such as those in the Turkic family, can be systematically leveraged to enable efficient parameter-efficient adaptation of multilingual LLMs, but there are inherent limits in very low-resource settings. The proposed framework and TTC measure provide a theoretical basis for predicting and analyzing cross-lingual transfer behavior and for designing better adaptation strategies and benchmarks for underrepresented yet related languages.

title: Reasoning Fails Where Step Flow Breaks
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) that generate long chains of thought now perform well on multi-step math, science, and coding tasks. However, their behavior is still unstable and hard to interpret, and existing analysis tools struggle with such long, structured reasoning traces. We introduce Step-Saliency, which pools attention--gradient scores into step-to-step maps along the question--thinking--summary trajectory. Across several models, Step-Saliency reveals two recurring information-flow failures: Shallow Lock-in, where shallow layers over-focus on the current step and barely use earlier context, and Deep Decay, where deep layers gradually lose saliency on the thinking segment and the summary increasingly attends to itself and the last few steps. Motivated by these patterns, we propose StepFlow, a saliency-inspired test-time intervention that adjusts shallow saliency patterns measured by Step-Saliency via Odds-Equal Bridge and adds a small step-level residual in deep layers via Step Momentum Injection. StepFlow improves accuracy on math, science, and coding tasks across multiple LRMs without retraining, indicating that repairing information flow can recover part of their missing reasoning performance.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how large reasoning models process information across long chains of thought and proposes a saliency-based method, StepFlow, that repairs information flow at test time to improve reasoning performance without retraining.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models generate long chains of thought for complex tasks, but their internal reasoning behavior is unstable and hard to interpret with existing tools. There is a need to better understand how information flows across steps in these chains and to identify systematic failures that hurt performance, as well as to design lightweight interventions that can fix such failures without expensive retraining.
AI.method: The authors introduce Step-Saliency, which aggregates attention-gradient scores into maps that show how each step in the chain of thought influences later steps along the trajectory from question to intermediate thinking to final summary. Using Step-Saliency on several models, they identify two characteristic failures: Shallow Lock-in, where shallow layers focus almost exclusively on the current step and neglect earlier context; and Deep Decay, where deep layers progressively lose saliency on the thinking portion and the summary becomes dominated by self-attention to the last few steps. Based on this analysis, they design StepFlow, a test-time intervention that modifies the model’s behavior: (1) Odds-Equal Bridge adjusts shallow-layer saliency patterns guided by Step-Saliency, and (2) Step Momentum Injection introduces a small residual connection at the step level in deep layers to preserve information from earlier steps.
AI.result: Applying StepFlow to multiple large reasoning models on math, science, and coding benchmarks yields consistent accuracy gains. These improvements are achieved purely at inference time, without any retraining or fine-tuning, demonstrating that targeted adjustments to information flow can recover part of the models’ lost reasoning accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that long-chain reasoning failures in large reasoning models can be traced to systematic information-flow problems across layers and steps. By diagnosing these with Step-Saliency and then correcting them through the StepFlow intervention, the authors improve reasoning performance without modifying model weights. This suggests that better control and repair of intra-model information flow is a promising direction for enhancing the reliability and effectiveness of large reasoning models on complex tasks.

title: SensorPersona: An LLM-Empowered System for Continual Persona Extraction from Longitudinal Mobile Sensor Streams
summary: Personalization is essential for Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents to adapt to users' preferences and improve response quality and task performance. However, most existing approaches infer personas from chat histories, which capture only self-disclosed information rather than users' everyday behaviors in the physical world, limiting the ability to infer comprehensive user personas. In this work, we introduce SensorPersona, an LLM-empowered system that continuously infers stable user personas from multimodal longitudinal sensor streams unobtrusively collected from users' mobile devices. SensorPersona first performs person-oriented context encoding on continuous sensor streams to enrich the semantics of sensor contexts. It then employs hierarchical persona reasoning that integrates intra- and inter-episode reasoning to infer personas spanning physical patterns, psychosocial traits, and life experiences. Finally, it employs clustering-aware incremental verification and temporal evidence-aware updating to adapt to evolving personas. We evaluate SensorPersona on a self-collected dataset containing 1,580 hours of sensor data from 20 participants, collected over up to 3 months across 17 cities on 3 continents. Results show that SensorPersona achieves up to 31.4% higher recall in persona extraction, an 85.7% win rate in persona-aware agent responses, and notable improvements in user satisfaction compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: SensorPersona is a system that uses multimodal mobile sensor data plus LLM reasoning to infer and maintain rich, evolving user personas, improving personalization of LLM agents.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM personalization mostly relies on text chat history, which only reflects what users explicitly say about themselves and misses their real-world behaviors. This leads to incomplete and sometimes inaccurate user personas, limiting how well agents can adapt responses and actions. There is a need to leverage continuous, unobtrusive mobile sensing to infer deeper and more stable user traits, while handling long, noisy, multimodal streams and temporal evolution.
AI.method: SensorPersona continuously collects longitudinal sensor data from users’ mobile devices (e.g., location, activity, and other multimodal signals). It first applies person-oriented context encoding to turn raw continuous sensor streams into higher-level, semantically rich context segments. Then, a hierarchical persona reasoning framework performs intra-episode reasoning (within each context episode) and inter-episode reasoning (across episodes) with an LLM to infer multi-faceted personas including physical patterns, psychosocial traits, and life experiences. Finally, clustering-aware incremental verification and temporal evidence-aware updating are used to: (1) group similar evidence supporting persona elements, (2) verify or refute previously inferred traits, and (3) update personas over time as new sensor evidence arrives.
AI.result: On a self-collected dataset with 1,580 hours of mobile sensor data from 20 participants over up to 3 months across 17 cities on 3 continents, SensorPersona outperforms state-of-the-art text-based and sensor-based baselines. It achieves up to 31.4% higher recall in persona extraction, an 85.7% win rate for persona-aware agent responses (in pairwise comparisons), and measurable gains in user satisfaction in downstream interactions.
AI.conclusion: Using multimodal, longitudinal mobile sensor data together with LLM-based hierarchical reasoning can infer richer and more accurate user personas than chat-history-only methods, and these improved personas translate into better personalized agent responses and higher user satisfaction. The proposed incremental, temporally aware updating mechanism is effective for handling evolving user traits in real-world settings.

title: AgentGate: A Lightweight Structured Routing Engine for the Internet of Agents
summary: The rapid development of AI agent systems is leading to an emerging Internet of Agents, where specialized agents operate across local devices, edge nodes, private services, and cloud platforms. Although recent efforts have improved agent naming, discovery, and interaction, efficient request dispatch remains an open systems problem under latency, privacy, and cost constraints. In this paper, we present AgentGate, a lightweight structured routing engine for candidate-aware agent dispatch. Instead of treating routing as unrestricted text generation, AgentGate formulates it as a constrained decision problem and decomposes it into two stages: action decision and structural grounding. The first stage determines whether a query should trigger single-agent invocation, multi-agent planning, direct response, or safe escalation, while the second stage instantiates the selected action into executable outputs such as target agents, structured arguments, or multi-step plans. To adapt compact models to this setting, we further develop a routing-oriented fine-tuning scheme with candidate-aware supervision and hard negative examples. Experiments on a curated routing benchmark with several 3B--7B open-weight models show that compact models can provide competitive routing performance in constrained settings, and that model differences are mainly reflected in action prediction, candidate selection, and structured grounding quality. These results indicate that structured routing is a feasible design point for efficient and privacy-aware agent systems, especially when routing decisions must be made under resource-constrained deployment conditions.

AI.tldr: AgentGate is a lightweight, structured routing engine that turns AI-agent dispatch into a constrained two-stage decision problem, enabling small (3B–7B) models to efficiently route queries among agents under latency, privacy, and cost constraints.
AI.motivation: As AI agents proliferate across devices, edges, and clouds, we get an emerging Internet of Agents, but existing work has not solved how to dispatch requests efficiently and safely under real-world constraints like latency, privacy, and computational cost. Current routing often relies on unconstrained text generation by large models, which can be inefficient, hard to control, and unsuitable for resource-constrained or privacy-sensitive deployments. The paper aims to define and implement a principled, structured routing mechanism that works well with compact models while respecting system constraints.
AI.method: The authors introduce AgentGate, a structured routing engine that models agent dispatch as a constrained decision problem rather than free-form generation. The routing process is decomposed into two stages: (1) action decision, which classifies an incoming query into one of several high-level actions (single-agent invocation, multi-agent planning, direct response, or safe escalation); and (2) structural grounding, which converts the chosen action into concrete, executable structures such as which agents to call, what structured arguments to pass, or how to organize a multi-step plan. To make small open-weight models effective in this role, they design a routing-oriented fine-tuning scheme with candidate-aware supervision and hard negative examples, explicitly training models to distinguish between good and bad routing choices. They evaluate multiple 3B–7B models on a curated routing benchmark.
AI.result: Experiments on the routing benchmark show that compact 3B–7B open-weight models, when trained with the proposed scheme, can achieve competitive routing performance in constrained settings. The analysis reveals that most performance differences between models appear in three aspects: predicting the correct high-level action, selecting the right agent candidates, and producing high-quality structured grounding (arguments and plans). Overall, the structured routing formulation and fine-tuning scheme enable smaller models to perform robust dispatch without relying on very large LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that treating agent routing as a structured, two-stage decision problem is an effective and practical design point for AI-agent systems. AgentGate demonstrates that small, resource-efficient models can handle routing competitively, which is particularly important for deployments with strict latency, privacy, or cost budgets. Structured routing thus provides a promising foundation for scalable, privacy-aware Internet-of-Agents architectures, where routing decisions must be reliable despite limited compute and heterogeneous environments.

title: Tool-MCoT: Tool Augmented Multimodal Chain-of-Thought for Content Safety Moderation
summary: The growth of online platforms and user content requires strong content moderation systems that can handle complex inputs from various media types. While large language models (LLMs) are effective, their high computational cost and latency present significant challenges for scalable deployment. To address this, we introduce Tool-MCoT, a small language model (SLM) fine-tuned for content safety moderation leveraging external framework. By training our model on tool-augmented chain-of-thought data generated by LLM, we demonstrate that the SLM can learn to effectively utilize these tools to improve its reasoning and decision-making. Our experiments show that the fine-tuned SLM achieves significant performance gains. Furthermore, we show that the model can learn to use these tools selectively, achieving a balance between moderation accuracy and inference efficiency by calling tools only when necessary.

AI.tldr: They propose Tool-MCoT, a small language model for multi-modal content moderation that leverages external tools via chain-of-thought training, achieving near-LLM performance with lower cost and latency.
AI.motivation: Online platforms must moderate large volumes of complex, multi-format user content. Using large language models for this is accurate but too slow and expensive for production-scale systems, so there is a need for lighter models that still reason well about safety but run efficiently.
AI.method: They fine-tune a small language model using chain-of-thought examples produced by a larger LLM that already uses an external tool framework. The training data shows the SLM not just the final moderation decisions but also when and how the LLM invokes external tools. The SLM is thus trained to imitate tool-augmented reasoning and to decide adaptively when to call tools for difficult cases.
AI.result: Experiments show that the fine-tuned small model substantially improves its moderation performance compared with its non–tool-augmented baseline. It can approximate the large model’s safety judgments more accurately while retaining the small model’s computational advantages. The model learns to invoke tools only on harder inputs, preserving efficiency while boosting accuracy compared to always-tool or never-tool usage.
AI.conclusion: Tool-MCoT demonstrates that small language models can be trained to use external tools and chain-of-thought effectively for content safety moderation. This yields a practical trade-off: near-LLM-level moderation quality with significantly reduced inference cost and latency, enabled by selective tool calling rather than relying solely on a large model.

title: ATANT: An Evaluation Framework for AI Continuity
comment: 7 pages, 8 tables. Framework and evaluation protocol available at https://github.com/Kenotic-Labs/ATANT
summary: We present ATANT (Automated Test for Acceptance of Narrative Truth), an open evaluation framework for measuring continuity in AI systems: the ability to persist, update, disambiguate, and reconstruct meaningful context across time. While the AI industry has produced memory components (RAG pipelines, vector databases, long context windows, profile layers), no published framework formally defines or measures whether these components produce genuine continuity. We define continuity as a system property with 7 required properties, introduce a 10-checkpoint evaluation methodology that operates without an LLM in the evaluation loop, and present a narrative test corpus of 250 stories comprising 1,835 verification questions across 6 life domains. We evaluate a reference implementation across 5 test suite iterations, progressing from 58% (legacy architecture) to 100% in isolated mode (250 stories) and 100% in 50-story cumulative mode, with 96% at 250-story cumulative scale. The cumulative result is the primary measure: when 250 distinct life narratives coexist in the same database, the system must retrieve the correct fact for the correct context without cross-contamination. ATANT is system-agnostic, model-independent, and designed as a sequenced methodology for building and validating continuity systems. The framework specification, example stories, and evaluation protocol are available at https://github.com/Kenotic-Labs/ATANT. The full 250-story corpus will be released incrementally.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ATANT, an open, system-agnostic evaluation framework to formally measure narrative continuity in AI systems using a structured, LLM-free test protocol and a large corpus of life-story narratives.
AI.motivation: Existing AI memory tools (RAG, vector DBs, long contexts, profiles) lack a formal, standardized way to evaluate whether they actually maintain coherent continuity of user or narrative context over time. The authors aim to fill this gap by defining continuity as a precise system property and by providing a concrete, reproducible benchmark and methodology.
AI.method: They formally define continuity in terms of 7 required properties and create a 10-checkpoint evaluation methodology that does not rely on LLMs in the evaluation loop. They build a narrative test corpus of 250 life stories across 6 domains, with 1,835 verification questions, and design tests for both isolated and cumulative modes, where many narratives share the same database. They provide a reference implementation and iterate on the system architecture across five test-suite runs.
AI.result: The reference system improves from 58% accuracy with a legacy architecture to 100% in isolated mode (all 250 stories) and 100% in 50-story cumulative mode, achieving 96% accuracy at full 250-story cumulative scale. The key metric is performance under cumulative conditions, where many distinct narratives coexist without cross-contamination of facts.
AI.conclusion: ATANT offers a model- and system-agnostic, open framework to define, build, and validate continuity in AI systems.
