By providing clear properties, a staged evaluation methodology, and a growing narrative corpus, it enables rigorous and comparable assessment of context continuity capabilities across different architectures and memory components.

title: A Comparative Study of Demonstration Selection for Practical Large Language Models-based Next POI Prediction
summary: This paper investigates demonstration selection strategies for predicting a user's next point-of-interest (POI) using large language models (LLMs), aiming to accurately forecast a user's subsequent location based on historical check-in data. While in-context learning (ICL) with LLMs has recently gained attention as a promising alternative to traditional supervised approaches, the effectiveness of ICL significantly depends on the selected demonstration. Although previous studies have examined methods such as random selection, embedding-based selection, and task-specific selection, there remains a lack of comprehensive comparative analysis among these strategies. To bridge this gap and clarify the best practices for real-world applications, we comprehensively evaluate existing demonstration selection methods alongside simpler heuristic approaches such as geographical proximity, temporal ordering, and sequential patterns. Extensive experiments conducted on three real-world datasets indicate that these heuristic methods consistently outperform more complex and computationally demanding embedding-based methods, both in terms of computational cost and prediction accuracy. Notably, in certain scenarios, LLMs using demonstrations selected by these simpler heuristic methods even outperform existing fine-tuned models, without requiring further training. Our source code is available at: https://github.com/ryonsd/DS-LLM4POI.

AI.tldr: They compare different ways to choose in-context demonstrations for LLM-based next POI prediction and find that simple heuristics (geo, time, sequence) beat complex embedding-based methods and can even outperform fine-tuned models without extra training.
AI.motivation: Next POI prediction is important for location-based services, and LLMs with in-context learning are an attractive alternative to supervised models. However, ICL performance is highly sensitive to which demonstrations are selected, and prior work has proposed multiple selection strategies without a systematic, fair comparison. Practitioners lack clear guidance on which strategy works best in realistic, resource-constrained scenarios.
AI.method: They formulate next POI prediction as an ICL task for LLMs and implement various demonstration selection strategies: existing approaches like random, embedding-based, and task-specific selection, along with simple heuristic strategies based on geographical proximity, temporal ordering, and sequential patterns in user check-ins. They then run extensive experiments on three real-world POI datasets, comparing prediction accuracy and computational cost across these strategies and against fine-tuned baselines.
AI.result: Across three real-world datasets, heuristic demonstration selection methods (geo, temporal, sequential) consistently outperform embedding-based methods in both accuracy and efficiency. In some settings, LLMs using demonstrations chosen by these heuristics even surpass specialized fine-tuned models, all without any additional training of the LLM.
AI.conclusion: For next POI prediction using LLMs with ICL, simple heuristic demonstration selection strategies are not only more computationally efficient but also more accurate than more complex embedding-based methods. This suggests that, in practical applications, prioritizing domain-informed, low-cost heuristics can yield state-of-the-art performance without the need for fine-tuning, and provides clearer guidance on best practices for deploying LLM-based POI recommenders.

title: Steering the Verifiability of Multimodal AI Hallucinations
summary: AI applications driven by multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are prone to hallucinations and pose considerable risks to human users. Crucially, such hallucinations are not equally problematic: some hallucination contents could be detected by human users(i.e., obvious hallucinations), while others are often missed or require more verification effort(i.e., elusive hallucinations). This indicates that multimodal AI hallucinations vary significantly in their verifiability. Yet, little research has explored how to control this property for AI applications with diverse security and usability demands. To address this gap, we construct a dataset from 4,470 human responses to AI-generated hallucinations and categorize these hallucinations into obvious and elusive types based on their verifiability by human users. Further, we propose an activation-space intervention method that learns separate probes for obvious and elusive hallucinations. We reveal that obvious and elusive hallucinations elicit different intervention probes, allowing for fine-grained control over the model's verifiability. Empirical results demonstrate the efficacy of this approach and show that targeted interventions yield superior performance in regulating corresponding verifiability. Moreover, simply mixing these interventions enables flexible control over the verifiability required for different scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to control how easily humans can detect hallucinations in multimodal LLMs, distinguishing between obvious and elusive hallucinations and intervening in the model’s activation space to tune this verifiability.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs often hallucinate, but not all hallucinations are equally risky. Some are clearly wrong and easy for humans to catch, while others are subtle and harder to verify, posing greater safety risks. Existing work mostly tries to reduce hallucinations overall, without differentiating by how verifiable they are or allowing applications to adjust hallucination verifiability according to different security and usability needs. This gap motivates a systematic study and control mechanism for the verifiability of hallucinations in MLLMs.
AI.method: The authors build a dataset from 4,470 human responses to AI-generated hallucinations, labeling hallucinations as obvious or elusive depending on how easily humans can detect them. They then operate in the model’s activation space, learning separate linear probes/interventions associated with obvious vs. elusive hallucinations. These probes are used to intervene on internal activations to increase or decrease the tendency toward specific types of hallucinations, effectively controlling the verifiability of model outputs.
AI.result: They find that obvious and elusive hallucinations correspond to distinct directions in activation space, i.e., they require different probes. Using these targeted probes, they can more effectively regulate whether the model’s hallucinations are easy or hard for humans to verify. Empirical evaluations show that interventions tuned to one hallucination type best control that specific type’s verifiability, and that combining probes provides a smooth, adjustable trade-off between levels of verifiability.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that hallucination verifiability is a controllable property in multimodal LLMs. By distinguishing obvious from elusive hallucinations and learning separate activation-space interventions, one can achieve fine-grained, scenario-dependent control over how detectable hallucinations are to humans. This offers a new safety and usability mechanism beyond just globally reducing hallucination rates.

title: Extracting Breast Cancer Phenotypes from Clinical Notes: Comparing LLMs with Classical Ontology Methods
summary: A significant amount of data held in Oncology Electronic Medical Records (EMRs) is contained in unstructured provider notes -- including but not limited to the chemotherapy (or cancer treatment) outcome, different biomarkers, the tumor's location, sizes, and growth patterns of a patient. The clinical studies show that the majority of oncologists are comfortable providing these valuable insights in their notes in a natural language rather than the relevant structured fields of an EMR. The major contribution of this research is to report an LLM-based framework to process provider notes and extract valuable medical knowledge and phenotype mentioned above, with a focus on the domain of oncology. In this paper, we focus on extracting phenotypes related to breast cancer using our LLM framework, and then compare its performance with earlier works that used knowledge-driven annotation system, paired with the NCIt Ontology Annotator. The results of the study show that an LLM-based information extraction framework can be easily adapted to extract phenotypes with an accuracy that is comparable to the classical ontology-based methods. However, once trained, they could be easily fine-tuned to cater for other cancer types and diseases.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and evaluates an LLM-based framework to extract oncology-related phenotypes (e.g., treatment outcomes, biomarkers, tumor characteristics) from unstructured EMR provider notes, showing performance comparable to ontology-based methods.
AI.motivation: Oncology EMRs contain rich but largely unstructured information in provider notes—such as treatment outcomes, biomarkers, and tumor characteristics—because clinicians prefer writing in natural language instead of filling structured fields. This unstructuredness limits secondary use of data for research, analytics, and decision support. Existing ontology-based annotation systems can extract some of this information but may be less flexible and harder to adapt to new disease areas. The authors are motivated to build a more adaptable and scalable extraction approach using large language models to unlock this clinical content, starting with breast cancer phenotypes.
AI.method: The authors design an LLM-based information extraction framework tailored to oncology provider notes. They apply it to breast cancer, configuring the model to identify and extract key phenotypes such as chemotherapy outcomes, biomarkers, and tumor location/size/growth patterns from free-text clinical notes. They then empirically compare the performance of this LLM framework against a traditional knowledge-driven system that uses the NCIt Ontology Annotator, evaluating extraction accuracy on breast-cancer-related phenotypes.
AI.result: The LLM-based framework achieves phenotype extraction accuracy that is comparable to that of the established ontology-based method (NCIt-based annotator) when applied to breast cancer notes. This indicates that an LLM can reliably recover key oncology phenotypes from unstructured text at a level similar to classical ontology-driven pipelines.
AI.conclusion: An LLM-based extraction system is a viable alternative to traditional ontology-based methods for mining oncology phenotypes from free-text EMR notes, achieving comparable accuracy while offering greater adaptability. Once trained for a particular domain (e.g., breast cancer), the framework can be efficiently fine-tuned and repurposed for other cancer types and diseases, suggesting good scalability and flexibility for broader clinical NLP applications.

title: TurboAgent: An LLM-Driven Autonomous Multi-Agent Framework for Turbomachinery Aerodynamic Design
summary:
The aerodynamic design of turbomachinery is a complex and tightly coupled multi-stage process involving geometry generation, performance prediction, optimization, and high-fidelity physical validation. Existing intelligent design approaches typically focus on individual stages or rely on loosely coupled pipelines, making fully autonomous end-to-end design challenging.To address this issue, this study proposes TurboAgent, a large language model (LLM)-driven autonomous multi-agent framework for turbomachinery aerodynamic design and optimization. The LLM serves as the core for task planning and coordination, while specialized agents handle generative design, rapid performance prediction, multi-objective optimization, and physics-based validation. The framework transforms traditional trial-and-error design into a data-driven collaborative workflow, with high-fidelity simulations retained for final verification.A transonic single-rotor compressor is used for validation. The results show strong agreement between target performance, generated designs, and CFD simulations. The coefficients of determination (R2) for mass flow rate, total pressure ratio, and isentropic efficiency all exceed 0.91, with normalized RMSE values below 8%. The optimization agent further improves isentropic efficiency by 1.61% and total pressure ratio by 3.02%. The complete workflow can be executed within approximately 30 minutes under parallel computing.
  These results demonstrate that TurboAgent enables an autonomous closed-loop design process from natural language requirements to final design generation, providing an efficient and scalable paradigm for turbomachinery aerodynamic design

AI.tldr: Proposes TurboAgent, an LLM-driven multi-agent framework that autonomously performs end-to-end turbomachinery aerodynamic design, from natural-language requirements to CFD-validated designs.
AI.motivation: Turbomachinery aerodynamic design is a complex, multi-stage, and tightly coupled process (geometry generation, performance prediction, optimization, and validation). Existing intelligent design approaches typically treat stages separately or use loosely coupled pipelines, which makes it hard to achieve a fully autonomous, efficient, and reliable end-to-end design workflow.
AI.method: Introduce TurboAgent, an autonomous multi-agent framework centered on a large language model. The LLM handles task planning and coordination across agents responsible for generative geometry design, fast surrogate-based performance prediction, multi-objective optimization, and high-fidelity physics-based CFD validation. The system is tested on a transonic single-rotor compressor case, running the full design loop from natural-language specifications to final design under parallel computing.
AI.result: For the test compressor, the generated designs show strong agreement between target performance and CFD simulations. Surrogate predictions achieve R^2 > 0.91 and normalized RMSE < 8% for mass flow rate, total pressure ratio, and isentropic efficiency. The optimization agent improves isentropic efficiency by 1.61% and total pressure ratio by 3.02%. The entire design workflow completes in about 30 minutes with parallel computation.
AI.conclusion: TurboAgent realizes an autonomous, closed-loop aerodynamic design process that starts from natural-language requirements and ends with CFD-validated turbomachinery designs. It significantly improves design efficiency and shows good prediction accuracy, suggesting a scalable and practical paradigm for future turbomachinery aerodynamic design and optimization.

title: TelcoAgent-Bench: A Multilingual Benchmark for Telecom AI Agents
summary: The integration of large language model (LLM) agents into telecom networks introduces new challenges, related to intent recognition, tool execution, and resolution generation, while taking into consideration different operational constraints. In this paper, we introduce TelcoAgent-Bench and TelcoAgent-Metrics, a Telecom-specific benchmarking framework for evaluating multilingual telecom LLM agents. The proposed framework assesses the semantic understanding as well as process-level alignment with structured troubleshooting flows and stability across repeated scenario variations. Our contribution includes a structured suite of metrics that assess intent recognition, ordered tool execution, resolution correctness, and stability across scenario variations, with the aim of quantifying the reliability and operational consistency of LLM agents in telecom environments. The framework is designed to operate in both English and Arabic, to address the need for multilingual agent deployment in operational network environments. Our experimental results show that although recent instruct-tuned models can understand telecom problems in a reasonable way, they usually struggle to consistently follow the required troubleshooting steps and to maintain stable behavior when exposed to different variations of the same scenario. This performance gap becomes more pronounced in unconstrained and bilingual settings.

AI.tldr: Introduces TelcoAgent-Bench and TelcoAgent-Metrics, a telecom-specific, multilingual benchmarking framework for LLM agents, focusing on semantic understanding, process alignment, and stability in troubleshooting tasks.
AI.motivation: LLM agents are being integrated into telecom networks, but face challenges in reliably recognizing intents, executing tools in the correct order, and generating correct resolutions under operational constraints, especially in multilingual settings. There is a need for a domain-specific benchmark to measure and improve their reliability and consistency.
AI.method: Design a Telecom-specific benchmarking framework (TelcoAgent-Bench) and an associated metric suite (TelcoAgent-Metrics) that evaluates LLM agents on semantic understanding, alignment with structured troubleshooting flows, ordered tool usage, resolution correctness, and behavioral stability across scenario variations, in both English and Arabic. Conduct experiments on recent instruct-tuned LLMs using the proposed benchmark.
AI.result: Experiments show that while recent instruct-tuned LLMs understand telecom problems reasonably well, they often fail to robustly follow the prescribed troubleshooting steps, struggle with ordered tool execution, and exhibit instability when facing variations of the same scenario, especially in unconstrained or bilingual conditions.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM agents are not yet reliably process-aligned or stable enough for telecom troubleshooting in realistic, multilingual operational environments. TelcoAgent-Bench and TelcoAgent-Metrics provide a structured way to quantify these gaps and can guide future improvements in telecom-specific LLM agent design and training.

title: Distributional Open-Ended Evaluation of LLM Cultural Value Alignment Based on Value Codebook
summary: As LLMs are globally deployed, aligning their cultural value orientations is critical for safety and user engagement. However, existing benchmarks face the Construct-Composition-Context ($C^3$) challenge: relying on discriminative, multiple-choice formats that probe value knowledge rather than true orientations, overlook subcultural heterogeneity, and mismatch with real-world open-ended generation. We introduce DOVE, a distributional evaluation framework that directly compares human-written text distributions with LLM-generated outputs. DOVE utilizes a rate-distortion variational optimization objective to construct a compact value-codebook from 10K documents, mapping text into a structured value space to filter semantic noise. Alignment is measured using unbalanced optimal transport, capturing intra-cultural distributional structures and sub-group diversity. Experiments across 12 LLMs show that DOVE achieves superior predictive validity, attaining a 31.56% correlation with downstream tasks, while maintaining high reliability with as few as 500 samples per culture.

AI.tldr: DOVE is a new framework to evaluate how well LLMs align with human cultural value orientations by comparing distributions of human and model-generated texts instead of using multiple-choice tests.
AI.motivation: Current cultural value alignment benchmarks are limited: they focus on multiple-choice questions that test value knowledge, not the model’s actual value orientation; they ignore subcultural heterogeneity within cultures; and they do not resemble real-world open-ended generation. The authors want an evaluation method that is distributional, sensitive to intra-cultural diversity, and closer to natural language generation settings.
AI.method: They propose DOVE, a distributional evaluation framework. First, they build a compact value-codebook from 10K human-written documents using a rate-distortion variational optimization objective that maps free text into a structured value space while filtering semantic noise. Then, for any culture or subgroup, they map both human and LLM-generated texts into this codebook/value space and compute alignment using unbalanced optimal transport, which can compare entire distributions and capture intra-cultural structure and subgroup diversity. They test DOVE on 12 LLMs.
AI.result: DOVE demonstrates better predictive validity than prior benchmarks, achieving a 31.56% correlation with downstream tasks that reflect cultural value alignment. It is also data efficient, providing high reliability with as few as 500 samples per culture when evaluating models across 12 LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Distributional, codebook-based evaluation via DOVE is a more valid and reliable way to assess LLM cultural value alignment than discriminative multiple-choice tests. It captures subcultural variation, aligns better with real-world open-ended use, and can robustly evaluate models with relatively small sample sizes per culture.

title: Riemann-Bench: A Benchmark for Moonshot Mathematics
summary: Recent AI systems have achieved gold-medal-level performance on the International Mathematical Olympiad, demonstrating remarkable proficiency at competition-style problem solving. However, competition mathematics represents only a narrow slice of mathematical reasoning: problems are drawn from limited domains, require minimal advanced machinery, and can often reward insightful tricks over deep theoretical knowledge. We introduce \bench{}, a private benchmark of 25 expert-curated problems designed to evaluate AI systems on research-level mathematics that goes far beyond the olympiad frontier. Problems are authored by Ivy League mathematics professors, graduate students, and PhD-holding IMO medalists, and routinely took their authors weeks to solve independently. Each problem undergoes double-blind verification by two independent domain experts who must solve the problem from scratch, and yields a unique, closed-form solution assessed by programmatic verifiers. We evaluate frontier models as unconstrained research agents, with full access to coding tools, search, and open-ended reasoning, using an unbiased statistical estimator computed over 100 independent runs per problem. Our results reveal that all frontier models currently score below 10\%, exposing a substantial gap between olympiad-level problem solving and genuine research-level mathematical reasoning. By keeping the benchmark fully private, we ensure that measured performance reflects authentic mathematical capability rather than memorization of training data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new private benchmark of 25 research-level math problems and shows that current frontier AI models score below 10%, revealing a large gap between olympiad performance and real research-level mathematical reasoning.
AI.motivation: Although AI systems now reach gold-medal performance on International Mathematical Olympiad problems, these tasks cover only a narrow, competition-style subset of mathematics. They typically draw from limited areas, require little advanced theory, and often reward clever tricks instead of deep understanding. The authors want to rigorously assess whether AI can handle genuine research-level mathematics, which involves harder, less-structured problems that even experts need weeks to solve.
AI.method: The authors construct a benchmark, \bench{}, of 25 problems authored by Ivy League mathematics professors, graduate students, and PhD-holding IMO medalists. Each problem is research-level, took weeks for the authors to solve, and yields a unique closed-form solution. Problems undergo double-blind verification by two independent domain experts who must solve them from scratch. Solutions are checked with programmatic verifiers. Frontier AI models are then evaluated as unconstrained research agents with access to coding tools, search, and open-ended reasoning. Performance is estimated using an unbiased statistical estimator over 100 independent runs per problem.
AI.result: All tested frontier models achieve scores below 10% on the \bench{} benchmark, even with tool use and multiple independent runs, indicating very low success on these research-level math problems.
AI.conclusion: There is a significant and currently underappreciated gap between AI systems’ abilities on olympiad-style math problems and their capabilities on genuine research-level mathematics. Private, expert-curated benchmarks like \bench{} that are not exposed to training data are necessary to honestly measure true mathematical reasoning ability, and current systems remain far from matching expert human research performance.

title: Illocutionary Explanation Planning for Source-Faithful Explanations in Retrieval-Augmented Language Models
comment: 24 pages; Accepted for publication at XAI'2026
summary: Natural language explanations produced by large language models (LLMs) are often persuasive, but not necessarily scrutable: users cannot easily verify whether the claims in an explanation are supported by evidence. In XAI, this motivates a focus on faithfulness and traceability, i.e., the extent to which an explanation's claims can be grounded in, and traced back to, an explicit source. We study these desiderata in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for programming education, where textbooks provide authoritative evidence. We benchmark six LLMs on 90 Stack Overflow questions grounded in three programming textbooks and quantify source faithfulness via source adherence metrics. We find that non Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) models have median source adherence of 0%, while baseline RAG systems still exhibit low median adherence (22-40%, depending on the model). Motivated by Achinstein's illocutionary theory of explanation, we introduce illocutionary macro-planning as a descriptive design principle for source-faithful explanations and instantiate it with chain-of-illocution prompting (CoI), which expands a query into implicit explanatory questions that drive retrieval. Across models, CoI yields statistically significant gains (up to 63%) in source adherence, although absolute adherence remains moderate and the gains are weak or non-significant for some models. A user study with 165 retained participants (220 recruited) indicates that these gains do not harm satisfaction, relevance, or perceived correctness.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates and improves how well RAG-based LLM explanations for programming questions are actually grounded in textbook sources, proposing a new prompting strategy that significantly boosts source faithfulness without hurting user satisfaction.
AI.motivation: LLM explanations often sound convincing but may not be properly supported by underlying evidence, especially in educational settings where correctness and traceability to authoritative materials are critical. Existing RAG systems still generate many claims that are not grounded in their retrieved sources, so there is a need for methods that explicitly plan and constrain explanations to be source-faithful while preserving user-perceived quality.
AI.method: The authors construct a benchmark of 90 Stack Overflow questions that can be answered using three programming textbooks as authoritative sources. They evaluate six LLMs in both non-RAG and baseline RAG setups, and introduce source adherence metrics to quantify how well explanation statements are supported by the textbooks. Drawing on Achinstein’s illocutionary theory of explanation, they propose illocutionary macro-planning as a design principle and instantiate it through chain-of-illocution prompting (CoI), which decomposes the original user query into a set of implicit explanatory subquestions used to drive retrieval and guide generation. They compare CoI-augmented RAG against baseline RAG across models and conduct a user study with 165 participants to assess satisfaction, relevance, and perceived correctness.
AI.result: Non-RAG LLMs show essentially no source faithfulness, with a median source adherence of 0%. Baseline RAG systems improve adherence but remain relatively low, with medians between 22% and 40% depending on the model. Applying the proposed CoI prompting on top of RAG yields statistically significant improvements in source adherence across models, with gains up to 63% relative improvement. However, absolute adherence levels are still only moderate, and for some models the improvements are small or not statistically significant. The user study shows that these faithfulness gains do not degrade user satisfaction, perceived relevance of answers, or perceived correctness.
AI.conclusion: RAG alone is insufficient to guarantee that LLM explanations are well grounded in their evidence sources, even when authoritative materials like textbooks are available. Illocutionary macro-planning, instantiated as chain-of-illocution prompting, can substantially increase the degree to which explanations adhere to their sources without harming users’ subjective experience. Nonetheless, source adherence remains far from perfect, suggesting that further work is needed on planning, retrieval, and generation strategies to produce truly scrutable, source-faithful explanations in educational and other high-stakes domains.

title: Beyond Surface Judgments: Human-Grounded Risk Evaluation of LLM-Generated Disinformation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can generate persuasive narratives at scale, raising concerns about their potential use in disinformation campaigns. Assessing this risk ultimately requires understanding how readers receive such content. In practice, however, LLM judges are increasingly used as a low-cost substitute for direct human evaluation, even though whether they faithfully track reader responses remains unclear. We recast evaluation in this setting as a proxy-validity problem and audit LLM judges against human reader responses. Using 290 aligned articles, 2,043 paired human ratings, and outputs from eight frontier judges, we examine judge--human alignment in terms of overall scoring, item-level ordering, and signal dependence. We find persistent judge--human gaps throughout. Relative to humans, judges are typically harsher, recover item-level human rankings only weakly, and rely on different textual signals, placing more weight on logical rigour while penalizing emotional intensity more strongly. At the same time, judges agree far more with one another than with human readers. These results suggest that LLM judges form a coherent evaluative group that is much more aligned internally than it is with human readers, indicating that internal agreement is not evidence of validity as a proxy for reader response.

AI.tldr: The paper audits how well LLM “judges” of persuasive articles match real human readers, and finds that LLMs agree strongly with each other but poorly with humans, so internal LLM agreement is not a valid substitute for measuring reader response.
AI.motivation: LLMs can cheaply evaluate large amounts of generated text and are increasingly used as stand-ins for human evaluators of persuasiveness and potential disinformation. However, it is unclear whether these automated judgements truly reflect how human readers perceive and rate such content, which is critical for risk assessment and for research that uses LLMs as evaluators.
AI.method: The authors frame evaluation as a proxy-validity problem: LLM judges are a proxy for human reader responses. They collect 290 aligned persuasive articles and 2,043 paired human ratings, and obtain evaluations from eight state-of-the-art LLM judges. They then compare LLM and human evaluations in three ways: (1) overall score distributions (are LLMs harsher or more lenient than humans?), (2) item-level ordering (do LLMs rank items in a similar order to humans?), and (3) signal dependence (which textual features drive ratings for humans vs LLMs?).
AI.result: Across all analyses, they find systematic discrepancies between LLM judges and human readers. LLMs tend to give harsher evaluations than humans, they reproduce human item-level ranking patterns only weakly, and they rely on a different mix of text signals—overemphasizing logical rigor and penalizing emotional intensity more than humans do. Meanwhile, the different LLM judges are highly consistent with each other, displaying much stronger internal agreement than agreement with human judgments.
AI.conclusion: LLM judges behave as a coherent evaluative cluster that is well-aligned internally but misaligned with human readers. Therefore, strong agreement among LLMs is not evidence that they are valid proxies for reader response. Relying on LLM judges to stand in for humans, especially in sensitive domains like disinformation and persuasion, can be misleading and demands more careful validation.

title: Invisible Influences: Investigating Implicit Intersectional Biases through Persona Engineering in Large Language Models
comment: 11 pages, 5 figures, ACM WebScience Conference, 6 Tables
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at human-like language generation but often embed and amplify implicit, intersectional biases, especially under persona-driven contexts. Existing bias audits rely on static, embedding-based tests (CEAT, I-WEAT, I-SEAT) that quantify absolute association strengths. We show that they have limitations in capturing dynamic shifts when models adopt social roles. We address this gap by introducing the Bias Amplification Differential and Explainability Score (BADx): a novel, scalable metric that measures persona-induced bias amplification and integrates local explainability insights. BADx comprises three components - differential bias scores (BAD, based on CEAT, I-WEAT, I-SEAT),Persona Sensitivity Index (PSI), and Volatility (Standard Deviation), augmented by LIME-based analysis for emphasizing explainability. This study is divided and performed as two different tasks. Task 1 establishes static bias baselines, and Task 2 applies six persona frames (marginalized and structurally advantaged) to measure BADx, PSI, and volatility. This is studied across five state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4o, DeepSeek-R1, LLaMA-4, Claude 4.0 Sonnet and Gemma-3n E4B). Results show persona context significantly modulates bias. GPT-4o exhibits high sensitivity and volatility; DeepSeek-R1 suppresses bias but with erratic volatility; LLaMA-4 maintains low volatility and a stable bias profile with limited amplification; Claude 4.0 Sonnet achieves balanced modulation; and Gemma-3n E4B attains the lowest volatility with moderate amplification. BADx performs better than static methods by revealing context-sensitive biases overlooked in static methods. Our unified method offers a systematic way to detect dynamic implicit intersectional bias in five popular LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes BADx, a new metric to detect how LLM biases change when different personas are applied, showing that persona context can amplify or dampen implicit, intersectional biases that static tests miss.
AI.motivation: Existing bias evaluation methods for LLMs (e.g., CEAT, I-WEAT, I-SEAT) are static and embedding-based, measuring absolute associations but failing to capture how biases dynamically shift when a model is conditioned with different social roles or personas. Since LLMs are frequently used in persona-driven settings (e.g., roleplay, chat agents), understanding how these contexts modulate bias is crucial for safety and fairness. The authors aim to fill this gap by creating a metric that explicitly measures persona-induced bias amplification and provides interpretable insights.
AI.method: The authors design BADx (Bias Amplification Differential and Explainability Score), which extends existing association tests (CEAT, I-WEAT, I-SEAT) into a differential, persona-aware framework. BADx has three quantitative components: (1) BAD – a differential bias score comparing bias under persona prompts versus a static baseline; (2) Persona Sensitivity Index (PSI) – capturing how strongly different personas change bias; and (3) Volatility – the standard deviation of bias scores across runs or prompts. They complement these with LIME-based local explainability to highlight which input features or tokens drive the observed bias shifts. The study is run in two tasks: Task 1 builds static baselines using standard tests; Task 2 applies six persona frames (three marginalized, three structurally advantaged, implicitly) to the same models and computes BADx, PSI, and volatility under each persona condition. Five state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4o, DeepSeek-R1, LLaMA-4, Claude 4.0 Sonnet, Gemma-3n E4B) are evaluated.
AI.result: Across all five LLMs, persona conditioning significantly alters measured biases, with distinct patterns per model. GPT-4o shows high sensitivity to persona framing and high volatility in bias scores, indicating strong context-dependent modulation. DeepSeek-R1 tends to suppress overall bias levels but with unstable, erratic volatility. LLaMA-4 keeps relatively low volatility and a consistent bias profile, showing limited amplification under different personas. Claude 4.0 Sonnet appears to modulate bias in a more balanced manner, neither highly volatile nor strongly amplifying. Gemma-3n E4B exhibits the lowest volatility, with only moderate amplification of bias under persona shifts. BADx reveals these nuanced, context-driven behaviors that traditional static association tests fail to expose.
AI.conclusion: Persona prompts can substantially change the bias behavior of LLMs, so static embedding-based bias audits are insufficient on their own. BADx offers a unified, scalable, and more informative framework for detecting dynamic, intersectional, and context-sensitive biases by combining differential bias scores, persona sensitivity, volatility, and LIME-based explainability. The results across five popular LLMs demonstrate that different models have distinct bias modulation profiles under persona conditioning, and that BADx can uncover these patterns, making it a useful tool for safety evaluation and model comparison.

title: Explaining Neural Networks in Preference Learning: a Post-hoc Inductive Logic Programming Approach
comment: Under consideration for publication in Theory and Practice of Logic Programming (TPLP)
summary: In this paper, we propose using Learning from Answer Sets to approximate black-box models, such as Neural Networks (NN), in the specific case of learning user preferences. We specifically explore the use of ILASP (Inductive Learning of Answer Set Programs) to approximate preference learning systems through weak constraints. We have created a dataset on user preferences over a set of recipes, which is used to train the NNs that we aim to approximate with ILASP. Our experiments investigate ILASP both as a global and a local approximator of the NNs. These experiments address the challenge of approximating NNs working on increasingly high-dimensional feature spaces while achieving appropriate fidelity on the target model and limiting the increase in computational time. To handle this challenge, we propose a preprocessing step that exploits Principal Component Analysis to reduce the dataset's dimensionality while keeping our explanations transparent. Under consideration for publication in Theory and Practice of Logic Programming (TPLP).

AI.tldr: They use ILASP (a logic-based learner) with weak constraints plus PCA to approximate and explain neural-network-based preference models, both globally and locally, on a recipe-preference dataset, balancing fidelity and computational cost.
AI.motivation: Black-box models like neural networks can capture complex user preferences but are hard to understand and explain. In preference learning, stakeholders need interpretable models and faithful explanations of the black-box behavior. There is a need for methods that approximate such black-box models with symbolic, logic-based representations that remain transparent while still being accurate and scalable to realistic, higher-dimensional data.
AI.method: 1) Construct a dataset of user preferences over recipes and train neural networks as the primary, black-box preference models. 2) Use ILASP (Inductive Learning of Answer Set Programs) with weak constraints to learn logic programs that approximate the NN’s preference behavior. 3) Investigate two approximation modes: global approximation (one ILASP model for the entire input space) and local approximation (models focused on neighborhoods of particular instances). 4) Address high-dimensional feature spaces via a preprocessing step with Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to reduce dimensionality while attempting to preserve interpretability of learned rules and constraints. 5) Evaluate the learned ILASP models in terms of fidelity to the NN and computational efficiency as dimensionality grows.
AI.result: Experiments show that ILASP can approximate neural-network-based preference models both globally and locally with reasonable fidelity. However, fidelity and computational cost are sensitive to the dimensionality of the feature space. Applying PCA as a preprocessing step helps ILASP scale better in higher dimensions while largely maintaining the transparency of the resulting explanations. The study quantifies the trade-offs between fidelity, complexity of the learned logic programs, and runtime as dimensionality increases.
AI.conclusion: Learning from Answer Sets via ILASP is a viable way to approximate black-box preference-learning neural networks with interpretable, logic-based models. When combined with PCA-based dimensionality reduction, ILASP can act as a global or local surrogate model that balances fidelity and computational efficiency while keeping explanations understandable. This suggests an effective pathway for explainable preference learning, suitable for domains like recipe recommendation and potentially extensible to other black-box systems.

title: Unsupervised Neural Network for Automated Classification of Surgical Urgency Levels in Medical Transcriptions
comment: 7 pages. Published in Proceedings of the IISE Annual Conference & Expo 2025. DOI: 10.21872/2025IISE_6828
summary: Efficient classification of surgical procedures by urgency is paramount to optimize patient care and resource allocation within healthcare systems. This study introduces an unsupervised neural network approach to automatically categorize surgical transcriptions into three urgency levels: immediate, urgent, and elective. Leveraging BioClinicalBERT, a domain-specific language model, surgical transcripts are transformed into high-dimensional embeddings that capture their semantic nuances. These embeddings are subsequently clustered using both K-means and Deep Embedding Clustering (DEC) algorithms, in which DEC demonstrates superior performance in the formation of cohesive and well-separated clusters. To ensure clinical relevance and accuracy, the clustering results undergo validation through the Modified Delphi Method, which involves expert review and refinement. Following validation, a neural network that integrates Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) layers with BioClinicalBERT embeddings is developed for classification tasks. The model is rigorously evaluated using cross-validation and metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, which achieve robust performance and demonstrate strong generalization capabilities on unseen data. This unsupervised framework not only addresses the challenge of limited labeled data but also provides a scalable and reliable solution for real-time surgical prioritization, which ultimately enhances operational efficiency and patient outcomes in dynamic medical environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an unsupervised neural-network-based system to cluster and then classify surgical reports by urgency (immediate, urgent, elective) using BioClinicalBERT embeddings, DEC clustering, expert validation, and a BiLSTM classifier, achieving robust performance for real-time surgical prioritization.
AI.motivation: Hospitals must triage surgical cases by urgency to optimize patient outcomes and use limited resources efficiently, but high-quality labeled data for training supervised models are scarce and manual labeling is costly and slow. The authors aim to build a data-driven, scalable method that can learn urgency categories from unlabeled surgical transcripts and support real-time prioritization.
AI.method: 1) Convert surgical transcriptions into contextual embeddings using BioClinicalBERT; 2) apply unsupervised clustering (K-means and Deep Embedding Clustering, DEC) to group cases into three urgency levels; 3) compare clustering quality, finding DEC better at creating compact, well-separated clusters; 4) have clinical experts validate and refine clusters via a Modified Delphi Method to ensure they match real urgency categories; 5) use the validated cluster assignments as labels to train a neural classifier that combines BioClinicalBERT with BiLSTM layers; 6) evaluate via cross-validation using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score to test robustness and generalization.
AI.result: DEC outperforms K-means in producing clinically coherent clusters of surgical cases corresponding to immediate, urgent, and elective categories. After expert validation, the subsequent BioClinicalBERT+BiLSTM classifier achieves high accuracy and strong precision/recall/F1, maintaining good performance on unseen data, indicating effective generalization for urgency classification from text alone.
AI.conclusion: An unsupervised pipeline using domain-specific language modeling, deep clustering, and expert-in-the-loop validation can reliably infer urgency labels from unlabeled surgical transcriptions and train a robust classifier. This approach mitigates the need for large labeled datasets, scales to real-time use, and can improve surgical scheduling and triage, thereby enhancing operational efficiency and patient outcomes in dynamic clinical settings.

title: What's Missing in Screen-to-Action? Towards a UI-in-the-Loop Paradigm for Multimodal GUI Reasoning
summary: Existing Graphical User Interface (GUI) reasoning tasks remain challenging, particularly in UI understanding. Current methods typically rely on direct screen-based decision-making, which lacks interpretability and overlooks a comprehensive understanding of UI elements, ultimately leading to task failure. To enhance the understanding and interaction with UIs, we propose an innovative GUI reasoning paradigm called UI-in-the-Loop (UILoop). Our approach treats the GUI reasoning task as a cyclic Screen-UI elements-Action process. By enabling Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to explicitly learn the localization, semantic functions, and practical usage of key UI elements, UILoop achieves precise element discovery and performs interpretable reasoning. Furthermore, we introduce a more challenging UI Comprehension task centered on UI elements with three evaluation metrics. Correspondingly, we contribute a benchmark of 26K samples (UI Comprehension-Bench) to comprehensively evaluate existing methods' mastery of UI elements. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UILoop achieves state-of-the-art UI understanding performance while yielding superior results in GUI reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes UILoop, a new GUI reasoning paradigm that improves UI understanding by explicitly modeling UI elements in a cyclic Screen–UI elements–Action process, and introduces a new UI comprehension benchmark to evaluate it.
AI.motivation: Existing GUI reasoning models make decisions directly from raw screens, leading to poor interpretability and weak understanding of UI elements such as their locations, semantics, and functions. This shallow understanding causes frequent failures on complex GUI tasks. The authors aim to build a framework where the model explicitly understands and reasons over UI elements, not just pixels, to improve both performance and interpretability.
AI.method: The authors introduce UI-in-the-Loop (UILoop), which formulates GUI reasoning as a cyclic process: (1) perceive the screen, (2) identify and understand key UI elements (localization, semantic roles, and usage), and then (3) decide actions based on these elements, feeding results back into the loop. They adapt Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to explicitly learn about UI elements and their roles. Additionally, they define a new UI Comprehension task focused on element-level understanding, propose three evaluation metrics for it, and construct a 26K-sample benchmark dataset (UI Comprehension-Bench) to test different methods’ mastery of UI elements.
AI.result: UILoop attains state-of-the-art performance on UI understanding metrics and outperforms previous approaches on broader GUI reasoning tasks. On the newly proposed UI Comprehension-Bench with three evaluation metrics, UILoop demonstrates superior element localization and semantic/function understanding compared with existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly incorporating UI elements into the reasoning loop—rather than relying solely on raw screen decision-making—improves both interpretability and effectiveness of GUI reasoning. UILoop, together with the UI Comprehension task and UI Comprehension-Bench, provides a stronger and more realistic evaluation and leads to state-of-the-art results in UI understanding and GUI reasoning.

title: Blending Human and LLM Expertise to Detect Hallucinations and Omissions in Mental Health Chatbot Responses
summary:
As LLM-powered chatbots are increasingly deployed in mental health services, detecting hallucinations and omissions has become critical for user safety. However, state-of-the-art LLM-as-a-judge methods often fail in high-risk healthcare
  contexts, where subtle errors can have serious consequences. We show that leading LLM judges achieve only 52% accuracy on mental health counseling data, with some hallucination detection approaches exhibiting near-zero recall. We identify the root cause
  as LLMs' inability to capture nuanced linguistic and therapeutic patterns recognized by domain experts. To address this, we propose a framework that integrates human expertise with LLMs to extract interpretable, domain-informed features across five
  analytical dimensions: logical consistency, entity verification, factual accuracy, linguistic uncertainty, and professional appropriateness. Experiments on a public mental health dataset and a new human-annotated dataset show that traditional machine
  learning models trained on these features achieve 0.717 F1 on our custom dataset and 0.849 F1 on a public benchmark for hallucination detection, with 0.59-0.64 F1 for omission detection across both datasets. Our results demonstrate that combining domain
  expertise with automated methods yields more reliable and transparent evaluation than black-box LLM judging in high-stakes mental health applications.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates and improves hallucination and omission detection for LLM chatbots in mental health counseling by combining domain-expert features with traditional ML, outperforming LLM-as-a-judge approaches.
AI.motivation: LLM chatbots are being rapidly adopted in mental health services, where even subtle hallucinations or omissions can endanger users. Existing LLM-as-a-judge systems perform poorly in this high-risk domain and miss nuanced therapeutic errors that experts recognize. There is a need for safer, more accurate, and interpretable evaluation methods tailored to mental health counseling.
AI.method: The authors first benchmark leading LLM-as-a-judge methods on mental health counseling data and show low accuracy and recall for hallucination/omission detection. They then design a framework that encodes human domain expertise into interpretable, domain-informed features along five analytical dimensions: logical consistency, entity verification, factual accuracy, linguistic uncertainty, and professional appropriateness. Using these features, they train traditional machine learning classifiers and evaluate them on both a public mental health dataset and a newly created, human-annotated dataset.
AI.result: LLM judges achieve only 52% accuracy on mental health counseling tasks, with some hallucination detection methods having nearly zero recall. In contrast, ML models trained on expert-informed features reach 0.717 F1 on the custom dataset and 0.849 F1 on a public hallucination detection benchmark, and obtain 0.59–0.64 F1 for omission detection on both datasets. These models provide better overall performance and recall than LLM-as-a-judge baselines.
AI.conclusion: LLM-as-a-judge approaches are unreliable for detecting hallucinations and omissions in high-stakes mental health counseling. Incorporating domain expertise into interpretable features and training traditional ML models on them yields substantially better, more reliable, and more transparent evaluation. This human-LLM hybrid framework is better suited for safety-critical mental health applications than black-box LLM judges.

title: EmoMAS: Emotion-Aware Multi-Agent System for High-Stakes Edge-Deployable Negotiation with Bayesian Orchestration
summary: Large language models (LLMs) has been widely used for automated negotiation, but their high computational cost and privacy risks limit deployment in privacy-sensitive, on-device settings such as mobile assistants or rescue robots. Small language models (SLMs) offer a viable alternative, yet struggle with the complex emotional dynamics of high-stakes negotiation. We introduces EmoMAS, a Bayesian multi-agent framework that transforms emotional decision-making from reactive to strategic. EmoMAS leverages a Bayesian orchestrator to coordinate three specialized agents: game-theoretic, reinforcement learning, and psychological coherence models. The system fuses their real-time insights to optimize emotional state transitions while continuously updating agent reliability based on negotiation feedback. This mixture-of-agents architecture enables online strategy learning without pre-training. We further introduce four high-stakes, edge-deployable negotiation benchmarks across debt, healthcare, emergency response, and educational domains. Through extensive agent-to-agent simulations across all benchmarks, both SLMs and LLMs equipped with EmoMAS consistently surpass all baseline models in negotiation performance while balancing ethical behavior. These results show that strategic emotional intelligence is also the key driver of negotiation success. By treating emotional expression as a strategic variable within a Bayesian multi-agent optimization framework, EmoMAS establishes a new paradigm for effective, private, and adaptive negotiation AI suitable for high-stakes edge deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes EmoMAS, a Bayesian multi-agent framework that gives small and large language models strategic emotional intelligence for automated negotiation, enabling effective, ethical, on-device negotiation in high-stakes scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based negotiation systems are powerful but too computationally expensive and pose privacy risks for on-device or edge deployment. Small language models are more suitable for such constrained settings but lack the ability to handle complex, high-stakes emotional dynamics in negotiation. There is a need for a negotiation framework that is both resource-efficient and emotionally strategic, suitable for private, edge-based high-stakes applications (e.g., mobile assistants, rescue robots).
AI.method: The authors design EmoMAS, a Bayesian multi-agent system with a central Bayesian orchestrator coordinating three specialized agents: (1) a game-theoretic agent, (2) a reinforcement learning agent, and (3) a psychological coherence agent. These agents provide real-time recommendations about negotiation moves and emotional expressions. The orchestrator fuses their signals, optimizes the emotional state transitions as a strategic variable, and continuously updates its belief about each agent’s reliability using negotiation feedback. This enables online learning and adaptation without pre-training, operating as a mixture-of-agents architecture that can run with both SLMs and LLMs.
AI.result: Across four newly introduced high-stakes, edge-deployable negotiation benchmarks (debt, healthcare, emergency response, and educational domains), EmoMAS-enhanced SLMs and LLMs outperform all baseline negotiation models. The framework improves negotiation success rates while maintaining or improving ethical behavior. Simulations show that explicitly modeling and optimizing strategic emotional expression is a key factor in achieving superior negotiation performance.
AI.conclusion: Strategic emotional intelligence, modeled as controllable emotional state transitions within a Bayesian multi-agent optimization framework, is crucial to successful automated negotiation. EmoMAS demonstrates that even resource-constrained SLMs can attain high negotiation performance comparable to or better than larger models when equipped with this framework. The work establishes a new paradigm for private, adaptive, and ethically aware negotiation AI, especially suited to high-stakes edge deployment scenarios.

title: STDec: Spatio-Temporal Stability Guided Decoding for dLLMs
summary: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have achieved rapid progress, viewed as a promising alternative to the autoregressive paradigm. However, most dLLM decoders still adopt a global confidence threshold, and do not explicitly model local context from neighboring decoded states or temporal consistency of predicted token IDs across steps. To address this issue, we propose a simple spatio-temporal stability guided decoding approach, named STDec. We observe strong spatio-temporal stability in dLLM decoding: newly decoded tokens tend to lie near decoded neighbors, and their predicted IDs often remain consistent across several denoising steps. Inspired by this stability, our STDec includes spatial-aware decoding and temporal-aware decoding. The spatial-aware decoding dynamically generates the token-adaptive threshold by aggregating the decoded states of nearby tokens. The temporal-aware decoding relaxes the decoding thresholds for tokens whose predicted token IDs remain consistent over denoising steps. Our STDec is training-free and remains compatible with cache-based acceleration methods. Across textual reasoning and multimodal understanding benchmarks, STDec substantially improves throughput while maintaining comparable task performance score. Notably, on MBPP with LLaDA, STDec achieves up to 14.17x speedup with a comparable score. Homepage: https://yzchen02.github.io/STDec.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces STDec, a training-free, spatio-temporal stability guided decoding method for diffusion LLMs that significantly speeds up decoding while preserving task performance.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion LLM decoders use a global confidence threshold and ignore local spatial context among tokens as well as temporal consistency of token predictions across denoising steps, which leads to suboptimal efficiency and utilization of the inherent stability in diffusion decoding.
AI.method: The authors empirically observe spatio-temporal stability in diffusion LLM decoding: new tokens are usually close to already decoded neighbors, and predicted token IDs persist across multiple denoising steps. Leveraging this, they propose STDec, consisting of (1) spatial-aware decoding that computes token-specific adaptive thresholds by aggregating nearby decoded states; and (2) temporal-aware decoding that relaxes thresholds for tokens whose predicted IDs remain consistent over time. STDec is designed to be training-free and compatible with cache-based acceleration.
AI.result: On textual reasoning and multimodal understanding benchmarks, STDec substantially improves decoding throughput without degrading task performance; for example, on MBPP with LLaDA, it reaches up to 14.17x speedup while maintaining comparable accuracy scores.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly exploiting spatio-temporal stability in diffusion LLM decoding through adaptive spatial and temporal thresholds, STDec offers a practical, training-free way to accelerate diffusion LLMs while preserving their quality, and integrates smoothly with existing acceleration techniques.

title: CAFP: A Post-Processing Framework for Group Fairness via Counterfactual Model Averaging
summary: Ensuring fairness in machine learning predictions is a critical challenge, especially when models are deployed in sensitive domains such as credit scoring, healthcare, and criminal justice. While many fairness interventions rely on data preprocessing or algorithmic constraints during training, these approaches often require full control over the model architecture and access to protected attribute information, which may not be feasible in real-world systems. In this paper, we propose Counterfactual Averaging for Fair Predictions (CAFP), a model-agnostic post-processing method that mitigates unfair influence from protected attributes without retraining or modifying the original classifier. CAFP operates by generating counterfactual versions of each input in which the sensitive attribute is flipped, and then averaging the model's predictions across factual and counterfactual instances. We provide a theoretical analysis of CAFP, showing that it eliminates direct dependence on the protected attribute, reduces mutual information between predictions and sensitive attributes, and provably bounds the distortion introduced relative to the original model. Under mild assumptions, we further show that CAFP achieves perfect demographic parity and reduces the equalized odds gap by at least half the average counterfactual bias.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CAFP, a model-agnostic post-processing method that uses counterfactual averaging over sensitive attributes to make predictions fairer without retraining the original model.
AI.motivation: Existing fairness interventions often require access to model internals, retraining, or explicit use of protected attributes during training, which is impractical in many real-world deployments. There is a need for a simple, post-hoc, model-agnostic approach that can reduce unfair dependence on sensitive attributes while keeping the original classifier intact.
AI.method: The authors propose Counterfactual Averaging for Fair Predictions (CAFP). For each input, they construct counterfactual versions where the protected attribute is flipped, obtain the model’s predictions for both factual and counterfactual inputs, and then average these predictions. They provide theoretical analysis showing how this procedure affects dependence on sensitive attributes, information-theoretic properties, and fairness metrics.
AI.result: Theoretical results show that CAFP removes direct dependence of predictions on the protected attribute, reduces mutual information between predictions and the sensitive attribute, and guarantees that prediction distortion relative to the original model remains bounded. Under mild conditions, CAFP can achieve perfect demographic parity and provably shrinks the equalized odds gap by at least half the average counterfactual bias.
AI.conclusion: CAFP is an effective, post-processing, model-agnostic technique for improving fairness in existing machine learning systems without retraining. It uses counterfactual reasoning over sensitive attributes to adjust predictions, comes with theoretical guarantees on fairness and distortion, and can be applied in practical settings where control over the underlying model is limited.

title: Severity-Aware Weighted Loss for Arabic Medical Text Generation
summary: Large language models have shown strong potential for Arabic medical text generation; however, traditional fine-tuning objectives treat all medical cases uniformly, ignoring differences in clinical severity. This limitation is particularly critical in healthcare settings, where errors in severe cases contain higher clinical risk. In this work, we propose a severity-aware weighted loss for fine-tuning Arabic language models on medical complaint-response data. The method depends on soft severity probabilities to dynamically scale token-level loss contributions during optimization, thereby prioritizing clinically critical interactions without modifying model architectures. Experiments are conducted using the MAQA dataset, which provides Arabic medical complaints and trusted human responses. Severity labels and probabilistic scores are automatically derived using a fine-tuned AraBERT-based classifier and incorporated exclusively at the loss level. The proposed approach is evaluated across ten Arabic large language models of varying architectures and parameter scales. While standard cross-entropy fine-tuning yields only modest improvements, severity-aware optimization consistently achieves larger gains. Using a balanced weighting configuration, performance improves from 54.04% to 66.14% for AraGPT2-Base, from 59.16% to 67.18% for AraGPT2-Medium, and from 57.83% to 66.86% for Qwen2.5-0.5B, with peak performance reaching 67.18%. Overall, severity-aware fine-tuning delivers improvements of up to 12.10% over non-fine-tuned baselines, demonstrating robust and architecture-consistent gains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a severity-aware weighted loss for fine-tuning Arabic medical LLMs, giving higher importance to clinically severe cases and achieving consistent performance gains over standard fine-tuning across multiple models.
AI.motivation: Existing fine-tuning methods for Arabic medical text generation treat all clinical cases equally, which is problematic because mistakes in severe medical cases are much riskier than in mild ones. The authors want a way for models to focus learning more on high-severity, high-risk interactions without changing the model architecture.
AI.method: They introduce a severity-aware weighted loss function that scales token-level cross-entropy loss using soft severity probabilities. Severity scores for each complaint-response pair are obtained from a fine-tuned AraBERT-based classifier on the MAQA Arabic medical dataset. These probabilistic severity weights are applied only at the loss level during fine-tuning, allowing the model to prioritize high-severity instances while keeping architectures unchanged. Different weighting configurations and ten Arabic LLMs of various sizes and architectures are evaluated.
AI.result: Across ten Arabic LLMs, standard cross-entropy fine-tuning leads to only modest performance gains, whereas the severity-aware loss yields consistently larger improvements. For example, with a balanced weighting scheme, performance improves from 54.04% to 66.14% for AraGPT2-Base, from 59.16% to 67.18% for AraGPT2-Medium, and from 57.83% to 66.86% for Qwen2.5-0.5B, with a best reported score of 67.18%. Overall, the severity-aware method provides up to 12.10% improvement over non-fine-tuned baselines.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating severity-aware weighting into the training loss enables Arabic medical LLMs to better prioritize clinically critical cases, leading to sizable and architecture-robust performance gains over both non-fine-tuned models and standard fine-tuning. This demonstrates that adjusting optimization to reflect clinical risk, without altering model architectures, is an effective strategy for safer and more accurate medical text generation in Arabic.

title: A-MBER: Affective Memory Benchmark for Emotion Recognition
summary:
AI assistants that interact with users over time need to interpret the user's current emotional state in order to respond appropriately and personally. However, this capability remains insufficiently evaluated. Existing emotion datasets mainly assess local or instantaneous affect, while long-term memory benchmarks focus largely on factual recall, temporal consistency, or knowledge updating. As a result, current resources provide limited support for testing whether a model can use remembered interaction history to interpret a user's present affective state.
  We introduce A-MBER, an Affective Memory Benchmark for Emotion Recognition, to evaluate this capability. A-MBER focuses on present affective interpretation grounded in remembered multi-session interaction history. Given an interaction trajectory and a designated anchor turn, a model must infer the user's current affective state, identify historically relevant evidence, and justify its interpretation in a grounded way. The benchmark is constructed through a staged pipeline with explicit intermediate representations, including long-horizon planning, conversation generation, annotation, question construction, and final packaging. It supports judgment, retrieval, and explanation tasks, together with robustness settings such as modality degradation and insufficient-evidence conditions.
  Experiments compare local-context, long-context, retrieved-memory, structured-memory, and gold-evidence conditions within a unified framework. Results show that A-MBER is especially discriminative on the subsets it is designed to stress, including long-range implicit affect, high-dependency memory levels, trajectory-based reasoning, and adversarial settings. These findings suggest that memory supports affective interpretation not simply by providing more history, but by enabling more selective, grounded, and context-sensitive use of past interaction

AI.tldr: The paper presents A-MBER, a benchmark to evaluate whether AI assistants can use long-term conversational memory to interpret a user’s current emotional state.
AI.motivation: Current emotion recognition datasets focus on short, local affect, and long-term memory benchmarks emphasize factual recall and consistency rather than affect. There is no good way to test if a model can leverage remembered interaction history to understand how a user feels now, which is crucial for personable, emotionally aware AI assistants.
AI.method: The authors build A-MBER, a benchmark centered on multi-session interaction trajectories. For each trajectory and an anchor turn, models must infer the user’s current affect, retrieve historically relevant evidence, and provide grounded justifications. The benchmark is created via a staged pipeline with explicit intermediate representations, including long-horizon planning, synthetic conversation generation, annotation, question construction, and packaging. It includes multiple tasks—judgment, retrieval, and explanation—and robustness settings such as degraded modality and insufficient-evidence cases.
AI.result: Through experiments, the paper compares different ways of providing context and memory—local context only, very long context, retrieved memory, structured memory, and gold evidence—within a shared evaluation framework. A-MBER sharply differentiates model performance on challenging subsets involving long-range implicit affect, high memory-dependency, trajectory-based reasoning, and adversarial conditions.
AI.conclusion: The findings indicate that effective affective interpretation in dialogue agents depends not merely on having more conversational history but on using memory selectively and in a grounded, context-sensitive way. A-MBER offers a focused benchmark to stress-test and analyze these capabilities across models and memory architectures.

title: In-Context Learning in Speech Language Models: Analyzing the Role of Acoustic Features, Linguistic Structure, and Induction Heads
summary: In-Context Learning (ICL) has been extensively studied in text-only Language Models, but remains largely unexplored in the speech domain. Here, we investigate how linguistic and acoustic features affect ICL in Speech Language Models. We focus on the Text-to-Speech (TTS) task, which allows us to analyze ICL from two angles: (1) how accurately the model infers the task from the demonstrations (i.e., generating the correct spoken content), and (2) to what extent the model mimics the acoustic characteristics of the demonstration speech in its output. We find that speaking rate strongly affects ICL performance and is also mimicked in the output, whereas pitch range and intensity have little impact on performance and are not consistently reproduced. Finally, we investigate the role of induction heads in speech-based ICL and show that these heads play a causal role: ablating the top-k induction heads completely removes the model's ICL ability, mirroring findings from text-based ICL.

AI.tldr: The paper studies in-context learning (ICL) in speech language models for text-to-speech, analyzing how linguistic and acoustic features and induction heads affect ICL behavior.
AI.motivation: While ICL is well-studied in text-only language models, its behavior and mechanisms in speech language models remain unclear. Understanding how speech-related features like speaking rate, pitch, and intensity influence ICL can improve control, interpretability, and design of speech models, and test whether mechanisms like induction heads found in text models also underlie speech-based ICL.
AI.method: They analyze a Speech Language Model on text-to-speech tasks using in-context demonstrations. They evaluate two aspects: (1) task inference accuracy—whether the model produces the correct spoken content given examples, and (2) style transfer—how much the model mimics acoustic properties (speaking rate, pitch range, intensity) of the demonstration speech. They run controlled experiments varying these acoustic attributes and then perform mechanistic analysis of attention heads, especially induction heads, using ablations of the top-k such heads to test their causal role in speech-based ICL.
AI.result: Speaking rate has a strong influence on ICL performance and is reliably mimicked in generated speech, while pitch range and intensity have little effect on ICL accuracy and are not consistently reproduced. Ablating the top-k induction heads in the model removes its ability to perform in-context learning in speech tasks, indicating these heads are necessary for speech-based ICL, analogous to findings in text models.
AI.conclusion: ICL in speech language models depends heavily on temporal acoustic features like speaking rate and is implemented by induction heads similar to those in text models. Not all acoustic attributes are equally leveraged: pitch range and intensity matter less for ICL and are not robustly transferred. The shared role of induction heads across text and speech suggests a common underlying mechanism of ICL, informing future model design and control for speech applications.

title: Planning Task Shielding: Detecting and Repairing Flaws in Planning Tasks through Turning them Unsolvable
summary: Most research in planning focuses on generating a plan to achieve a desired set of goals. However, a goal specification can also be used to encode a property that should never hold, allowing a planner to identify a trace that would reach a flawed state. In such cases, the objective may shift to modifying the planning task to ensure that the flawed state is never reached-in other words, to make the planning task unsolvable. In this paper we introduce planning task shielding: the problem of detecting and repairing flaws in planning tasks. We propose $allmin$, an optimal algorithm that solves these tasks by minimally modifying the original actions to render the planning task unsolvable. We empirically evaluate the performance of $allmin$ in shielding planning tasks of increasing size, showing how it can effectively shield the system by turning the planning task unsolvable.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces planning task shielding, where planning tasks are deliberately modified so that unsafe or flawed states become unreachable, effectively making the task unsolvable.
AI.motivation: Traditional planning focuses on finding plans that achieve desired goals. However, sometimes the specification encodes states that must never be reached (e.g., unsafe or flawed states). In such settings, the aim is not to find a successful plan but to ensure that no plan can reach the flawed state. This creates a need for methods that can automatically detect and repair planning tasks so that these undesirable states are unreachable.
AI.method: The authors formalize the notion of planning task shielding as detecting and repairing flaws in planning tasks. They introduce ALLMIN, an optimal algorithm that minimally modifies the original actions in the planning domain so that the task becomes unsolvable. Minimality here means performing the smallest necessary changes to actions such that no plan exists that can reach the flawed state. They then run empirical evaluations on planning tasks of increasing size.
AI.result: Experimental results show that ALLMIN can effectively modify planning tasks so that they become unsolvable, thereby shielding the system from reaching flawed states. The algorithm scales to increasingly large tasks and successfully finds minimal modifications that block all plans to the undesired state.
AI.conclusion: Planning task shielding is a useful framework for preventing planners from reaching flawed or unsafe states by transforming the underlying planning problem into an unsolvable one. The proposed algorithm ALLMIN can optimally and minimally repair planning tasks, and empirical evidence indicates that it is effective and scales with task size.

title: A Severity-Based Curriculum Learning Strategy for Arabic Medical Text Generation
summary: Arabic medical text generation is increasingly needed to help users interpret symptoms and access general health guidance in their native language. Nevertheless, many existing methods assume uniform importance across training samples, overlooking differences in clinical severity. This simplification can hinder the model's ability to properly capture complex or high-risk cases. To overcome this issue, this work introduces a Severity-based Curriculum Learning Strategy for Arabic Medical Text Generation, where the training process is structured to move gradually from less severe to more critical medical conditions. The approach divides the dataset into ordered stages based on severity and incrementally exposes the model to more challenging cases during fine-tuning, allowing it to first learn basic medical patterns before addressing more complex scenarios. The proposed method is evaluated on a subset of the Medical Arabic Question Answering (MAQA) dataset, which includes Arabic medical questions describing symptoms alongside corresponding responses. In addition, the dataset is annotated with three severity levels (Mild, Moderate, and Critical) using a rule-based method developed in this study. The results demonstrate that incorporating severity-aware curriculum learning leads to consistent performance improvements across all tested models, with gains of around +4% to +7% over baseline models and +3% to +6% compared with conventional fine-tuning approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a severity-based curriculum learning strategy for Arabic medical text generation that trains models from mild to critical cases, using a severity-annotated subset of the MAQA dataset, and achieves 4–7% gains over baselines and 3–6% over standard fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing Arabic medical text generation methods treat all training samples as equally important, ignoring clinical severity differences.
