This can reduce performance on complex or high-risk cases, which are crucial in health-related applications. The authors aim to incorporate clinical severity into the training process to better handle challenging medical scenarios and provide more reliable Arabic medical guidance.
AI.method: They introduce a Severity-based Curriculum Learning Strategy where the training data is partitioned into stages according to three severity levels: Mild, Moderate, and Critical. A rule-based system is developed to automatically assign severity labels to questions in a subset of the MAQA dataset. During fine-tuning, the model is first trained on mild cases, then progressively exposed to moderate and finally critical cases, following the curriculum learning paradigm. Multiple Arabic medical text generation models are trained and evaluated with this curriculum and compared against baselines and standard fine-tuning.
AI.result: Severity-aware curriculum learning yields consistent performance improvements across all evaluated models. The reported gains are approximately +4% to +7% compared to baseline models and +3% to +6% compared with conventional fine-tuning, indicating better handling of medical question-answer generation when severity is incorporated into the training schedule.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating clinical severity into the curriculum of Arabic medical text generation enhances model performance and robustness, especially on more complex or critical cases. The rule-based severity annotation plus staged training from mild to critical conditions proves effective, suggesting that severity-aware curriculum learning is a promising direction for improving safety and quality in Arabic medical NLP systems.

title: EVGeoQA: Benchmarking LLMs on Dynamic, Multi-Objective Geo-Spatial Exploration
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable reasoning capabilities, their potential for purpose-driven exploration in dynamic geo-spatial environments remains under-investigated. Existing Geo-Spatial Question Answering (GSQA) benchmarks predominantly focus on static retrieval, failing to capture the complexity of real-world planning that involves dynamic user locations and compound constraints. To bridge this gap, we introduce EVGeoQA, a novel benchmark built upon Electric Vehicle (EV) charging scenarios that features a distinct location-anchored and dual-objective design. Specifically, each query in EVGeoQA is explicitly bound to a user's real-time coordinate and integrates the dual objectives of a charging necessity and a co-located activity preference. To systematically assess models in such complex settings, we further propose GeoRover, a general evaluation framework based on a tool-augmented agent architecture to evaluate the LLMs' capacity for dynamic, multi-objective exploration. Our experiments reveal that while LLMs successfully utilize tools to address sub-tasks, they struggle with long-range spatial exploration. Notably, we observe an emergent capability: LLMs can summarize historical exploration trajectories to enhance exploration efficiency. These findings establish EVGeoQA as a challenging testbed for future geo-spatial intelligence. The dataset and prompts are available at https://github.com/Hapluckyy/EVGeoQA/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EVGeoQA, a geo-spatial question answering benchmark centered on EV charging scenarios, and GeoRover, a tool-augmented framework to evaluate LLMs’ dynamic, multi-objective spatial exploration capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current geo-spatial QA benchmarks mostly test static information retrieval and do not model realistic planning tasks where a user moves in space and must satisfy multiple, often competing goals (e.g., reach a charger while also doing another activity). The authors want a benchmark and evaluation setup that capture dynamic user locations, compound constraints, and long-range spatial reasoning, particularly relevant to EV charging use cases.
AI.method: They construct EVGeoQA, a benchmark where each question is tied to a specific user GPS coordinate and requires satisfying two objectives simultaneously: (1) meeting an EV charging need and (2) matching a preferred co-located activity (e.g., shopping, dining). They then design GeoRover, a general evaluation framework based on a tool-augmented LLM agent. The agent can call geo-tools to explore the spatial environment step by step, plan routes, and search for options while being evaluated on its ability to solve these dynamic, multi-objective tasks.
AI.result: Experiments with LLM agents on EVGeoQA show that models are able to use tools for localized sub-tasks (e.g., querying nearby locations), but they perform poorly on tasks requiring broad, long-range spatial exploration and complex planning. However, the authors identify an emergent behavior: LLMs can leverage summaries of their past exploration trajectories to plan more efficiently, thereby improving exploration performance.
AI.conclusion: EVGeoQA, combined with the GeoRover framework, exposes limitations of current LLMs in dynamic, multi-objective geo-spatial reasoning while also revealing emergent strengths in using historical trajectory summaries. The benchmark serves as a challenging testbed for advancing geo-spatial intelligence and provides resources (dataset and prompts) for future research in this domain.

title: The Illusion of Superposition? A Principled Analysis of Latent Thinking in Language Models
summary: Latent reasoning via continuous chain-of-thoughts (Latent CoT) has emerged as a promising alternative to discrete CoT reasoning. Operating in continuous space increases expressivity and has been hypothesized to enable superposition: the ability to maintain multiple candidate solutions simultaneously within a single representation. Despite theoretical arguments, it remains unclear whether language models actually leverage superposition when reasoning using latent CoTs. We investigate this question across three regimes: a training-free regime that constructs latent thoughts as convex combinations of token embeddings, a fine-tuned regime where a base model is adapted to produce latent thoughts, and a from-scratch regime where a model is trained entirely with latent thoughts to solve a given task. Using Logit Lens and entity-level probing to analyze internal representations, we find that only models trained from scratch exhibit signs of using superposition. In the training-free and fine-tuned regimes, we find that the superposition either collapses or is not used at all, with models discovering shortcut solutions instead. We argue that this is due to two complementary phenomena: i) pretraining on natural language data biases models to commit to a token in the last layers ii) capacity has a huge effect on which solutions a model favors. Together, our results offer a unified explanation for when and why superposition arises in continuous chain-of-thought reasoning, and identify the conditions under which it collapses.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether continuous chain-of-thought representations in language models actually use superposition—holding multiple candidate solutions at once—and finds this only reliably happens when models are trained from scratch with latent CoTs, not when they’re adapted from pretrained models or used in a training-free way.
AI.motivation: Discrete chain-of-thought reasoning in language models operates at the token level and may limit expressivity. Continuous (latent) CoTs are hypothesized to be more expressive and to support superposition, potentially enabling richer reasoning. However, it’s unknown if real models truly exploit this superposition or just behave similarly to discrete CoTs, so the authors aim to empirically test how and when superposition arises in practice.
AI.method: They examine three regimes of using latent CoTs: (1) training-free, where latent thoughts are constructed as convex combinations of token embeddings; (2) fine-tuning, where a pretrained base model is adapted to emit and consume latent thoughts; and (3) from-scratch training, where a model is trained end-to-end on a task using latent thoughts. They analyze internal representations with tools like Logit Lens and entity-level probing to see whether multiple candidate solutions are simultaneously represented, and whether this persists through layers or collapses to a single answer.
AI.result: Only the from-scratch latent CoT models show clear evidence of superposition in their internal states. In the training-free and fine-tuned setups, the supposed superposed latent states either quickly collapse to a single candidate or are effectively ignored, with models instead finding shortcut strategies that don’t rely on maintaining multiple hypotheses. They also find that pretrained models are biased by their language modeling objective to commit to a single token in later layers, and that model capacity strongly influences which reasoning strategies are preferred.
AI.conclusion: Superposition in continuous chain-of-thoughts is not an automatic consequence of using continuous latent representations; it depends critically on how the model is trained. Pretrained language models adapted to latent CoTs tend to collapse superposition due to a bias to commit to specific tokens in later layers and to exploit simpler shortcuts, especially under capacity constraints. In contrast, models trained from scratch with latent CoTs can learn to genuinely maintain multiple candidate solutions in superposition. This provides a unified account of when superposition appears in continuous CoT reasoning and clarifies the conditions under which it fails to emerge.

title: Reason in Chains, Learn in Trees: Self-Rectification and Grafting for Multi-turn Agent Policy Optimization
summary: Reinforcement learning for Large Language Model agents is often hindered by sparse rewards in multi-step reasoning tasks. Existing approaches like Group Relative Policy Optimization treat sampled trajectories as independent chains, assigning uniform credit to all steps in each chain and ignoring the existence of critical steps that may disproportionally impact reasoning outcome. In this paper, we propose T-STAR(Tree-structured Self-Taught Agent Rectification), a framework that recovers the latent correlated reward structure across seemingly independent trajectories. Specifically, we consolidate trajectories into a unified Cognitive Tree by identifying and merging functionally similar steps/nodes. It enables an Introspective Valuation mechanism that back-propagates trajectory-level rewards through the tree to obtain a new notion of variance-reduced relative advantage at step-level. Using the Cognitive Tree, we also develop In-Context Thought Grafting to synthesize corrective reasoning by contrasting successful and failed branches at critical divergence points/steps. Our proposed Surgical Policy Optimization then capitalizes on the rich policy gradient information concentrated at these critical points/steps through a Bradley-Terry type of surgical loss. Extensive experiments across embodied, interactive, reasoning, and planning benchmarks demonstrate that T-STAR achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines, with gains most pronounced on tasks requiring extended reasoning chains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces T-STAR, a framework that builds a tree over multiple sampled reasoning trajectories to assign fine-grained credit to critical steps and perform targeted policy optimization for LLM agents.
AI.motivation: Multi-step reasoning tasks for LLM-based agents suffer from sparse, trajectory-level rewards. Existing RL methods like Group Relative Policy Optimization treat each trajectory as an independent chain and distribute reward uniformly across all steps, failing to recognize and emphasize critical decision points that disproportionately determine success or failure. This leads to inefficient learning and weak credit assignment in long reasoning chains.
AI.method: T-STAR first consolidates multiple sampled trajectories into a unified "Cognitive Tree" by detecting and merging functionally similar steps, exposing shared prefixes and divergence points. On this tree, it performs Introspective Valuation, which back-propagates trajectory-level rewards through the structure to compute a variance-reduced, step-level relative advantage signal. It then performs In-Context Thought Grafting, which contrasts successful and failed branches around critical divergence nodes to synthesize improved reasoning traces. Finally, Surgical Policy Optimization applies a Bradley–Terry-style loss focused on these critical points/steps to update the policy using the concentrated, high-signal gradient information.
AI.result: Across a range of benchmarks—embodied environments, interactive tasks, reasoning, and planning problems—T-STAR consistently outperforms strong RL baselines for LLM agents. The benefits are especially large on tasks that require long, multi-step reasoning chains, indicating more effective credit assignment and learning from sparse rewards.
AI.conclusion: By recovering latent reward correlations across trajectories and focusing learning on critical reasoning steps, T-STAR provides a more sample-efficient and effective RL framework for LLM agents. Its Cognitive Tree, introspective valuation, thought grafting, and surgical optimization together yield better performance than prior methods, particularly on complex tasks demanding extended reasoning sequences.

title: Application-Driven Pedagogical Knowledge Optimization of Open-Source LLMs via Reinforcement Learning and Supervised Fine-Tuning
comment: * These authors contributed equally to this work and share first authorship
summary: We present an innovative multi-stage optimization strategy combining reinforcement learning (RL) and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to enhance the pedagogical knowledge of large language models (LLMs), as illustrated by EduQwen 32B-RL1, EduQwen 32B-SFT, and an optional third-stage model EduQwen 32B-SFT-RL2: (1) RL optimization that implements progressive difficulty training, focuses on challenging examples, and employs extended reasoning rollouts; (2) a subsequent SFT phase that leverages the RL-trained model to synthesize high-quality training data with difficulty-weighted sampling; and (3) an optional second round of RL optimization. EduQwen 32B-RL1, EduQwen 32B-SFT, and EduQwen 32B-SFT-RL2 are an application-driven family of open-source pedagogical LLMs built on a dense Qwen3-32B backbone. These models remarkably achieve high enough accuracy on the Cross-Domain Pedagogical Knowledge (CDPK) Benchmark to establish new state-of-the-art (SOTA) results across the interactive Pedagogy Benchmark Leaderboard and surpass significantly larger proprietary systems such as the previous benchmark leader Gemini-3 Pro. These dense 32-billion-parameter models demonstrate that domain-specialized optimization can transform mid-sized open-source LLMs into true pedagogical domain experts that outperform much larger general-purpose systems, while preserving the transparency, customizability, and cost-efficiency required for responsible educational AI deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EduQwen, a family of 32B-parameter pedagogical LLMs trained with a three-stage pipeline (RL → SFT → optional RL) that achieves SOTA performance on a cross-domain teaching benchmark, outperforming much larger proprietary models like Gemini-3 Pro.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs, even very large ones, often lack deep, reliable pedagogical knowledge and teaching skills required for educational applications. There is a need for transparent, cost-efficient, open-source models that can act as expert tutors across domains while being tailored and governed responsibly. The authors aim to show that with the right training strategy, a mid-sized open-source model can be turned into a pedagogical expert that rivals or surpasses larger closed models.
AI.method: They start from a dense Qwen3-32B backbone and apply a multi-stage optimization strategy: (1) First-stage reinforcement learning (RL1) uses progressive difficulty training, focusing on challenging examples and extending reasoning rollouts to strengthen teaching and reasoning abilities. (2) A supervised fine-tuning (SFT) stage uses the RL1 model to synthesize high-quality pedagogical training data and applies difficulty-weighted sampling to emphasize harder cases. (3) An optional second RL stage (RL2) further refines performance. This yields three models: EduQwen 32B-RL1, EduQwen 32B-SFT, and EduQwen 32B-SFT-RL2.
AI.result: All EduQwen variants achieve very high scores on the Cross-Domain Pedagogical Knowledge (CDPK) Benchmark, setting new SOTA on the interactive Pedagogy Benchmark Leaderboard. They outperform significantly larger proprietary systems, including Gemini-3 Pro, in pedagogical tasks, indicating strong teaching, reasoning, and domain-transfer abilities within a 32B-parameter budget.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed RL+SFT multi-stage pipeline can turn a mid-sized, dense, open-source LLM into a domain-specialized pedagogical expert that surpasses much larger proprietary general-purpose systems. This demonstrates that domain specialization and thoughtful training strategy, rather than sheer scale alone, can deliver top-tier educational performance while preserving transparency, customizability, and cost-efficiency important for responsible deployment in education.

title: How Much LLM Does a Self-Revising Agent Actually Need?
summary:
Recent LLM-based agents often place world modeling, planning, and reflection inside a single language model loop. This can produce capable behavior, but it makes a basic scientific question difficult to answer: which part of the agent's competence actually comes from the LLM, and which part comes from explicit structure around it?
  We study this question not by claiming a general answer, but by making it empirically tractable. We introduce a declared reflective runtime protocol that externalizes agent state, confidence signals, guarded actions, and hypothetical transitions into inspectable runtime structure. We instantiate this protocol in a declarative runtime and evaluate it on noisy Collaborative Battleship [4] using four progressively structured agents over 54 games (18 boards $\times$ 3 seeds).
  The resulting decomposition isolates four components: posterior belief tracking, explicit world-model planning, symbolic in-episode reflection, and sparse LLM-based revision. Across this decomposition, explicit world-model planning improves substantially over a greedy posterior-following baseline (+24.1pp win rate, +0.017 F1). Symbolic reflection operates as a real runtime mechanism -- with prediction tracking, confidence gating, and guarded revision actions -- even though its current revision presets are not yet net-positive in aggregate. Adding conditional LLM revision at about 4.3\% of turns yields only a small and non-monotonic change: average F1 rises slightly (+0.005) while win rate drops (31$\rightarrow$29 out of 54).
  These results suggest a methodological contribution rather than a leaderboard claim: externalizing reflection turns otherwise latent agent behavior into inspectable runtime structure, allowing the marginal role of LLM intervention to be studied directly.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a protocol that externalizes an LLM agent’s internal reasoning and state, then empirically decomposes and measures how much performance comes from explicit structure versus the LLM itself in a game environment.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents often bundle world modeling, planning, and reflection inside a single opaque LLM loop, making it hard to scientifically identify which competencies come from the model versus the surrounding agent architecture. The authors want a way to make this distinction empirically measurable instead of anecdotal, and to turn hidden internal processes into observable structure for analysis.
AI.method: They design a declared reflective runtime protocol that makes agent state, confidence scores, guarded actions, and hypothetical transitions explicit and inspectable at runtime. They implement this in a declarative runtime and test four progressively more structured agents on a noisy Collaborative Battleship task over 54 games. The agents are designed so that components—posterior belief tracking, explicit world-model planning, symbolic reflection, and sparse LLM-based revision—can be separately enabled and evaluated.
AI.result: Decomposing the agent shows that explicit world-model planning significantly outperforms a greedy posterior-following baseline, improving win rate by 24.1 percentage points and F1 by 0.017. Symbolic reflection indeed functions as a concrete runtime mechanism (with prediction tracking, confidence gating, and guarded revisions), but its current revision strategies are not yet beneficial overall. Adding conditional LLM-based revision on roughly 4.3% of turns makes only a small, non-monotonic difference: F1 increases slightly by 0.005 but overall win rate decreases from 31 to 29 wins out of 54.
AI.conclusion: The main contribution is methodological rather than performance-focused: by externalizing reflection into a structured runtime protocol, the authors make agent internals observable and thereby enable direct study of the marginal contribution of the LLM versus explicit structural components like planning and reflection. This provides a framework for rigorous empirical analysis of LLM-agent architectures rather than merely reporting aggregate scores.

title: ART: Attention Replacement Technique to Improve Factuality in LLMs
summary: Hallucination in large language models (LLMs) continues to be a significant issue, particularly in tasks like question answering, where models often generate plausible yet incorrect or irrelevant information. Although various methods have been proposed to mitigate hallucinations, the relationship between attention patterns and hallucinations has not been fully explored. In this paper, we analyze the distribution of attention scores across each layer and attention head of LLMs, revealing a common and intriguing phenomenon: shallow layers of LLMs primarily rely on uniform attention patterns, where the model distributes its attention evenly across the entire sequence. This uniform attention pattern can lead to hallucinations, as the model fails to focus on the most relevant information. To mitigate this issue, we propose a training-free method called Attention Replacement Technique (ART), which replaces these uniform attention patterns in the shallow layers with local attention patterns. This change directs the model to focus more on the relevant contexts, thus reducing hallucinations. Through extensive experiments, ART demonstrates significant reductions in hallucinations across multiple LLM architectures, proving its effectiveness and generalizability without requiring fine-tuning or additional training data.

AI.tldr: The paper links uniform attention patterns in shallow layers of LLMs to hallucinations and introduces a training-free Attention Replacement Technique (ART) that swaps uniform with local attention to reduce hallucinations across models.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in LLMs remain a core reliability problem, especially for question answering, and existing mitigation methods do not sufficiently explain or exploit how internal attention patterns relate to hallucination behavior. The authors aim to understand this connection and leverage it to design a simple, general, and training-free fix.
AI.method: They empirically analyze attention-score distributions across layers and heads of various LLMs, discovering that shallow layers often show nearly uniform attention over the sequence. They hypothesize that such uniform attention leads to poor focus on relevant context and hence hallucinations. To address this, they introduce the Attention Replacement Technique (ART), which, during inference, detects uniform attention patterns in shallow layers and replaces them with more local attention patterns that emphasize nearby or contextually relevant tokens, without any fine-tuning or extra data.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple LLM architectures and hallucination-sensitive tasks (e.g., question answering) show that applying ART at inference significantly reduces hallucination rates. The method works consistently across models, indicating robustness and model-agnostic applicability, and achieves these gains without additional training cost.
AI.conclusion: Uniform attention in shallow layers is a common property of LLMs and is linked to hallucinations because it prevents the model from concentrating on key context tokens. By replacing these uniform patterns with local attention through the training-free ART method, hallucinations can be substantially reduced across architectures, offering a practical, general mechanism to improve LLM reliability without retraining.

title: Knowledge Graphs Generation from Cultural Heritage Texts: Combining LLMs and Ontological Engineering for Scholarly Debates
summary: Cultural Heritage texts contain rich knowledge that is difficult to query systematically due to the challenges of converting unstructured discourse into structured Knowledge Graphs (KGs). This paper introduces ATR4CH (Adaptive Text-to-RDF for Cultural Heritage), a systematic five-step methodology for Large Language Model-based Knowledge Extraction from Cultural Heritage documents. We validate the methodology through a case study on authenticity assessment debates. Methodology - ATR4CH combines annotation models, ontological frameworks, and LLM-based extraction through iterative development: foundational analysis, annotation schema development, pipeline architecture, integration refinement, and comprehensive evaluation. We demonstrate the approach using Wikipedia articles about disputed items (documents, artifacts...), implementing a sequential pipeline with three LLMs (Claude Sonnet 3.7, Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-4o-mini). Findings - The methodology successfully extracts complex Cultural Heritage knowledge: 0.96-0.99 F1 for metadata extraction, 0.7-0.8 F1 for entity recognition, 0.65-0.75 F1 for hypothesis extraction, 0.95-0.97 for evidence extraction, and 0.62 G-EVAL for discourse representation. Smaller models performed competitively, enabling cost-effective deployment. Originality - This is the first systematic methodology for coordinating LLM-based extraction with Cultural Heritage ontologies. ATR4CH provides a replicable framework adaptable across CH domains and institutional resources. Research Limitations - The produced KG is limited to Wikipedia articles. While the results are encouraging, human oversight is necessary during post-processing. Practical Implications - ATR4CH enables Cultural Heritage institutions to systematically convert textual knowledge into queryable KGs, supporting automated metadata enrichment and knowledge discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ATR4CH, a five-step, LLM-based methodology to convert unstructured Cultural Heritage texts into structured RDF knowledge graphs, validated on Wikipedia articles about disputed cultural items.
AI.motivation: Cultural Heritage texts contain dense, nuanced knowledge that is hard to query because it is embedded in unstructured narrative form. Existing approaches struggle to systematically transform this discourse into structured Knowledge Graphs aligned with Cultural Heritage ontologies. There is a need for a repeatable, ontology-aware methodology that leverages recent advances in Large Language Models to extract entities, hypotheses, evidence, and discourse structures from CH documents in a way that supports querying, metadata enrichment, and knowledge discovery.
AI.method: The authors propose ATR4CH, a five-step adaptive Text-to-RDF methodology: (1) foundational analysis of the Cultural Heritage domain and authenticity debates; (2) development of an annotation schema aligned with Cultural Heritage ontologies; (3) design of a pipeline architecture that orchestrates multiple LLMs and annotation models; (4) iterative integration refinement to improve interaction between ontologies, schemas, and LLM outputs; and (5) comprehensive evaluation. They implement a sequential pipeline using three LLMs (Claude Sonnet 3.7, Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-4o-mini) to process Wikipedia articles on disputed artifacts and documents, extracting metadata, entities, hypotheses, evidence, and discourse representations, and then mapping these into RDF KGs.
AI.result: ATR4CH achieves high performance across several extraction tasks: F1 scores of 0.96–0.99 for metadata extraction, 0.70–0.80 for entity recognition, 0.65–0.75 for hypothesis extraction, 0.95–0.97 for evidence extraction, and a G-EVAL score of 0.62 for discourse representation. Smaller LLMs perform competitively with larger ones, suggesting that the approach can be deployed cost-effectively. The methodology yields structured Knowledge Graphs from Wikipedia-based Cultural Heritage debates about authenticity, demonstrating its feasibility and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: ATR4CH is presented as the first systematic methodology that explicitly coordinates LLM-based information extraction with Cultural Heritage ontologies to produce RDF Knowledge Graphs. The framework is replicable and adaptable across different Cultural Heritage domains and institutional resource settings. However, the current validation is limited to Wikipedia articles, and human oversight remains necessary in post-processing. Nonetheless, ATR4CH offers practical benefits for Cultural Heritage institutions by enabling automated, ontology-aware transformation of narrative texts into queryable KGs that support metadata enrichment and knowledge discovery.

title: FMI@SU ToxHabits: Evaluating LLMs Performance on Toxic Habit Extraction in Spanish Clinical Texts
comment: 8 pages, 1 figure, 6 tables, Challenge and Workshop BC9 Large Language Models for Clinical and Biomedical NLP, International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence IJCAI 2025
summary: The paper presents an approach for the recognition of toxic habits named entities in Spanish clinical texts. The approach was developed for the ToxHabits Shared Task. Our team participated in subtask 1, which aims to detect substance use and abuse mentions in clinical case reports and classify them in four categories (Tobacco, Alcohol, Cannabis, and Drug). We explored various methods of utilizing LLMs for the task, including zero-shot, few-shot, and prompt optimization, and found that GPT-4.1's few-shot prompting performed the best in our experiments. Our method achieved an F1 score of 0.65 on the test set, demonstrating a promising result for recognizing named entities in languages other than English.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates different ways of using large language models (LLMs) to recognize toxic habit entities (tobacco, alcohol, cannabis, drugs) in Spanish clinical texts, finding that GPT-4.1 few-shot prompting works best, achieving F1=0.65.
AI.motivation: Most clinical NLP resources and systems focus on English, and recognizing mentions of toxic habits (substance use/abuse) in other languages like Spanish is underexplored yet important for patient care and research. The ToxHabits Shared Task provides a benchmark to advance methods for detecting substance-related entities in Spanish clinical case reports.
AI.method: The authors participated in the ToxHabits Shared Task subtask 1, which requires detecting and classifying mentions of substance use/abuse into four types: Tobacco, Alcohol, Cannabis, and Drug. They experimented with multiple ways of leveraging large language models for named entity recognition, specifically using zero-shot prompting, few-shot prompting, and prompt optimization strategies with GPT-4.1, and compared the performance of these configurations.
AI.result: Among the tested configurations, GPT-4.1 with a few-shot prompting strategy delivered the best performance, achieving an F1 score of 0.65 on the shared task test set for toxic habit entity recognition in Spanish clinical texts.
AI.conclusion: Few-shot prompting of GPT-4.1 is an effective approach for recognizing toxic habit-related named entities in Spanish clinical texts, yielding promising performance and suggesting that LLM-based methods can generalize well to clinical NER tasks in languages other than English.

title: Attention Flows: Tracing LLM Conceptual Engagement via Story Summaries
summary: Although LLM context lengths have grown, there is evidence that their ability to integrate information across long-form texts has not kept pace. We evaluate one such understanding task: generating summaries of novels. When human authors of summaries compress a story, they reveal what they consider narratively important. Therefore, by comparing human and LLM-authored summaries, we can assess whether models mirror human patterns of conceptual engagement with texts. To measure conceptual engagement, we align sentences from 150 human-written novel summaries with the specific chapters they reference. We demonstrate the difficulty of this alignment task, which indicates the complexity of summarization as a task. We then generate and align additional summaries by nine state-of-the-art LLMs for each of the 150 reference texts. Comparing the human and model-authored summaries, we find both stylistic differences between the texts and differences in how humans and LLMs distribute their focus throughout a narrative, with models emphasizing the ends of texts. Comparing human narrative engagement with model attention mechanisms suggests explanations for degraded narrative comprehension and targets for future development. We release our dataset to support future research.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well large language models (LLMs) understand and summarize long narratives (novels) by comparing their summaries to human-written ones.
AI.motivation: Context windows in LLMs are growing, but their true ability to integrate and reason over long texts may lag behind. Summarizing novels is a demanding test of long-range comprehension and narrative understanding. Human summaries implicitly encode what people see as important in a story, so comparing them with LLM summaries can reveal gaps in models’ narrative comprehension and conceptual engagement.
AI.method: The authors collect 150 human-written summaries of novels and align each summary sentence with the specific novel chapter(s) it refers to, showing that this alignment itself is difficult and reflects the complexity of the summarization task. They then prompt nine state-of-the-art LLMs to generate summaries of the same 150 novels, align model summary sentences to chapters in the same way, and compare human vs. model summaries in terms of style and how attention/focus is distributed across different parts of the narrative. They also relate these behavioral patterns to models’ internal attention mechanisms.
AI.result: The study finds noticeable stylistic differences between human and LLM summaries and, more importantly, systematic differences in how they allocate focus across the narrative: LLMs tend to overemphasize the ends of novels compared with humans. The alignment task is shown to be non-trivial, underscoring the complexity of long-form summarization. Analysis of attention patterns in the models points to potential reasons for the degradation in narrative comprehension over long contexts.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs, even with long context windows, do not match human-like conceptual engagement with long narratives, especially in how they prioritize different parts of a story. Their summaries disproportionately focus on later sections, which may contribute to weaker overall narrative understanding. The authors provide an aligned dataset of human and model summaries as a public resource and highlight directions for improving long-context narrative comprehension in future LLM development.

title: State-of-the-Art Arabic Language Modeling with Sparse MoE Fine-Tuning and Chain-of-Thought Distillation
summary: This paper introduces Arabic-DeepSeek-R1, an application-driven open-source Arabic LLM that leverages a sparse MoE backbone to address the digital equity gap for under-represented languages, and establishes a new SOTA across the entire Open Arabic LLM Leaderboard (OALL). Our four-phase CoT distillation scheme integrates Arabic-specific linguistic verification and regional ethical norms into a 372M-token, contamination-controlled 80/20 Arabic-English training mixture. Arabic-DeepSeek-R1 achieves the highest average score across the seven-benchmark OALL suite while establishing SOTA or near-SOTA, including dominant results on grammar-focused MadinahQA (surpassing both GPT-5.1 and the OALL leader by substantial margins), safety-oriented AraTrust, multi-ability AlGhafa, and retrieval-augmented ALRAGE. Our results indicate that the combination of sparse MoE architecture, culturally-informed CoT distillation with explicit Arabic linguistic checks, and strategic bilingual data curation enables an open-source adapted model to systematically outperform the proprietary frontier system GPT-5.1 on the majority of benchmarks evaluating comprehensive language-specific tasks: the first such demonstration for Arabic LLMs. These findings indicate that much of Arabic's performance deficit in current LLM ecosystems stems from under-specialization rather than architectural limitations, and that parameter-efficient adaptation of open reasoning models can yield breakthrough SOTA performance without industrial-scale pretraining costs. Arabic-DeepSeek-R1 establishes a validated and replicable framework for sovereign and domain-specific language technologies, demonstrating that strategic, culturally-grounded adaptation of sparse MoE backbones offers a viable and cost-effective pathway to achieving record-breaking performance across standardized benchmarks for low-resource languages.

AI.tldr: Arabic-DeepSeek-R1 is an open-source Arabic-focused LLM using a sparse MoE architecture and culturally-informed CoT distillation to reach SOTA on the Open Arabic LLM Leaderboard, even surpassing GPT-5.1 on most Arabic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: There is a digital equity gap for under-represented languages like Arabic because existing LLMs, including frontier proprietary models, underperform on language-specific and culturally grounded tasks. The authors aim to show that this deficit is due more to under-specialization than to fundamental architectural limits, and to provide a practical, replicable way for sovereign and domain-specific actors to build high-performing Arabic language technologies without industrial-scale pretraining.
AI.method: The authors build Arabic-DeepSeek-R1 on a sparse Mixture-of-Experts backbone and perform a four-phase chain-of-thought (CoT) distillation process that incorporates Arabic-specific linguistic verification and regional ethical norms. Training uses a contamination-controlled 372M-token corpus with an 80/20 Arabic-English mix. They evaluate the model on the seven-benchmark Open Arabic LLM Leaderboard (OALL), including grammar-focused MadinahQA, safety-oriented AraTrust, multi-ability AlGhafa, and retrieval-augmented ALRAGE, comparing against both open models and the proprietary GPT-5.1.
AI.result: Arabic-DeepSeek-R1 achieves the highest average score across the full OALL benchmark suite, setting SOTA or near-SOTA on most individual tasks. It delivers particularly strong gains on MadinahQA, AraTrust, AlGhafa, and ALRAGE, outperforming both the prior OALL leader and GPT-5.1 by substantial margins on key Arabic-focused benchmarks. The model thus systematically surpasses a frontier proprietary system on the majority of comprehensive Arabic language evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that Arabic LLM underperformance is primarily a consequence of under-specialization and data/cultural misalignment rather than limitations of current LLM architectures. It shows that parameter-efficient adaptation of open sparse MoE reasoning backbones, combined with culturally grounded CoT distillation and carefully curated bilingual data, can deliver breakthrough SOTA performance for a low-resource language like Arabic. Arabic-DeepSeek-R1 is proposed as a validated, reproducible framework for building sovereign and domain-specific language technologies in under-represented languages in a cost-effective way.

title: When to Call an Apple Red: Humans Follow Introspective Rules, VLMs Don't
summary: Understanding when Vision-Language Models (VLMs) will behave unexpectedly, whether models can reliably predict their own behavior, and if models adhere to their introspective reasoning are central challenges for trustworthy deployment. To study this, we introduce the Graded Color Attribution (GCA) dataset, a controlled benchmark designed to elicit decision rules and evaluate participant faithfulness to these rules. GCA consists of line drawings that vary pixel-level color coverage across three conditions: world-knowledge recolorings, counterfactual recolorings, and shapes with no color priors. Using GCA, both VLMs and human participants establish a threshold: the minimum percentage of pixels of a given color an object must have to receive that color label. We then compare these rules with their subsequent color attribution decisions. Our findings reveal that models systematically violate their own introspective rules. For example, GPT-5-mini violates its stated introspection rules in nearly 60\% of cases on objects with strong color priors. Human participants remain faithful to their stated rules, with any apparent violations being explained by a well-documented tendency to overestimate color coverage. In contrast, we find that VLMs are excellent estimators of color coverage, yet blatantly contradict their own reasoning in their final responses. Across all models and strategies for eliciting introspective rules, world-knowledge priors systematically degrade faithfulness in ways that do not mirror human cognition. Our findings challenge the view that VLM reasoning failures are difficulty-driven and suggest that VLM introspective self-knowledge is miscalibrated, with direct implications for high-stakes deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Graded Color Attribution (GCA) dataset to test whether vision-language models follow their own stated decision rules about color labeling, finding that models frequently violate their own introspective rules while humans largely do not.
AI.motivation: To enable trustworthy deployment of vision-language models, we need to know when they will behave unexpectedly and whether they can accurately predict and follow their own decision rules. Existing benchmarks do not tightly control for priors and introspective rule adherence, especially around simple perceptual tasks such as color attribution.
AI.method: The authors construct the GCA dataset of line drawings whose pixel-level color coverage is systematically varied under three conditions: recolorings based on real-world knowledge, counterfactual recolorings that oppose typical object colors, and shapes without strong color priors. They ask both VLMs and human participants to first state a threshold rule: the minimum percentage of pixels of a color required to label an object with that color. They then evaluate how faithfully each participant’s subsequent color-labeling decisions follow their stated rule, comparing errors and patterns across the different conditions.
AI.result: Vision-language models systematically violate their own previously stated color-threshold rules, especially for objects with strong real-world color priors. For instance, GPT-5-mini contradicts its own introspection in almost 60% of such cases. Humans largely follow their rules; apparent rule violations can be explained by a known bias to overestimate color coverage. Interestingly, VLMs themselves are accurate at estimating color coverage but nonetheless produce final color labels that conflict with their own reasoning, and this lack of faithfulness is consistently worsened by world-knowledge priors across all tested models and elicitation strategies.
AI.conclusion: VLM reasoning failures in this setting cannot be fully attributed to task difficulty: even on simple perceptual tasks where they estimate color accurately, models often contradict their own introspective rules. This indicates that VLM introspective self-knowledge is miscalibrated and that world-knowledge priors can systematically degrade rule faithfulness in ways unlike human cognition. These findings raise concerns about relying on model self-reports and introspective explanations in high-stakes deployments, calling for methods that better align model behavior with their stated decision rules.

title: Team Fusion@ SU@ BC8 SympTEMIST track: transformer-based approach for symptom recognition and linking
comment: 6 pages, 3 tables, Proceedings of the BioCreative VIII Challenge and Workshop: Curation and Evaluation in the era of Generative Models, American Medical Informatics Association 2023 Annual Symposium
summary: This paper presents a transformer-based approach to solving the SympTEMIST named entity recognition (NER) and entity linking (EL) tasks. For NER, we fine-tune a RoBERTa-based (1) token-level classifier with BiLSTM and CRF layers on an augmented train set. Entity linking is performed by generating candidates using the cross-lingual SapBERT XLMR-Large (2), and calculating cosine similarity against a knowledge base. The choice of knowledge base proves to have the highest impact on model accuracy.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based system for symptom NER and entity linking, using RoBERTa+BiLSTM+CRF for NER and SapBERT-based retrieval for EL; knowledge-base choice is crucial.
AI.motivation: Improve performance on the SympTEMIST shared-task by leveraging modern transformer architectures for both named entity recognition and entity linking of symptom mentions, and to study how knowledge-base selection affects EL accuracy.
AI.method: Fine-tune a RoBERTa-based token-level classifier enhanced with BiLSTM and CRF layers on an augmented training set for NER. For EL, use cross-lingual SapBERT (XLMR-Large) to generate entity candidates and compute cosine similarity between mention and candidate embeddings against a symptom knowledge base, comparing different KB choices.
AI.result: The system successfully performs NER and entity linking for the SympTEMIST tasks, and experiments show that the choice of knowledge base has the largest impact on entity linking accuracy, more so than model architecture tweaks.
AI.conclusion: Transformer-based models combined with BiLSTM+CRF are effective for symptom NER, and SapBERT-based embedding similarity works well for EL; however, optimizing and carefully selecting the underlying knowledge base is critical for maximizing overall performance.

title: Learning to Interrupt in Language-based Multi-agent Communication
summary: Multi-agent systems using large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities across various domains. However, current agent communication suffers from verbose output that overload context and increase computational costs. Although existing approaches focus on compressing the message from the speaker side, they struggle to adapt to different listeners and identify relevant information. An effective way in human communication is to allow the listener to interrupt and express their opinion or ask for clarification. Motivated by this, we propose an interruptible communication framework that allows the agent who is listening to interrupt the current speaker. Through prompting experiments, we find that current LLMs are often overconfident and interrupt before receiving enough information. Therefore, we propose a learning method that predicts the appropriate interruption points based on the estimated future reward and cost. We evaluate our framework across various multi-agent scenarios, including 2-agent text pictionary games, 3-agent meeting scheduling, and 3-agent debate. The results of the experiment show that our HANDRAISER can reduce the communication cost by 32.2% compared to the baseline with comparable or superior task performance. This learned interruption behavior can also be generalized to different agents and tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HANDRAISER, an interruptible communication framework for multi-agent systems using LLMs that lets listening agents learn when to interrupt speakers, cutting communication cost while preserving task performance.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent communication is verbose, causing context overload and high computation cost. Prior work mainly compresses messages on the speaker side and struggles to adapt to different listeners or filter relevant information. Human conversation efficiency is often improved by listener-initiated interruptions for clarification or guidance, suggesting a similar mechanism could benefit LLM agents.
AI.method: The authors design an interruptible communication framework where the listening agent can interrupt the current speaker. They conduct prompting experiments to study default LLM interruption behavior and find that models are overconfident, interrupting too early. To address this, they introduce a learning method that predicts appropriate interruption points by estimating future reward (task performance) versus communication cost. This policy for interruption is trained and then applied across multiple multi-agent tasks.
AI.result: In experiments on several multi-agent scenarios—2-agent text pictionary, 3-agent meeting scheduling, and 3-agent debate—the proposed HANDRAISER framework reduces communication cost by 32.2% compared to a baseline without learned interruption, while achieving comparable or better task performance. The learned interruption policy generalizes to different agents and tasks beyond those it was trained on.
AI.conclusion: Allowing listeners to learn when to interrupt speakers in LLM-based multi-agent systems can significantly reduce communication overhead without harming (and sometimes improving) task performance. HANDRAISER demonstrates that interruption behavior, learned via reward–cost trade-offs, is both effective and transferable across scenarios, offering a more adaptive alternative to purely speaker-side compression approaches.

title: Context-Aware Dialectal Arabic Machine Translation with Interactive Region and Register Selection
comment: 14 pages, 5 figures, 5 tables. Preprint under review
summary:
Current Machine Translation (MT) systems for Arabic often struggle to account for dialectal diversity, frequently homogenizing dialectal inputs into Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) and offering limited user control over the target vernacular. In this work, we propose a context-aware and steerable framework for dialectal Arabic MT that explicitly models regional and sociolinguistic variation. Our primary technical contribution is a Rule-Based Data Augmentation (RBDA) pipeline that expands a 3,000-sentence seed corpus into a balanced 57,000-sentence parallel dataset, covering eight regional varieties eg., Egyptian, Levantine, Gulf, etc. By fine-tuning an mT5-base model conditioned on lightweight metadata tags, our approach enables controllable generation across dialects and social registers in the translation output.
  Through a combination of automatic evaluation and qualitative analysis, we observe an apparent accuracy-fidelity trade-off: high-resource baselines such as NLLB (No Language Left Behind) achieve higher aggregate BLEU scores (13.75) by defaulting toward the MSA mean, while exhibiting limited dialectal specificity. In contrast, our model achieves lower BLEU scores (8.19) but produces outputs that align more closely with the intended regional varieties. Supporting qualitative evaluation, including an LLM-assisted cultural authenticity analysis, suggests improved dialectal alignment compared to baseline systems (4.80/5 vs. 1.0/5). These findings highlight the limitations of standard MT metrics for dialect-sensitive tasks and motivate the need for evaluation practices that better reflect linguistic diversity in Arabic MT.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a controllable machine translation framework for Arabic dialects that uses rule-based data augmentation and metadata-conditioned mT5 fine-tuning to produce dialect-specific outputs, revealing that standard BLEU metrics undervalue dialectal fidelity.
AI.motivation: Arabic has rich dialectal variation, but most MT systems collapse dialectal input into Modern Standard Arabic and provide little control over target dialect or social register. This leads to translations that ignore regional and sociolinguistic nuances. The authors are motivated to create an MT system that explicitly handles dialect diversity and allows users to steer translations toward specific varieties while questioning whether common MT metrics properly capture performance on dialect-sensitive tasks.
AI.method: They design a context-aware, steerable MT framework centered on a Rule-Based Data Augmentation (RBDA) pipeline. Starting from a 3,000-sentence seed corpus, they algorithmically generate a larger, balanced 57,000-sentence parallel dataset spanning eight Arabic dialects (e.g., Egyptian, Levantine, Gulf). They then fine-tune an mT5-base model using lightweight metadata tags that encode dialect and social register, enabling controllable generation of dialect-specific translations. Evaluation includes automatic metrics (BLEU) and qualitative analyses, including LLM-assisted cultural authenticity assessment.
AI.result: The RBDA pipeline successfully scales the seed corpus to a substantially larger, dialect-balanced dataset. When fine-tuned on this data with dialect metadata, the resulting model can generate outputs aligned with specified dialects and registers. Compared to a strong baseline like NLLB, their model has lower BLEU (8.19 vs. 13.75) but higher dialectal specificity and better alignment with intended regional varieties. Cultural authenticity evaluation via LLM and other qualitative methods yields substantially higher scores for their model (4.80/5) than for the baseline (1.0/5).
AI.conclusion: The work shows that prioritizing dialectal fidelity in Arabic MT can conflict with maximizing traditional automatic metrics such as BLEU, because strong baselines often default to MSA and thereby score higher numerically but ignore dialectal nuance. Their approach demonstrates that controllable, dialect-aware MT is feasible using rule-based augmentation and metadata-conditioned fine-tuning. The findings argue that standard MT evaluation practices are inadequate for dialect-sensitive scenarios and underscore the need for metrics and methodologies that better capture linguistic and sociocultural diversity in Arabic translation.

title: Multi-objective Evolutionary Merging Enables Efficient Reasoning Models
summary: Reasoning models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in solving complex problems by leveraging long chains of thought. However, this more deliberate reasoning comes with substantial computational overhead at inference time. The Long-to-Short (L2S) reasoning problem seeks to maintain high accuracy using fewer tokens, but current training-free model merging approaches rely on scalarized, fixed-hyperparameter arithmetic methods that are highly brittle and force suboptimal compromises. To address this gap, we introduce Evo-L2S, a novel framework that formulates L2S reasoning as a multi-objective optimization challenge. By leveraging evolutionary model merging, Evo-L2S explicitly optimizes the trade-off between accuracy and output length to produce a robust Pareto front of merged models. To make this search computationally tractable for large language models, we propose an entropy-based subset sampling technique that drastically reduces the overhead of fitness estimation. Comprehensive experiments across 1.5B, 7B, and 14B parameter scales on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that Evo-L2S can reduce the length of generated reasoning traces by over 50% while preserving, or even improving, the problem-solving accuracy of the original reasoning models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Evo-L2S, an evolutionary model-merging framework that shortens chains-of-thought in reasoning models while keeping or improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reasoning language models achieve strong performance via long chains-of-thought, but this causes high computational cost at inference. Existing long-to-short (L2S) approaches that merge models without training rely on simple arithmetic with fixed hyperparameters, which is brittle and cannot flexibly balance accuracy against token length. A more robust and principled way is needed to optimize both accuracy and reasoning length simultaneously.
AI.method: The authors cast L2S reasoning as a multi-objective optimization problem over model merges, jointly optimizing for accuracy and short output length. They design Evo-L2S, an evolutionary search framework that explores different ways of merging models and maintains a Pareto front of candidate merges. To make this feasible for large models, they introduce an entropy-based subset sampling strategy that selects a small but informative subset of data for estimating each candidate’s fitness, reducing the computational cost of the search.
AI.result: On six mathematical reasoning benchmarks and models of 1.5B, 7B, and 14B parameters, Evo-L2S cuts the length of generated reasoning traces by more than 50% while maintaining or even improving the original models’ problem-solving accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Evo-L2S shows that evolutionary, multi-objective model merging can effectively shorten reasoning chains without sacrificing accuracy. By optimizing directly on accuracy–length trade-offs and using entropy-based sampling to keep search costs manageable, the framework provides a robust solution to the long-to-short reasoning problem for large language models.

title: DataSTORM: Deep Research on Large-Scale Databases using Exploratory Data Analysis and Data Storytelling
summary:
Deep research with Large Language Model (LLM) agents is emerging as a powerful paradigm for multi-step information discovery, synthesis, and analysis. However, existing approaches primarily focus on unstructured web data, while the challenges of conducting deep research over large-scale structured databases remain relatively underexplored. Unlike web-based research, effective data-centric research requires more than retrieval and summarization and demands iterative hypothesis generation, quantitative reasoning over structured schemas, and convergence toward a coherent analytical narrative.
  In this paper, we present DataSTORM, an LLM-based agentic system capable of autonomously conducting research across both large-scale structured databases and internet sources. Grounded in principles from Exploratory Data Analysis and Data Storytelling, DataSTORM reframes deep research over structured data as a thesis-driven analytical process: discovering candidate theses from data, validating them through iterative cross-source investigation, and developing them into coherent analytical narratives. We evaluate DataSTORM on InsightBench, where it achieves a new state-of-the-art result with a 19.4% relative improvement in insight-level recall and 7.2% in summary-level score. We further introduce a new dataset built on ACLED, a real-world complex database, and demonstrate that DataSTORM outperforms proprietary systems such as ChatGPT Deep Research across both automated metrics and human evaluations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DataSTORM, an LLM-agent system for deep research that jointly analyzes large structured databases and web sources, achieving state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Most existing LLM-based deep research systems work on unstructured web data and focus on retrieval plus summarization. They are not well-suited for large structured databases, where effective analysis requires hypothesis formulation, quantitative reasoning over schemas, and building coherent analytical narratives. The authors aim to bridge this gap by enabling LLM agents to do thesis-driven, data-centric research over structured data combined with web information.
AI.method: The authors design DataSTORM, an LLM-based agentic framework grounded in Exploratory Data Analysis and Data Storytelling. The system reframes deep research on structured data as a thesis-driven process: (1) discover candidate theses from structured databases and external sources, (2) iteratively validate and refine these theses via cross-source investigation and quantitative reasoning over database schemas, and (3) develop validated theses into coherent analytical narratives. DataSTORM operates over both large-scale structured databases and internet sources, orchestrating multi-step research workflows via LLM agents.
AI.result: On the InsightBench benchmark, DataSTORM sets a new state-of-the-art, improving insight-level recall by 19.4% and summary-level scores by 7.2% compared to prior methods. On a newly introduced dataset based on the real-world ACLED database, DataSTORM outperforms strong baselines including proprietary systems such as ChatGPT Deep Research, according to both automatic metrics and human evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Thesis-driven LLM agent frameworks like DataSTORM can effectively extend deep research from unstructured web data to large structured databases. By integrating exploratory data analysis, hypothesis generation, quantitative reasoning, and narrative construction across databases and web sources, DataSTORM achieves superior insight discovery and summarization quality, indicating a promising direction for data-centric LLM research systems.

title: ValueGround: Evaluating Culture-Conditioned Visual Value Grounding in MLLMs
summary: Cultural values are expressed not only through language but also through visual scenes and everyday social practices. Yet existing evaluations of cultural values in language models are almost entirely text-only, making it unclear whether models can ground culture-conditioned judgments when response options are visualized. We introduce ValueGround, a benchmark for evaluating culture-conditioned visual value grounding in multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Built from World Values Survey (WVS) questions, ValueGround uses minimally contrastive image pairs to represent opposing response options while controlling irrelevant variation. Given a country, a question, and an image pair, a model must choose the image that best matches the country's value tendency without access to the original response-option texts. Across six MLLMs and 13 countries, average accuracy drops from 72.8% in the text-only setting to 65.8% when options are visualized, despite 92.8% accuracy on option-image alignment. Stronger models are more robust, but all remain prone to prediction reversals. Our benchmark provides a controlled testbed for studying cross-modal transfer of culture-conditioned value judgments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ValueGround, a benchmark to test whether multimodal language models can correctly ground culture-related value judgments in images instead of text.
AI.motivation: Most prior evaluations of cultural values in language models rely only on textual prompts and answers, leaving open whether models can still make correct, culture-conditioned value judgments when those choices are presented visually. Since culture is expressed through visual scenes and social practices, there is a need to test if models can transfer their cultural knowledge across modalities.
AI.method: The authors construct ValueGround from World Values Survey (WVS) questions. For each question, they create minimally contrastive image pairs that visually represent opposing response options while holding other aspects constant. Models are given a country, a WVS-style question, and the associated image pair, and must pick the image matching that country’s typical value tendency, without seeing the original textual options. They evaluate six multimodal LLMs on 13 countries, also measuring how well options align with images.
AI.result: Models perform well in text-only settings (72.8% accuracy) but their performance drops when the response options are presented visually (65.8% accuracy), even though they achieve high accuracy (92.8%) at aligning textual options with their images. More capable models are somewhat more robust to the modality shift, but all evaluated models still exhibit frequent reversals in their predicted value judgments when moving from text to images.
AI.conclusion: Current multimodal LLMs struggle to consistently transfer culture-conditioned value knowledge from text to images, indicating incomplete visual grounding of cultural values. ValueGround offers a controlled benchmark to systematically study this cross-modal transfer and to guide the development of models with more robust, visually grounded cultural value understanding.

title: Closing the Speech-Text Gap with Limited Audio for Effective Domain Adaptation in LLM-Based ASR
summary: Conventional end-to-end automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems rely on paired speech-text data for domain adaptation. Recent LLM-based ASR architectures connect a speech encoder to a large language model via a projection module, enabling adaptation with text-only data. However, this introduces a modality gap, as the LLM is not exposed to the noisy representations produced by the speech projector. We investigate whether small amounts of speech can mitigate this mismatch. We compare three strategies: text-only adaptation, paired speech-text adaptation, and mixed batching (MB), which combines both. Experiments in in-domain and out-of-domain settings show that even limited speech consistently improves performance. Notably, MB using only 10% of the target-domain (less than 4 hours) speech achieves word error rates comparable to, or better than, conventional ASR fine-tuning with the full dataset, indicating that small amounts of speech provide a strong modality-alignment signal.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to best adapt LLM-based ASR systems to new domains when only small amounts of speech are available, showing that mixing a little paired speech with abundant text can match or beat standard full-data ASR fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: LLM-based ASR models can leverage abundant text-only data for domain adaptation by connecting a speech encoder to an LLM via a projection module, but this creates a modality mismatch: the LLM is adapted on clean text tokens, while at inference it receives noisy, projected speech features. This gap can limit performance, especially in new domains, and collecting large amounts of labeled speech is expensive. The authors want to know whether small quantities of speech can effectively align modalities and improve adaptation efficiency.
AI.method: They investigate three adaptation strategies for LLM-based ASR: (1) text-only adaptation, where only text data is used to adapt the LLM; (2) paired speech-text adaptation, where the system is fine-tuned using fully supervised speech-text pairs; and (3) mixed batching (MB), where each training batch combines text-only data with limited paired speech-text data to jointly adapt the model. They run experiments in both in-domain and out-of-domain scenarios, varying the amount of target-domain speech (down to about 10% / <4 hours) and comparing word error rates against a conventional end-to-end ASR fine-tuned with the full paired dataset.
AI.result: Across both in-domain and out-of-domain settings, incorporating even small amounts of speech data consistently improves ASR performance relative to text-only adaptation. The mixed batching strategy, using only about 10% of the target-domain speech (less than 4 hours), achieves word error rates that are comparable to or better than a conventional ASR model fine-tuned with 100% of the paired speech-text data. This shows that a small amount of speech carries disproportionately large benefits for aligning the speech encoder’s projected representations with the LLM.
AI.conclusion: The modality gap between projected speech features and the LLM’s text space significantly affects LLM-based ASR adaptation, but it can be effectively reduced with a small quantity of speech data. Mixed batching of limited speech-text pairs with abundant text-only data offers an efficient adaptation strategy, reaching or surpassing the performance of full-data conventional ASR fine-tuning. Small amounts of speech thus provide a strong modality-alignment signal, enabling more data-efficient domain adaptation of LLM-based ASR systems.

title: MedConclusion: A Benchmark for Biomedical Conclusion Generation from Structured Abstracts
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are widely explored for reasoning-intensive research tasks, yet resources for testing whether they can infer scientific conclusions from structured biomedical evidence remain limited. We introduce $\textbf{MedConclusion}$, a large-scale dataset of $\textbf{5.7M}$ PubMed structured abstracts for biomedical conclusion generation. Each instance pairs the non-conclusion sections of an abstract with the original author-written conclusion, providing naturally occurring supervision for evidence-to-conclusion reasoning. MedConclusion also includes journal-level metadata such as biomedical category and SJR, enabling subgroup analysis across biomedical domains. As an initial study, we evaluate diverse LLMs under conclusion and summary prompting settings and score outputs with both reference-based metrics and LLM-as-a-judge. We find that conclusion writing is behaviorally distinct from summary writing, strong models remain closely clustered under current automatic metrics, and judge identity can substantially shift absolute scores. MedConclusion provides a reusable data resource for studying scientific evidence-to-conclusion reasoning. Our code and data are available at: https://github.com/Harvard-AI-and-Robotics-Lab/MedConclusion.

AI.tldr: MedConclusion is a 5.7M-sample dataset of PubMed structured abstracts for training and evaluating LLMs on generating biomedical conclusions from evidence sections.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are increasingly used for research and reasoning, there is a lack of large, structured resources to test whether they can derive scientific conclusions specifically from biomedical evidence rather than perform generic summarization.
AI.method: The authors construct MedConclusion by collecting 5.7M structured PubMed abstracts and pairing each abstract’s non-conclusion sections (e.g., background, methods, results) with its author-written conclusion. They enrich each instance with journal-level metadata such as biomedical category and SJR and then benchmark multiple LLMs under conclusion-generation and summary-generation prompts, evaluating with reference-based automatic metrics and LLM-as-a-judge scoring.
AI.result: They observe that (1) conclusion generation behaves differently from generic summarization, (2) strong LLMs receive very similar scores under standard automatic metrics, suggesting limited discriminative power, and (3) evaluation outcomes are sensitive to the choice of LLM judge, which can significantly affect absolute scores.
AI.conclusion: MedConclusion offers a large, reusable benchmark to study how LLMs convert structured biomedical evidence into conclusions, highlighting key differences from summarization and exposing weaknesses in current evaluation metrics and judge-based assessment practices.

title: Fine-tuning Whisper for Pashto ASR: strategies and scale
summary: Pashto is absent from Whisper's pre-training corpus despite being one of CommonVoice's largest language collections, leaving off-the-shelf models unusable: all Whisper sizes output Arabic, Dari, or Urdu script on Pashto audio, achieving word error rates above 100%. We compare four fine-tuning strategies for whisper-base on CommonVoice Pashto v20: vanilla full fine-tuning, LoRA (rank 64), frozen-encoder (2/6 layers), and multistage Urdu-to-Pashto transfer. We extend vanilla fine-tuning to whisper-small and whisper-large-v3-turbo on CommonVoice Pashto v24 (113 hours). Vanilla fine-tuning achieves WER 21.22% on CV20, outperforming LoRA by 33.36 pp, frozen-encoder by 14.76 pp, and Urdu transfer by 44.56 pp. Frozen-encoder fine-tuning degrades performance on whisper-base (6 encoder layers): layer-function separation does not hold at this depth, and freezing removes a third of trainable capacity. Urdu-to-Pashto transfer fails due to an unverified intermediate checkpoint, phonological mismatch, and insufficient training. On CV24, whisper-small achieves WER 24.89% (2.24 pp over whisper-base at 3.3x parameters); whisper-large-v3-turbo achieves 23.37% (a further 1.52 pp). Diminishing returns indicate whisper-small is the practical optimum at 113 hours. Online augmentation provides 7.25 pp WER benefit over matched training. Error analysis identifies word-final suffix confusion (masculine -ay vs. feminine -a) and retroflex substitutions involving the Pashto-unique consonant /ts/ as dominant failure modes. Fine-tuned checkpoints and evaluation scripts are released on HuggingFace.

AI.tldr: The paper fine-tunes Whisper for Pashto, a language missing from Whisper’s pre-training, evaluates multiple adaptation strategies, and finds that straightforward full-model fine-tuning works best, with whisper-small offering the best accuracy–efficiency trade-off at current data scale.
AI.motivation: Whisper’s multilingual ASR capabilities do not extend to Pashto because the language is absent from its pre-training corpus, causing off-the-shelf models to transcribe Pashto speech in related scripts (Arabic, Dari, Urdu) with extremely high error rates. Given that Pashto has a large speech corpus in CommonVoice, there is a clear need to adapt Whisper effectively for this under-served language. The authors aim to understand which fine-tuning strategies actually work for Pashto, and whether parameter-efficient or cross-lingual transfer methods can match or beat full fine-tuning in low-to-moderate resource settings.
AI.method: The authors systematically compare four adaptation strategies for the whisper-base model using CommonVoice Pashto v20: (1) vanilla full-parameter fine-tuning, (2) LoRA with rank 64, (3) frozen-encoder fine-tuning where 2 or 6 encoder layers are frozen to encourage modularity and reduce training cost, and (4) a multistage Urdu-to-Pashto transfer pipeline that first adapts the model to Urdu, then to Pashto. They then scale the best-performing simple strategy—vanilla fine-tuning—to larger model sizes (whisper-small and whisper-large-v3-turbo) using a newer Pashto dataset version (CommonVoice v24, 113 hours). They additionally test online data augmentation against matched (non-augmented) training and perform qualitative error analysis to characterize typical recognition failures, especially around morphology and Pashto-specific phonology.
AI.result: On CommonVoice Pashto v20, full fine-tuning of whisper-base achieves a WER of 21.22%, substantially outperforming the alternatives: it is 33.36 percentage points better than LoRA, 14.76 points better than frozen-encoder fine-tuning, and 44.56 points better than the Urdu-to-Pashto transfer setup. Frozen-encoder training with 6 frozen encoder layers notably harms performance, suggesting that the encoder does not have cleanly separable layer functions at this depth and that freezing too many layers removes about a third of effective trainable capacity. The Urdu transfer approach underperforms due to reliance on an unverified intermediate Urdu checkpoint, phonological mismatches between Urdu and Pashto, and too little training. On the larger CV24 set (113 hours), vanilla fine-tuning of whisper-small reaches 24.89% WER, improving on whisper-base by 2.24 percentage points while using about 3.3 times more parameters; whisper-large-v3-turbo further improves WER to 23.37%, only 1.52 points better than whisper-small. Online augmentation yields an additional 7.25 percentage point WER reduction compared to matched training. Error analysis reveals that frequent mistakes involve confusion of word-final gendered suffixes (-ay vs. -a) and retroflex consonant substitutions centering on the Pashto-unique affricate /ts/.
AI.conclusion: Vanilla full-model fine-tuning is the most effective strategy for adapting Whisper to Pashto, clearly outperforming parameter-efficient (LoRA) and frozen-encoder approaches, as well as a cross-lingual Urdu-to-Pashto transfer scheme. However, simply scaling to larger Whisper variants shows diminishing returns: whisper-small offers the best balance between performance and model size at roughly 113 hours of Pashto data, making it the most practical choice for real-world deployment under current data constraints. Effective data augmentation is important for performance, and the main remaining challenges lie in modeling Pashto-specific phonology and gender-marking morphology. To support further research and application, the authors release their fine-tuned Pashto Whisper checkpoints and evaluation tools on HuggingFace.

title: Does a Global Perspective Help Prune Sparse MoEs Elegantly?
summary:
Empirical scaling laws for language models have encouraged the development of ever-larger LLMs, despite their growing computational and memory costs. Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoEs) offer a promising alternative by activating only a subset of experts per forward pass, improving efficiency without sacrificing performance. However, the large number of expert parameters still leads to substantial memory consumption.
  Existing pruning methods typically allocate budgets uniformly across layers, overlooking the heterogeneous redundancy that arises in sparse MoEs. We propose GRAPE (Global Redundancy-Aware Pruning of Experts, a global pruning strategy that dynamically allocates pruning budgets based on cross-layer redundancy. Experiments on Mixtral-8x7B, Mixtral-8x22B, DeepSeek-MoE, Qwen-MoE, and GPT-OSS show that, under the same pruning budget, GRAPE consistently achieves the best average performance. On the three main models reported in the paper, it improves average accuracy over the strongest local baseline by 1.40% on average across pruning settings, with gains of up to 2.45%.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GRAPE, a global redundancy-aware expert pruning method for sparse Mixture-of-Experts language models, which reallocates pruning budgets across layers based on redundancy and outperforms existing local pruning strategies under the same sparsity levels.
AI.motivation: Scaling laws push toward ever-larger language models, but their computational and memory costs become prohibitive. Sparse Mixture-of-Experts architectures improve efficiency by activating only a few experts per token, yet they still require massive memory to store all experts. Existing pruning approaches treat all layers similarly and ignore that different MoE layers have different levels of redundancy, leading to suboptimal parameter reduction. There is a need for a pruning method that accounts for heterogeneous redundancy across layers to better trade off memory and performance in MoE LLMs.
AI.method: The authors propose GRAPE (Global Redundancy-Aware Pruning of Experts), which performs global pruning of experts across all MoE layers instead of assigning each layer a fixed pruning budget.
