title: Environmental, Social and Governance Sentiment Analysis on Slovene News: A Novel Dataset and Models
comment: Accepted at the The 7th Financial Narrative Processing Workshop at LREC 26'
summary: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) considerations are increasingly integral to assessing corporate performance, reputation, and long-term sustainability. Yet, reliable ESG ratings remain limited for smaller companies and emerging markets. We introduce the first publicly available Slovene ESG sentiment dataset and a suite of models for automatic ESG sentiment detection. The dataset, derived from the MaCoCu Slovene news collection, combines large language model (LLM)-assisted filtering with human annotation of company-related ESG content. We evaluate the performance of monolingual (SloBERTa) and multilingual (XLM-R) models, embedding-based classifiers (TabPFN), hierarchical ensemble architectures, and large language models. Results show that LLMs achieve the strongest performance on Environmental (Gemma3-27B, F1-macro: 0.61) and Social aspects (gpt-oss 20B, F1-macro: 0.45), while fine-tuned SloBERTa is the best model on Governance classification (F1-macro: 0.54). We then show in a small case study how the best-preforming classifier (gpt-oss) can be applied to investigate ESG aspects for selected companies across a long time frame.

AI.tldr: They build the first Slovene ESG sentiment dataset from news and compare multiple NLP models for ESG sentiment classification, showing LLMs work best for Environmental and Social, while a fine-tuned Slovene BERT model works best for Governance.
AI.motivation: ESG ratings are important for evaluating companies but are scarce and unreliable for small firms and emerging markets, especially in less-resourced languages like Slovene. There is a need for language-specific ESG datasets and tested models to automatically detect ESG-related sentiment and support analysis over time.
AI.method: They construct a Slovene ESG sentiment dataset from the MaCoCu Slovene news collection by using LLM-based filtering to identify company-related ESG content and then applying human annotation. They benchmark several model families: monolingual Slovene transformer (SloBERTa), multilingual transformer (XLM-R), embedding-based classifier (TabPFN), hierarchical ensemble methods, and several large language models. They evaluate macro-F1 across the three ESG dimensions (E, S, G) and conduct a small longitudinal case study using the best model.
AI.result: On Environmental sentiment classification, an LLM (Gemma3-27B) achieves the best performance with macro-F1 of 0.61. For Social aspects, another LLM (gpt-oss 20B) is best with macro-F1 of 0.45. For Governance, a fine-tuned monolingual SloBERTa model outperforms others with macro-F1 of 0.54. In a case study, they demonstrate that the best-performing LLM classifier (gpt-oss) can be used to track ESG-related signals for selected companies over a long time period.
AI.conclusion: High-capacity LLMs currently provide the strongest performance for Environmental and Social ESG sentiment detection in Slovene news, while a specialized monolingual transformer is more effective for Governance. The newly released Slovene ESG sentiment dataset and models enable automated ESG analysis in a low-resource language and can support longitudinal company-level ESG monitoring and research.

title: Discourse Coherence and Response-Guided Context Rewriting for Multi-Party Dialogue Generation
summary: Previous research on multi-party dialogue generation has predominantly leveraged structural information inherent in dialogues to directly inform the generation process. However, the prevalence of colloquial expressions and incomplete utterances in dialogues often impedes comprehension and weakens the fidelity of dialogue structure representations, which is particularly pronounced in multi-party dialogues. In this work, we propose a novel framework DRCR (Discourse coherence and Response-guided Context Rewriting) to improve multi-party dialogue generation through dialogue context rewriting. Specifically, DRCR employs two complementary feedback signals, discourse coherence and response quality, to construct preference data for both context rewriting and response generation. Moreover, we propose a dynamic self-evolution learning method that allows the rewriter and responder to continuously enhance their capabilities through mutual interaction in an iterative training loop. Comprehensive experiments conducted on four multi-party dialogue datasets substantiate the effectiveness of DRCR.

AI.tldr: A framework (DRCR) that rewrites noisy multi-party dialogue context using discourse coherence and response-quality feedback, then iteratively co-trains a rewriter and responder to improve multi-party dialogue generation.
AI.motivation: Multi-party dialogue generation is difficult because real conversations contain colloquial, incomplete, and messy utterances. This noise harms the quality of learned dialogue structure and thus weakens generation performance, especially when methods try to directly use dialogue structure. The authors are motivated to clean or rewrite the context into a more coherent form before generation, and to do so in a way that is directly tied to both discourse-level coherence and the final response quality.
AI.method: They propose DRCR (Discourse coherence and Response-guided Context Rewriting). DRCR first rewrites the dialogue context to improve clarity and structure. It uses two feedback signals: (1) discourse coherence, assessing whether the rewritten context forms a coherent discourse; and (2) response quality, assessing whether the rewritten context leads to better responses. These signals are used to build preference data for both the context rewriter and the response generator. They further introduce a dynamic self-evolution learning strategy, where the rewriter and responder are trained in an iterative loop, each benefiting from the other's improvements over time.
AI.result: Experiments on four multi-party dialogue datasets show that DRCR outperforms existing methods on multi-party dialogue generation metrics, indicating that context rewriting guided by discourse and response feedback leads to better generated dialogues.
AI.conclusion: Rewriting noisy multi-party dialogue contexts with explicit guidance from discourse coherence and response quality, and co-evolving the rewriter and responder in an iterative training loop, is an effective way to improve multi-party dialogue generation performance across multiple datasets.

title: WRAP++: Web discoveRy Amplified Pretraining
comment: Work in progress. Correspondence to ucaswu@tencent.com or wuxing@iie.ac.cn
summary: Synthetic data rephrasing has emerged as a powerful technique for enhancing knowledge acquisition during large language model (LLM) pretraining. However, existing approaches operate at the single-document level, rewriting individual web pages in isolation. This confines synthesized examples to intra-document knowledge, missing cross-document relationships and leaving facts with limited associative context. We propose WRAP++ (Web discoveRy Amplified Pretraining), which amplifies the associative context of factual knowledge by discovering cross-document relationships from web hyperlinks and synthesizing joint QA over each discovered document pair. Concretely, WRAP++ discovers high-confidence relational motifs including dual-links and co-mentions, and synthesizes QA that requires reasoning across both documents. This produces relational knowledge absent from either source document alone, creating diverse entry points to the same facts. Because the number of valid entity pairs grows combinatorially, this discovery-driven synthesis also amplifies data scale far beyond single-document rewriting. Instantiating WRAP++ on Wikipedia, we amplify ~8.4B tokens of raw text into 80B tokens of cross-document QA data. On SimpleQA, OLMo-based models at both 7B and 32B scales trained with WRAP++ substantially outperform single-document approaches and exhibit sustained scaling gains, underscoring the advantage of cross-document knowledge discovery and amplification.

AI.tldr: WRAP++ is a method to generate large-scale, cross-document QA-style synthetic data for LLM pretraining by exploiting web hyperlinks to discover related document pairs, leading to better knowledge acquisition than single-document rephrasing.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic data rephrasing for LLM pretraining works at the single-document level, so it only captures intra-document knowledge and fails to exploit cross-document relationships and richer associative context. This limits how well models can learn connections between facts and constrains data scaling. The authors want to improve factual knowledge acquisition and scaling by leveraging relationships between documents on the web.
AI.method: WRAP++ (Web discoveRy Amplified Pretraining) automatically discovers related document pairs using web hyperlink structures and entity co-mentions, identifying high-confidence relational motifs such as dual-links and co-mentions. For each discovered document pair, it synthesizes joint QA examples that require reasoning across both documents, ensuring that the answer relies on information distributed between them. This discovery-driven approach allows combinatorial growth in valid entity/document pairs, turning raw web text (e.g., Wikipedia) into a much larger cross-document QA corpus for pretraining.
AI.result: Applied to Wikipedia, WRAP++ expands about 8.4B tokens of raw text into 80B tokens of cross-document QA data. When used to pretrain OLMo-based models at 7B and 32B parameters, these models significantly outperform counterparts trained on conventional single-document synthetic data (e.g., single-page rewriting) on the SimpleQA benchmark, and the gains increase with model size, indicating effective scaling.
AI.conclusion: Cross-document, hyperlink-driven knowledge discovery and QA synthesis (WRAP++) is more effective than single-document rephrasing for LLM pretraining. It not only dramatically amplifies data scale via combinatorial document pairing but also enriches the associative context of factual knowledge. The improved SimpleQA results and sustained scaling behavior suggest that exploiting web-level relational structure is a promising direction for building stronger, knowledge-intensive LLMs.

title: When Is Thinking Enough? Early Exit via Sufficiency Assessment for Efficient Reasoning
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) have achieved remarkable performance in complex reasoning tasks, driven by their powerful inference-time scaling capability. However, LRMs often suffer from overthinking, which results in substantial computational redundancy and significantly reduces efficiency. Early-exit methods aim to mitigate this issue by terminating reasoning once sufficient evidence has been generated, yet existing approaches mostly rely on handcrafted or empirical indicators that are unreliable and impractical. In this work, we introduce Dynamic Thought Sufficiency in Reasoning (DTSR), a novel framework for efficient reasoning that enables the model to dynamically assess the sufficiency of its chain-of-thought (CoT) and determine the optimal point for early exit. Inspired by human metacognition, DTSR operates in two stages: (1) Reflection Signal Monitoring, which identifies reflection signals as potential cues for early exit, and (2) Thought Sufficiency Check, which evaluates whether the current CoT is sufficient to derive the final answer. Experimental results on the Qwen3 models show that DTSR reduces reasoning length by 28.9%-34.9% with minimal performance loss, effectively mitigating overthinking. We further discuss overconfidence in LRMs and self-evaluation paradigms, providing valuable insights for early-exit reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DTSR, a metacognition-inspired framework that lets large reasoning models stop their chain-of-thought early when current reasoning is already sufficient, reducing unnecessary computation while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models achieve strong performance via long chain-of-thought reasoning but often "overthink", producing unnecessarily long reasoning traces that waste computation and harm efficiency. Existing early-exit approaches depend on hand-crafted or empirical stopping signals, which are unreliable and hard to generalize. There is a need for a principled, learnable mechanism that lets the model itself judge when its current reasoning is sufficient to answer, analogous to human metacognition about when we have thought enough.
AI.method: They introduce Dynamic Thought Sufficiency in Reasoning (DTSR), a two-stage framework. First, a Reflection Signal Monitoring stage tracks internal reflection signals during chain-of-thought generation as potential indicators that the model might be ready to stop. Second, a Thought Sufficiency Check stage evaluates whether the current partial chain-of-thought is actually sufficient to derive the final answer, and, if so, triggers early exit. The design is inspired by human self-monitoring of thought sufficiency and is implemented on top of Qwen3 large reasoning models, enabling dynamic, per-instance decisions about when to terminate reasoning.
AI.result: On Qwen3 models, DTSR cuts reasoning length by about 28.9%–34.9% while incurring only minimal performance degradation, demonstrating that much of the original reasoning was redundant. The framework also surfaces and helps analyze phenomena such as overconfidence in large reasoning models and the behavior of self-evaluation based stopping criteria.
AI.conclusion: DTSR offers an effective, metacognition-inspired solution to overthinking in large reasoning models: it allows models to dynamically decide when their current chain-of-thought is sufficient, achieving substantial computational savings with little loss in accuracy. The work suggests that principled self-evaluation and reflection-signal modeling are promising directions for early-exit reasoning and for better understanding confidence and metacognition in large reasoning models.

title: On the Step Length Confounding in LLM Reasoning Data Selection
comment: Accepted by Findings of ACL 2026. 15 pages, 9 figures. Code: https://github.com/wangbing1416/ASLEC
summary: Large reasoning models have recently demonstrated strong performance on complex tasks that require long chain-of-thought reasoning, through supervised fine-tuning on large-scale and high-quality datasets. To construct such datasets, existing pipelines generate long reasoning data from more capable Large Language Models (LLMs) and apply manually heuristic or naturalness-based selection methods to filter high-quality samples. Despite the proven effectiveness of naturalness-based data selection, which ranks data by the average log probability assigned by LLMs, our analysis shows that, when applied to LLM reasoning datasets, it systematically prefers samples with longer reasoning steps (i.e., more tokens per step) rather than higher-quality ones, a phenomenon we term step length confounding. Through quantitative analysis, we attribute this phenomenon to low-probability first tokens in reasoning steps; longer steps dilute their influence, thereby inflating the average log probabilities. To address this issue, we propose two variant methods: ASLEC-DROP, which drops first-token probabilities when computing average log probability, and ASLEC-CASL, which applies a causal debiasing regression to remove the first tokens' confounding effect. Experiments across four LLMs and five evaluation benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in mitigating the step length confounding problem.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and fixes a bias in how we select long chain-of-thought training data for reasoning models: current "naturalness" filters favor longer reasoning steps instead of genuinely better reasoning, and the authors propose adjusted scoring methods that remove this bias.
AI.motivation: Recent large reasoning models rely on supervised fine-tuning with long chain-of-thought traces generated by stronger LLMs. A key step is filtering those traces to keep only high-quality reasoning. Naturalness-based selection, which uses average log probability as a quality proxy, has worked well, but the authors observe that on reasoning datasets it appears to systematically prefer samples with longer reasoning steps. This suggests that the selection metric may be confounded by step length, potentially harming data quality and thus downstream reasoning performance. The motivation is to understand and correct this bias so that data selection truly reflects reasoning quality, not artifacts of tokenization or step length.
AI.method: 1) Empirical analysis: The authors study how naturalness-based selection (ranking by average log probability) behaves on LLM-generated reasoning datasets, and they identify a phenomenon where longer reasoning steps tend to get higher scores independent of quality. They analyze token-level probabilities and show that first tokens in reasoning steps often have low probability, which overly penalizes short steps. 2) Causal analysis: They frame the effect of step length and first-token probabilities as a confounding problem, where step length mediates/dilutes the negative effect of low-probability first tokens on the average log probability. 3) Two proposed methods: (a) ASLEC-DROP: modify the scoring rule by dropping the first-token probabilities in each reasoning step when computing average log probability, thus neutralizing their disproportionate influence. (b) ASLEC-CASL: use a causal debiasing regression model to explicitly estimate and remove the confounding effect of first tokens’ probabilities on the selection score. 4) Experiments: Evaluate both methods across four different LLMs and five reasoning benchmarks, comparing selected data quality and downstream model performance to standard naturalness-based selection.
AI.result: They show that standard naturalness-based selection is indeed biased toward samples with longer reasoning steps and that this bias is explained by low-probability first tokens whose contribution is diluted in longer steps. Both proposed variants, ASLEC-DROP and ASLEC-CASL, significantly reduce this step length confounding. Empirically, datasets filtered with these methods lead to better or more robust performance on multiple reasoning benchmarks and across four different LLM backbones, compared to using uncorrected average log probability as the selection criterion.
AI.conclusion: Naturalness-based filtering using average log probability is not a neutral quality measure on reasoning data; it is systematically confounded by step length due to low-probability first tokens within reasoning steps. This leads to a preference for unnecessarily long chains of thought, rather than truly high-quality reasoning. By modeling and correcting this confounding—either with a simple heuristic (dropping first-token probabilities) or a causal debiasing regression—the selection process can more accurately capture reasoning quality. The proposed ASLEC-DROP and ASLEC-CASL methods effectively mitigate step length confounding and improve the usefulness of selected reasoning data for training large reasoning models.

title: GCoT-Decoding: Unlocking Deep Reasoning Paths for Universal Question Answering
summary: Chain-of-Thought reasoning can enhance large language models, but it requires manually designed prompts to guide the model. Recently proposed CoT-decoding enables the model to generate CoT-style reasoning paths without prompts, but it is only applicable to problems with fixed answer sets. To address this limitation, we propose a general decoding strategy GCoT-decoding that extends applicability to a broader range of question-answering tasks. GCoT-decoding employs a two-stage branching method combining Fibonacci sampling and heuristic error backtracking to generate candidate decoding paths. It then splits each path into a reasoning span and an answer span to accurately compute path confidence, and finally aggregates semantically similar paths to identify a consensus answer, replacing traditional majority voting. We conduct extensive experiments on six datasets covering both fixed and free QA tasks. Our method not only maintains strong performance on fixed QA but also achieves significant improvements on free QA, demonstrating its generality.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes GCoT-decoding, a general chain-of-thought decoding strategy that works for both fixed-answer and free-form QA tasks, improving reasoning performance without manually crafted CoT prompts.
AI.motivation: Existing Chain-of-Thought reasoning approaches need manually designed prompts and are often limited to tasks with fixed answer sets. A recent prompt-free CoT-decoding method removes prompt engineering but only works when answers come from a predefined set. There is a need for a general decoding strategy that can automatically induce CoT reasoning and handle open-ended question answering where answers are not restricted to fixed options.
AI.method: The authors introduce GCoT-decoding, a general decoding framework for large language models. It uses a two-stage branching process that combines Fibonacci sampling (to explore diverse reasoning paths with a controlled branching factor) and heuristic error backtracking (to correct or abandon low-quality reasoning branches). Each generated path is split into a reasoning span and an answer span so that path confidence can be computed accurately based on the answer segment. Finally, instead of simple majority voting, the method clusters or aggregates semantically similar paths to form a consensus answer across diverse reasoning trajectories.
AI.result: Across six datasets spanning both fixed-answer and free-form QA tasks, GCoT-decoding matches strong performance on fixed-answer benchmarks while delivering substantial gains on free-form question answering compared to prior CoT-decoding and standard decoding baselines.
AI.conclusion: GCoT-decoding generalizes prompt-free Chain-of-Thought reasoning to a wider variety of QA tasks, including those with free-form answers. By using structured branching, error backtracking, span-based confidence estimation, and semantic aggregation of reasoning paths, it offers a more robust and general decoding strategy for leveraging CoT in large language models.

title: HingeMem: Boundary Guided Long-Term Memory with Query Adaptive Retrieval for Scalable Dialogues
summary: Long-term memory is critical for dialogue systems that support continuous, sustainable, and personalized interactions. However, existing methods rely on continuous summarization or OpenIE-based graph construction paired with fixed Top-\textit{k} retrieval, leading to limited adaptability across query categories and high computational overhead. In this paper, we propose HingeMem, a boundary-guided long-term memory that operationalizes event segmentation theory to build an interpretable indexing interface via boundary-triggered hyperedges over four elements: person, time, location, and topic. When any such element changes, HingeMem draws a boundary and writes the current segment, thereby reducing redundant operations and preserving salient context. To enable robust and efficient retrieval under diverse information needs, HingeMem introduces query-adaptive retrieval mechanisms that jointly decide (a) \textit{what to retrieve}: determine the query-conditioned routing over the element-indexed memory; (b) \textit{how much to retrieve}: control the retrieval depth based on the estimated query type. Extensive experiments across LLM scales (from 0.6B to production-tier models; \textit{e.g.}, Qwen3-0.6B to Qwen-Flash) on LOCOMO show that HingeMem achieves approximately $20\%$ relative improvement over strong baselines without query categories specification, while reducing computational cost (68\%$\downarrow$ question answering token cost compared to HippoRAG2). Beyond advancing memory modeling, HingeMem's adaptive retrieval makes it a strong fit for web applications requiring efficient and trustworthy memory over extended interactions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HingeMem, a boundary-guided long-term memory system for dialogue agents that segments conversations into events and retrieves memories adaptively by query type, improving both accuracy and efficiency over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing long-term memory approaches for dialogue systems often use continuous summarization or OpenIE-based graphs combined with fixed Top-k retrieval. These methods struggle to adapt retrieval behavior to different kinds of user queries, can miss relevant context, and incur high computational costs, especially over long interactions. There is a need for a more interpretable, efficient, and query-adaptive memory mechanism that better mirrors how humans segment events and recall information.
AI.method: HingeMem is built around event segmentation theory and defines hyperedges over four core elements: person, time, location, and topic. When any of these elements changes during a dialogue, a boundary is drawn and the current segment is written to memory, forming a boundary-triggered hyperedge. This creates an interpretable index over the conversation history. For retrieval, HingeMem uses query-adaptive mechanisms that (a) route the query to the appropriate element-indexed portions of memory (deciding what to retrieve) and (b) adjust how many segments to retrieve by estimating the query type (deciding how much to retrieve), instead of using a fixed Top-k strategy.
AI.result: On the LOCOMO benchmark and across language models from 0.6B parameters up to production-scale models such as Qwen-Flash, HingeMem delivers about 20% relative performance improvement compared with strong baselines that do not have explicit query category information. It also significantly reduces computational cost, including a 68% reduction in question-answering token cost compared to the HippoRAG2 method.
AI.conclusion: By combining boundary-based event segmentation with query-adaptive retrieval, HingeMem offers an efficient, interpretable, and more human-like long-term memory system for dialogue agents. Its ability to reduce redundancy in memory writing and dynamically tailor retrieval depth and scope leads to better performance and lower cost, making it particularly well-suited for real-world web applications that require reliable, scalable memory over prolonged interactions.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Diagnosing Algebraic Reasoning Failures in LLMs Across Nine Complexity Dimensions
comment: Under Review as a conference paper at COLM 2026
summary: Algebraic reasoning remains one of the most informative stress tests for large language models, yet current benchmarks provide no mechanism for attributing failure to a specific cause. When a model fails an algebraic problem, a single accuracy score cannot reveal whether the expression was too deeply nested, the operator too uncommon, the intermediate state count too high, or the dependency chain too long. Prior work has studied individual failure modes in isolation, but no framework has varied each complexity factor independently under strict experimental control. No prior system has offered automatic generation and verification of problems of increasing complexity to track model progress over time. We introduce a nine-dimension algebraic complexity framework in which each factor is varied independently while all others are held fixed, with problem generation and verification handled by a parametric pipeline requiring no human annotation. Each dimension is grounded in a documented LLM failure mode and captures a structurally distinct aspect of algebraic difficulty, including expression nesting depth, simultaneous intermediate result count, sub-expression complexity, operator hardness, and dependent reasoning chain length. We evaluated seven instruction-tuned models spanning 8B to 235B parameters across all nine dimensions and find that working memory is the dominant scale-invariant bottleneck. Every model collapses between 20 and 30 parallel branches regardless of parameter count, pointing to a hard architectural constraint rather than a solvable capacity limitation. Our analysis further identifies a minimal yet diagnostically sufficient subset of five dimensions that together span the full space of documented algebraic failure modes, providing a complete complexity profile of a model's algebraic reasoning capacity.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a controlled, multi-dimensional framework to measure and diagnose why large language models fail at algebraic reasoning, revealing working memory limits as the primary bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Existing algebraic reasoning benchmarks for LLMs only provide aggregate accuracy and cannot disentangle specific failure causes such as nesting depth, operator type, or dependency length. Prior research examined individual factors separately, lacked independent control of multiple complexity dimensions, and did not support automatic generation and verification of algebra problems at systematically increasing difficulty levels. This prevents precise diagnosis of model weaknesses and limits our ability to track genuine reasoning improvements over time.
AI.method: The authors design a nine-dimensional algebraic complexity framework where each dimension corresponds to a known LLM failure mode (e.g., nesting depth, number of simultaneous intermediate results, sub-expression complexity, operator hardness, and dependent reasoning chain length). They build a parametric, fully automatic problem generation and verification pipeline that can vary a single complexity factor while holding the others fixed, requiring no human annotation. Using this framework, they evaluate seven instruction-tuned LLMs ranging from 8B to 235B parameters along each dimension, measuring performance as complexity scales.
AI.result: Across models and dimensions, the dominant limitation is a working-memory-like bottleneck: all tested models fail when required to manage between 20–30 parallel branches (simultaneous intermediate results), and this collapse threshold is essentially invariant to model size. This suggests an architectural constraint rather than a simple lack of parameters or capacity. Additionally, the analysis shows that only five of the nine complexity dimensions are needed to reconstruct a complete picture of algebraic difficulty, as this subset is sufficient to span all documented algebraic failure modes in the study.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a fine-grained and scalable way to diagnose algebraic reasoning in LLMs, moving beyond single aggregate scores to interpretable dimensions tied to concrete failure modes. The findings imply that scaling model size alone will not overcome key reasoning bottlenecks, since working memory constraints appear structurally embedded in current architectures. The identified five-dimension subset provides a practical diagnostic toolkit for tracking and comparing models’ algebraic reasoning capabilities over time.

title: MedDialBench: Benchmarking LLM Diagnostic Robustness under Parametric Adversarial Patient Behaviors
summary:
Interactive medical dialogue benchmarks have shown that LLM diagnostic accuracy degrades significantly when interacting with non-cooperative patients, yet existing approaches either apply adversarial behaviors without graded severity or case-specific grounding, or reduce patient non-cooperation to a single ungraded axis, and none analyze cross-dimension interactions.
  We introduce MedDialBench, a benchmark enabling controlled, dose-response characterization of how individual patient behavior dimensions affect LLM diagnostic robustness. It decomposes patient behavior into five dimensions -- Logic Consistency, Health Cognition, Expression Style, Disclosure, and Attitude -- each with graded severity levels and case-specific behavioral scripts. This controlled factorial design enables graded sensitivity analysis, dose-response profiling, and cross-dimension interaction detection.
  Evaluating five frontier LLMs across 7,225 dialogues (85 cases x 17 configurations x 5 models), we find a fundamental asymmetry: information pollution (fabricating symptoms) produces 1.7-3.4x larger accuracy drops than information deficit (withholding information), and fabricating is the only configuration achieving statistical significance across all five models (McNemar p < 0.05). Among six dimension combinations, fabricating is the sole driver of super-additive interaction: all three fabricating-involving pairs produce O/E ratios of 0.70-0.81 (35-44% of eligible cases fail under the combination despite succeeding under each dimension alone), while all non-fabricating pairs show purely additive effects (O/E ~ 1.0). Inquiry strategy moderates deficit but not pollution: exhaustive questioning recovers withheld information, but cannot compensate for fabricated inputs. Models exhibit distinct vulnerability profiles, with worst-case drops ranging from 38.8 to 54.1 percentage points.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MedDialBench, a benchmark to systematically test how different types and severities of non-cooperative patient behaviors affect LLM diagnostic accuracy in interactive medical dialogues, revealing that fabricated symptoms are far more harmful than missing information and drive super-additive failures when combined with other adverse behaviors.
AI.motivation: Existing medical dialogue benchmarks show that LLMs perform worse with non-cooperative patients, but current stress tests are limited: they often use adversarial behaviors without graded severity, ignore case-specific context, collapse non-cooperation into a single crude dimension, and fail to analyze how different behavior types interact. The authors want a controlled, fine-grained way to measure how specific patient behaviors individually and jointly degrade diagnostic robustness, to better understand LLM vulnerabilities and guide model and interface design.
AI.method: The authors design MedDialBench, which decomposes patient behavior into five distinct dimensions—Logic Consistency, Health Cognition, Expression Style, Disclosure, and Attitude—each with multiple severity levels and case-specific behavioral scripts. Using a factorial experimental design, they generate interactive dialogue scenarios where these dimensions can be independently varied and combined, enabling graded sensitivity and interaction analysis. They then evaluate five state-of-the-art LLMs on 7,225 simulated doctor–patient dialogues, spanning 85 clinical cases, 17 behavior configurations, and 5 models, and analyze diagnostic accuracy, dose-response to severity, and cross-dimension interaction patterns using statistical tests such as McNemar tests and observed/expected (O/E) failure ratios.
AI.result: Across more than seven thousand dialogues, all models experience substantial accuracy degradation under non-cooperative behaviors, but in an asymmetric way: information pollution (fabricated symptoms) causes 1.7–3.4 times larger drops in diagnostic accuracy than information deficits (withheld information). Fabrication is the only behavior configuration that significantly harms all five models by McNemar p < 0.05. When examining pairs of adverse behavior dimensions, combinations that include fabrication show super-additive harm: 35–44% of cases that each individual behavior dimension alone would not cause to fail now fail under the combination, reflected by O/E ratios of 0.70–0.81. In contrast, all non-fabrication pairs behave additively with O/E ≈ 1.0. Exhaustive questioning strategies help the models recover performance when information is missing but not when it is polluted with false symptoms. Vulnerability varies by model, with worst-case accuracy drops between approximately 39 and 54 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLMs in medical dialogue are much more fragile to information pollution than to information deficit, with fabricated symptoms uniquely driving super-additive failure patterns when combined with other adverse patient behaviors. MedDialBench offers a structured way to profile these vulnerabilities across multiple behavior dimensions and severities, enabling dose-response and interaction analyses. The findings imply that improving robustness to fabricated or misleading patient inputs is more critical than only mitigating missing information, and that better detection or handling of inconsistent or fabricated content will be essential for safer LLM deployment in clinical conversational settings.

title: Cognitive Loop of Thought: Reversible Hierarchical Markov Chain for Efficient Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Multi-step Chain-of-Thought (CoT) has significantly advanced the mathematical reasoning capabilities of LLMs by leveraging explicit reasoning steps. However, the widespread adoption of Long CoT often results in sequence lengths that exceed manageable computational limits. While existing approaches attempt to alleviate this by reducing KV Cache redundancy via Markov chain-like structures, they introduce two critical limitations: inherent memorylessness (loss of context) and limited backward reasoning capability. To address these limitations, we propose a novel Chain-of-Thought framework based on Reversible Hierarchical Markov Chain, termed Cognitive Loop of Thought (CLoT), and a backward reasoning dataset CLoT-Instruct. In CLoT, problems are decomposed into sub-problems with hierarchical dependencies. Inspired by human cognitive processes, we introduce a backward verification mechanism at each hierarchical layer. Furthermore, we implement a pruning strategy: once higher-level sub-problems are verified, redundant lower-level sub-problems are pruned to maximize efficiency. This approach effectively mitigates error propagation and enhances reasoning robustness. Experiments on four mathematical benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. Notably, on the AddSub dataset using GPT-4o-mini, CLoT achieves 99.0% accuracy, outperforming traditional CoT and CoT-SC by 4.1% and 2.9%, respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Cognitive Loop of Thought (CLoT), a reversible hierarchical Markov Chain-based Chain-of-Thought framework with backward verification and pruning to improve mathematical reasoning accuracy and efficiency in LLMs.
AI.motivation: Long multi-step Chain-of-Thought improves mathematical reasoning but leads to excessively long sequences and high computational cost. Existing KV cache reduction methods rely on Markov-like memoryless structures, which lose important context and limit backward reasoning. The authors aim to retain rich context and enable verification-style backward reasoning while still controlling sequence length and computation.
AI.method: They introduce CLoT, a Chain-of-Thought framework based on a Reversible Hierarchical Markov Chain. Problems are decomposed into hierarchically organized sub-problems. At each hierarchical level, a backward verification mechanism checks the correctness of higher-level reasoning steps. Once higher-level sub-problems are verified, a pruning strategy discards redundant lower-level sub-problems from the sequence to reduce computation and prevent unnecessary error propagation. They also construct a backward reasoning instruction dataset, CLoT-Instruct, to train or adapt models to this cognitive loop process.
AI.result: On four mathematical reasoning benchmarks, CLoT improves both robustness and accuracy compared with standard CoT and CoT with self-consistency. On the AddSub dataset using GPT-4o-mini, CLoT reaches 99.0% accuracy, surpassing traditional CoT by 4.1 percentage points and CoT-SC by 2.9 percentage points, showing that reversible hierarchical reasoning with pruning yields better performance under constrained sequence length.
AI.conclusion: Recasting Chain-of-Thought as a reversible hierarchical Markov process with embedded backward verification and pruning allows LLMs to perform more reliable and efficient mathematical reasoning than standard long CoT approaches. The CLoT framework addresses the memorylessness and lack of backward reasoning in prior KV-cache-reduction methods, reducing error propagation and sequence length while boosting accuracy on mathematical benchmarks.

title: Do We Need Distinct Representations for Every Speech Token? Unveiling and Exploiting Redundancy in Large Speech Language Models
summary: Large Speech Language Models (LSLMs) typically operate at high token rates (tokens/s) to ensure acoustic fidelity, yet this results in sequence lengths that far exceed the underlying semantic content, incurring prohibitive inference costs. In this paper, we empirically revisit the necessity of such granular token-level processing. Through layer-wise oracle interventions, we unveil a structured redundancy hierarchy: while shallow layers encode essential acoustic details, deep layers exhibit extreme redundancy, allowing for aggressive compression. Motivated by these findings, we introduce Affinity Pooling, a training-free, similarity-based token merging mechanism. By strategically applying this method at both input and deep layers, we effectively compress speech representations without compromising semantic information. Extensive evaluations across three tasks demonstrate that our approach reduces prefilling FLOPs by 27.48\% while maintaining competitive accuracy. Practical deployment further confirms significant efficiency gains, yielding up to $\sim$1.7$\times$ memory savings and $\sim$1.1$\times$ faster time-to-first-token on long utterances. Our results challenge the necessity of fully distinct token representations, providing new perspectives on LSLM efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that deep layers of Large Speech Language Models contain highly redundant token representations and introduces a simple, training-free token merging method (Affinity Pooling) that compresses sequences and speeds up inference without hurting accuracy much.
AI.motivation: Large Speech Language Models process speech at very high token rates to preserve acoustic detail, which leads to long sequences and expensive inference. The authors want to understand whether all these tokens are really necessary at all layers, and if not, how to safely compress them to make LSLMs more efficient without retraining.
AI.method: 1) Perform layer-wise oracle interventions to measure how sensitive model performance is to removing or compressing tokens at different depths, revealing where redundancy exists. 2) Based on the observed redundancy hierarchy (shallow vs deep layers), design Affinity Pooling, a training-free, similarity-based token merging scheme that groups similar tokens and pools their representations. 3) Apply pooling both at the input side and in deep layers of the LSLM and evaluate on several speech tasks. 4) Measure computational savings (FLOPs, memory, latency) and accuracy trade-offs.
AI.result: Experiments on three speech tasks show that Affinity Pooling cuts prefilling FLOPs by about 27.5% while keeping accuracy close to the baseline. In realistic deployment settings for long utterances, the approach yields up to about 1.7x memory reduction and around 1.1x speedup in time-to-first-token, with minimal performance degradation, confirming that many deep-layer token representations are redundant.
AI.conclusion: The study finds that LSLMs do not need fully distinct token-level representations throughout all layers: shallow layers must stay fine-grained for acoustic details, but deep layers exhibit strong redundancy that can be exploited for compression. The proposed Affinity Pooling method leverages this structure to reduce sequence length and computation in a training-free way, improving practical efficiency while largely preserving semantic performance and offering a new angle for designing more efficient speech LLMs.

title: AGSC: Adaptive Granularity and Semantic Clustering for Uncertainty Quantification in Long-text Generation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in long-form generation, yet their application is hindered by the hallucination problem. While Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) is essential for assessing reliability, the complex structure makes reliable aggregation across heterogeneous themes difficult, in addition, existing methods often overlook the nuance of neutral information and suffer from the high computational cost of fine-grained decomposition. To address these challenges, we propose AGSC (Adaptive Granularity and GMM-based Semantic Clustering), a UQ framework tailored for long-form generation. AGSC first uses NLI neutral probabilities as triggers to distinguish irrelevance from uncertainty, reducing unnecessary computation. It then applies Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) soft clustering to model latent semantic themes and assign topic-aware weights for downstream aggregation. Experiments on BIO and LongFact show that AGSC achieves state-of-the-art correlation with factuality while reducing inference time by about 60% compared to full atomic decomposition.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AGSC, a framework for uncertainty quantification in long-form LLM generation that better correlates with factuality while being more efficient.
AI.motivation: Long-form outputs from LLMs often contain hallucinations, but existing uncertainty quantification methods struggle to reliably aggregate uncertainty across heterogeneous semantic themes, ignore the role of neutral/irrelevant content, and are computationally expensive due to fine-grained decomposition.
AI.method: AGSC (Adaptive Granularity and GMM-based Semantic Clustering) first uses NLI-based neutral probabilities to separate irrelevant content from genuinely uncertain content, thereby avoiding unnecessary detailed analysis. It then performs soft clustering with a Gaussian Mixture Model over latent semantic themes in the text and uses these clusters to assign topic-aware weights when aggregating token- or span-level uncertainty into a document-level score.
AI.result: On the BIO and LongFact datasets, AGSC attains state-of-the-art correlation between its uncertainty estimates and factuality labels, while cutting inference time by about 60% relative to methods that perform full atomic (fine-grained) decomposition of the text.
AI.conclusion: By combining adaptive granularity triggered by NLI neutrality with GMM-based semantic clustering, AGSC provides a more accurate and significantly more efficient uncertainty quantification approach for long-form LLM generation, making it better suited for practical use where both reliability and speed matter.

title: The AI Skills Shift: Mapping Skill Obsolescence, Emergence, and Transition Pathways in the LLM Era
comment: 11 pages, 12 figures, 2 tables, 17 references. Code and data available at
summary: As Large Language Models reshape the global labor market, policymakers and workers need empirical data on which occupational skills may be most susceptible to automation. We present the Skill Automation Feasibility Index (SAFI), benchmarking four frontier LLMs -- LLaMA 3.3 70B, Mistral Large, Qwen 2.5 72B, and Gemini 2.5 Flash -- across 263 text-based tasks spanning all 35 skills in the U.S. Department of Labor's O*NET taxonomy (1,052 total model calls, 0% failure rate). Cross-referencing with real-world AI adoption data from the Anthropic Economic Index (756 occupations, 17,998 tasks), we propose an AI Impact Matrix -- an interpretive framework that positions skills along four quadrants: High Displacement Risk, Upskilling Required, AI-Augmented, and Lower Displacement Risk. Key findings: (1) Mathematics (SAFI: 73.2) and Programming (71.8) receive the highest automation feasibility scores; Active Listening (42.2) and Reading Comprehension (45.5) receive the lowest; (2) a "capability-demand inversion" where skills most demanded in AI-exposed jobs are those LLMs perform least well at in our benchmark; (3) 78.7% of observed AI interactions are augmentation, not automation; (4) all four models converge to similar skill profiles (3.6-point spread), suggesting that text-based automation feasibility may be more skill-dependent than model-dependent. SAFI measures LLM performance on text-based representations of skills, not full occupational execution. All data, code, and model responses are open-sourced.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Skill Automation Feasibility Index (SAFI) to quantify how susceptible different occupational skills are to automation by frontier LLMs, and combines this with real-world AI adoption data to map skills into displacement vs augmentation risk categories.
AI.motivation: As LLMs increasingly affect the labor market, policymakers, firms, and workers lack empirical, skill-level evidence on which aspects of work are most automatable, which are likely to be augmented, and where upskilling is needed. Existing discussions are often occupation-level, speculative, or not grounded in systematic benchmarking of current LLM capabilities across a comprehensive skills taxonomy such as O*NET. The paper aims to provide a standardized, transparent way to measure automation feasibility of skills and to interpret these measures in light of real-world AI usage in jobs.
AI.method: The authors construct the Skill Automation Feasibility Index (SAFI) by designing 263 text-based tasks that cover all 35 skills in the U.S. Department of Labor's O*NET taxonomy. They benchmark four frontier LLMs (LLaMA 3.3 70B, Mistral Large, Qwen 2.5 72B, Gemini 2.5 Flash) on these tasks, making 1,052 model calls with no failures, and derive per-skill performance scores. They then link these skill-level scores to real-world AI adoption evidence from the Anthropic Economic Index, which contains data on 17,998 tasks across 756 occupations. Using these two data sources, they construct an AI Impact Matrix that classifies skills into quadrants—High Displacement Risk, Upskilling Required, AI-Augmented, Lower Displacement Risk—based on both model capability (SAFI) and observed AI use in the labor market.
AI.result: Mathematics (SAFI 73.2) and Programming (71.8) emerge as the most automation-feasible skills, while Active Listening (42.2) and Reading Comprehension (45.5) are among the least automatable in text-based form. The authors observe a capability-demand inversion: skills that are most demanded in AI-exposed jobs tend to be those where LLM performance is relatively weak in their benchmark. They also find that 78.7% of observed AI interactions in the Anthropic Economic Index are augmentative rather than fully automating tasks. Across all four frontier models, skill-level performance profiles are highly similar, with only a 3.6-point spread, indicating that variation in automation feasibility is driven more by the type of skill than by which specific LLM is used.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current frontier LLMs are most capable of automating highly structured, formalizable skills like mathematics and programming, while they are less effective at skills that involve nuanced human interaction and comprehension, such as active listening and reading comprehension as operationalized here. Because most observed AI use in real jobs is augmentative, AI is currently more of a complement than a substitute for human labor across many skills. The similarity of skill profiles across different models suggests that assessments of automation risk should focus on the nature of the skill rather than specific LLM brands. SAFI is positioned as a transparent, extensible benchmark for policymakers, researchers, and practitioners to reason about text-based automation feasibility, with the caveat that it evaluates skill proxies rather than complete job performance; all artifacts are open-sourced to support further research and policy analysis.

title: SemEval-2026 Task 9: Detecting Multilingual, Multicultural and Multievent Online Polarization
summary: We present SemEval-2026 Task 9, a shared task on online polarization detection, covering 22 languages and comprising over 110K annotated instances. Each data instance is multi-labeled with the presence of polarization, polarization type, and polarization manifestation. Participants were asked to predict labels in three sub-tasks: (1) detecting the presence of polarization, (2) identifying the type of polarization, and (3) recognizing the polarization manifestation. The three tasks attracted over 1,000 participants worldwide and more than 10k submission on Codabench. We received final submissions from 67 teams and 73 system description papers. We report the baseline results and analyze the performance of the best-performing systems, highlighting the most common approaches and the most effective methods across different subtasks and languages. The dataset of this task is publicly available.

AI.tldr: SemEval-2026 Task 9 introduces a large-scale, multilingual shared task and dataset for detecting online polarization, with three related subtasks and extensive participation analysis.
AI.motivation: Online polarization is a growing concern in digital communication, yet existing resources for its automatic detection are limited, especially across many languages and nuanced aspects of polarization. The paper aims to create a standardized benchmark and dataset that enable the research community to systematically study and compare methods for detecting not only whether polarization exists but also its types and manifestations in online content, and to understand what methods work best across languages.
AI.method: The organizers built a multilingual dataset of more than 110K online instances in 22 languages, each annotated with three label dimensions: presence of polarization, type of polarization, and manifestation of polarization. They defined three corresponding shared-task subtasks, hosted the competition on Codabench, and collected systems and submissions from participating teams. They implemented baselines, evaluated all submissions on the three subtasks across languages, and then analyzed the best-performing systems to identify common modeling approaches and methods that are most effective in different settings.
AI.result: The task attracted over 1,000 participants, more than 10,000 Codabench submissions, and final system runs from 67 teams plus 73 system description papers. Baseline performance metrics are reported, and comparative analyses show which approaches yield the strongest results in each subtask and across languages. The outcome includes a publicly released, large-scale multilingual dataset and a benchmark of state-of-the-art methods for polarization detection.
AI.conclusion: SemEval-2026 Task 9 successfully establishes a comprehensive multilingual benchmark for online polarization detection, demonstrating strong engagement from the research community and providing evidence on effective modeling strategies for detecting polarization presence, type, and manifestation. The publicly available dataset and results are intended to serve as a foundation for future work on understanding and mitigating online polarization across languages.

title: Self-Preference Bias in Rubric-Based Evaluation of Large Language Models
summary: LLM-as-a-judge has become the de facto approach for evaluating LLM outputs. However, judges are known to exhibit self-preference bias (SPB): they tend to favor outputs produced by themselves or by models from their own family. This skews evaluations and, thus, hinders model development, especially in settings of recursive self-improvement. We present the first study of SPB in rubric-based evaluation, an increasingly popular benchmarking paradigm where judges issue binary verdicts on individual evaluation criteria, instead of assigning holistic scores or rankings. Using IFEval, a benchmark with programmatically verifiable rubrics, we show that SPB persists even when evaluation criteria are entirely objective: among rubrics where generators fail, judges can be up to 50\% more likely to incorrectly mark them as satisfied when the output is their own. We also find that, similarly to other evaluation paradigms, ensembling multiple judges helps mitigate SPB, but without fully eliminating it. On HealthBench, a medical chat benchmark with subjective rubrics, we observe that SPB skews model scores by up to 10 points, a potentially decisive margin when ranking frontier models. We analyze the factors that drive SPB in this setting, finding that negative rubrics, extreme rubric lengths, and subjective topics like emergency referrals are particularly susceptible.

AI.tldr: The paper studies self-preference bias (SPB) in LLM-as-a-judge settings with rubric-based evaluations and shows that models unfairly favor their own outputs, even with objective criteria, and that ensembling only partially mitigates this problem.
AI.motivation: LLM-as-a-judge is widely used to evaluate LLM outputs, yet judges often show self-preference, favoring responses from themselves or related models. This bias can distort benchmarks and undermine model development, especially for recursive self-improvement. Prior work focused on holistic scores or rankings, leaving unclear how SPB behaves in rubric-based evaluations, which are becoming more popular and can be programmatically verified for objectivity. Understanding whether and how SPB persists in such settings is crucial for reliable evaluation and fair model comparison.
AI.method: The authors investigate SPB in rubric-based evaluations where LLM judges issue binary pass/fail decisions on individual criteria. They use IFEval, a benchmark with automatically verifiable, fully objective rubrics, to quantify how often judges incorrectly mark failed criteria as satisfied when judging their own outputs versus others. They then study whether aggregating multiple judges (ensembling) reduces SPB. Finally, they analyze SPB on HealthBench, a medical chat benchmark with more subjective rubrics, quantifying score distortions and examining which rubric properties (polarity, length, topic subjectivity) are most associated with stronger SPB.
AI.result: On IFEval, the authors find that SPB persists even with entirely objective, programmatically checkable rubrics: when generators fail certain rubrics, judges are up to 50% more likely to incorrectly mark these as satisfied if the output was produced by the same model family. Ensembling multiple judges reduces, but does not eliminate, this bias. On HealthBench, SPB introduces substantial distortions in model evaluation, shifting scores by as much as 10 points, enough to affect frontier model rankings. They further identify that negative rubrics, extremely short or long rubrics, and rubrics involving subjective or sensitive medical topics such as emergency referrals are especially prone to SPB effects.
AI.conclusion: Self-preference bias is robust and significant in rubric-based LLM-as-a-judge evaluations, even when criteria are fully objective and programmatically verifiable. This bias can materially distort benchmark outcomes and model rankings, raising concerns for current evaluation practices and for recursive self-improvement scenarios where models judge their own outputs. While ensembling multiple judges offers partial mitigation, it is insufficient to fully remove SPB. The paper highlights the need for more robust evaluation protocols and rubric designs that explicitly account for and minimize self-preference bias, particularly in high-stakes domains like medical advice.

title: STRIDE-ED: A Strategy-Grounded Stepwise Reasoning Framework for Empathetic Dialogue Systems
summary: Empathetic dialogue requires not only recognizing a user's emotional state but also making strategy-aware, context-sensitive decisions throughout response generation. However, the lack of a comprehensive empathy strategy framework, explicit task-aligned multi-stage reasoning, and high-quality strategy-aware data fundamentally limits existing approaches, preventing them from effectively modeling empathetic dialogue as a complex, multi-stage cognitive and decision-making process. To address these challenges, we propose STRIDE-ED, a STRategy-grounded, Interpretable, and DEep reasoning framework that models Empathetic Dialogue through structured, strategy-conditioned reasoning. To support effective learning, we develop a strategy-aware data refinement pipeline integrating LLM-based annotation, multi-model consistency-weighted evaluation, and dynamic sampling to construct high-quality training data aligned with empathetic strategies. Furthermore, we adopt a two-stage training paradigm that combines supervised fine-tuning with multi-objective reinforcement learning to better align model behaviors with target emotions, empathetic strategies, and response formats. Extensive experiments demonstrate that STRIDE-ED generalizes across diverse open-source LLMs and consistently outperforms existing methods on both automatic metrics and human evaluations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces STRIDE-ED, a framework that models empathetic dialogue as structured, strategy-conditioned multi-stage reasoning, using a new data refinement pipeline and a two-stage training paradigm to improve empathy in dialogue systems.
AI.motivation: Existing empathetic dialogue systems mainly focus on emotion recognition and surface-level response generation. They lack a comprehensive framework of empathy strategies, explicit multi-stage reasoning aligned with the task, and high-quality, strategy-aware training data. These limitations prevent current models from capturing empathetic dialogue as a complex cognitive and decision-making process, constraining their ability to generate genuinely empathetic and context-appropriate responses.
AI.method: The authors propose STRIDE-ED, a strategy-grounded, interpretable deep reasoning framework for empathetic dialogue. It performs structured, strategy-conditioned reasoning over multiple stages of response generation. To enable this, they build a strategy-aware data refinement pipeline that uses LLM-based annotation, consistency-weighted evaluation across multiple models, and dynamic sampling to create high-quality, strategy-aligned training data. They then apply a two-stage training paradigm: first supervised fine-tuning and then multi-objective reinforcement learning, jointly optimizing for alignment with target emotions, empathetic strategies, and desired response formats.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse open-source LLM backbones show that STRIDE-ED generalizes well and consistently outperforms prior empathetic dialogue methods. The gains are observed on standard automatic metrics and confirmed through human evaluations, indicating both quantitative and qualitative improvements in empathetic response generation.
AI.conclusion: Modeling empathetic dialogue as structured, strategy-conditioned multi-stage reasoning—supported by refined, strategy-aware data and a two-stage SFT+RL training pipeline—leads to more effective and interpretable empathetic dialogue systems. STRIDE-ED offers a generalizable framework that can be applied to different LLMs and improves performance over existing approaches on both automatic and human-evaluated measures.

title: The Impact of Steering Large Language Models with Persona Vectors in Educational Applications
summary: Activation-based steering can personalize large language models at inference time, but its effects in educational settings remain unclear. We study persona vectors for seven character traits in short-answer generation and automated scoring on the ASAP-SAS benchmark across three models spanning two architectures. Persona steering lowers answer quality overall, with much larger effects on open-ended English Language Arts (ELA) prompts than on factual science prompts; interpretive and argumentative tasks are up to 11x more sensitive. On the scoring side, we observe predictable valence-aligned calibration shifts: evil and impolite scorers grade more harshly, while good and optimistic scorers grade more leniently. ELA tasks are 2.5-3x more susceptible to scorer personalization than science tasks, and the Mixture-of-Experts model shows roughly 6x larger calibration shifts than the dense models. To our knowledge, this is the first study to systematically examine the effects of activation-steered persona traits in educational generation and scoring, and the results highlight the need for task-aware and architecture-aware calibration when deploying steered models in educational settings.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how activation-based persona steering affects both answer generation quality and automated scoring in educational tasks, finding that steering generally harms answer quality and shifts scoring calibration in predictable, trait-aligned ways, especially in English Language Arts tasks and in Mixture-of-Experts models.
AI.motivation: While activation-based steering allows personalization of large language models at inference time, its concrete impact on educational applications—such as student answer generation and automated grading—has not been well understood. Because education is a high-stakes domain that increasingly uses LLMs, it is crucial to know whether persona steering degrades answer quality or biases scores, and whether these effects vary by task type or model architecture.
AI.method: The authors construct persona vectors corresponding to seven character traits and apply activation-based steering to three different language models representing two architectures (dense vs Mixture-of-Experts). They evaluate the impact of these persona vectors on (1) short-answer generation quality and (2) automated scoring behavior using the ASAP-SAS benchmark. They compare performance across prompt types (open-ended ELA vs factual science) and analyze how each persona trait shifts grading calibration and answer sensitivity.
AI.result: Persona steering degrades answer quality overall, particularly for open-ended ELA prompts; interpretive and argumentative tasks can be up to 11 times more sensitive to steering than factual science prompts. On the scoring side, persona-steered models show systematic, valence-aligned calibration shifts: personas like "evil" and "impolite" grade more harshly, while "good" and "optimistic" personas grade more leniently. ELA scoring is 2.5–3 times more affected by personalization than science scoring. Additionally, the Mixture-of-Experts model exhibits calibration shifts roughly six times larger than those seen in dense models.
AI.conclusion: Activation-based persona steering, while attractive for personalization, introduces notable degradation in answer quality and systematic, trait-aligned biases in automated scoring, especially for open-ended ELA tasks and in Mixture-of-Experts architectures. These findings imply that steered models in educational settings require careful, task-aware and architecture-aware calibration and monitoring, and that naive use of persona steering may be inappropriate in high-stakes educational applications.

title: Fast-dVLM: Efficient Block-Diffusion VLM via Direct Conversion from Autoregressive VLM
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) predominantly rely on autoregressive decoding, which generates tokens one at a time and fundamentally limits inference throughput. This limitation is especially acute in physical AI scenarios such as robotics and autonomous driving, where VLMs are deployed on edge devices at batch size one, making AR decoding memory-bandwidth-bound and leaving hardware parallelism underutilized. While block-wise discrete diffusion has shown promise for parallel text generation, extending it to VLMs remains challenging due to the need to jointly handle continuous visual representations and discrete text tokens while preserving pretrained multimodal capabilities. We present Fast-dVLM, a block-diffusion-based VLM that enables KV-cache-compatible parallel decoding and speculative block decoding for inference acceleration. We systematically compare two AR-to-diffusion conversion strategies: a two-stage approach that first adapts the LLM backbone with text-only diffusion fine-tuning before multimodal training, and a direct approach that converts the full AR VLM in one stage. Under comparable training budgets, direct conversion proves substantially more efficient by leveraging the already multimodally aligned VLM; we therefore adopt it as our recommended recipe. We introduce a suite of multimodal diffusion adaptations, block size annealing, causal context attention, auto-truncation masking, and vision efficient concatenation, that collectively enable effective block diffusion in the VLM setting. Extensive experiments across 11 multimodal benchmarks show Fast-dVLM matches its autoregressive counterpart in generation quality. With SGLang integration and FP8 quantization, Fast-dVLM achieves over 6x end-to-end inference speedup over the AR baseline.

AI.tldr: Fast-dVLM replaces slow autoregressive decoding in vision-language models with block-wise diffusion-based decoding to enable highly parallel, KV-cache-compatible, and speculative generation, achieving up to 6x faster inference while preserving multimodal quality.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive decoding in VLMs is inherently sequential and memory‑bandwidth‑bound, severely limiting throughput on edge devices and in physical AI applications like robotics and autonomous driving, where batch size is typically one and hardware parallelism is underused. There is a need for a decoding scheme that can exploit parallelism yet still work with pre-trained multimodal models that mix continuous visual features and discrete text tokens.
AI.method: The paper proposes Fast-dVLM, which converts an autoregressive VLM into a block-wise discrete diffusion model that can decode multiple tokens in parallel. It systematically compares two strategies for converting AR models to diffusion: (1) a two-stage scheme that first diffusion‑fine‑tunes the text-only LLM backbone and then does multimodal training, and (2) a one-stage direct conversion of the full multimodal AR VLM. Fast-dVLM adopts the direct strategy as it is more efficient under equal training budgets. To make block diffusion work for VLMs, the authors introduce several architectural and training adaptations: multimodal diffusion adjustments for handling both continuous vision features and discrete text; block size annealing to stabilize training; causal context attention to preserve left‑to‑right conditioning; auto‑truncation masking to handle variable-length outputs; and vision‑efficient concatenation for better integration of visual tokens. The model is made compatible with KV caches and speculative block decoding to maximize inference speed, and it is further integrated with SGLang and FP8 quantization for practical deployment.
AI.result: On 11 multimodal benchmarks, Fast-dVLM achieves generation quality comparable to its original autoregressive VLM baseline, indicating that the AR-to-diffusion conversion does not significantly hurt performance. Inference measurements show that, when combined with SGLang and FP8 quantization, Fast-dVLM delivers over 6× end-to-end speedup compared with the autoregressive counterpart, especially benefiting scenarios where batch size is one and parallelism is otherwise underutilized.
AI.conclusion: Fast-dVLM demonstrates that block-wise diffusion decoding can effectively replace autoregressive decoding in vision-language models without sacrificing multimodal generation quality, while enabling substantial inference acceleration. Direct conversion of an existing multimodally aligned AR VLM into a diffusion model is more training-efficient than a two-stage backbone-first approach. With multimodal-specific diffusion adaptations and systems-level optimizations like KV-cache compatibility and FP8 quantization, Fast-dVLM makes parallel block decoding a practical solution for speeding up VLM inference, particularly in edge and physical AI applications.

title: Dynamic Context Evolution for Scalable Synthetic Data Generation
summary: Large language models produce repetitive output when prompted independently across many batches, a phenomenon we term cross-batch mode collapse: the progressive loss of output diversity when a language model is prompted repeatedly without access to its prior generations. Practitioners have long mitigated this with ad hoc deduplication and seed rotation, but no principled framework exists. We introduce Dynamic Context Evolution (DCE), comprising three mechanisms: (1) verbalized tail sampling (the model labels each idea with a guess about how obvious it is, and obvious ideas are discarded), which filters high-probability candidates via model self-assessment; (2) semantic memory, which maintains a persistent embedding index to reject near-duplicates across batches; and (3) adaptive prompt evolution, which reconstructs the generation prompt each batch using memory state and rotating diversity strategies. In experiments across three domains (sustainable packaging concepts, educational exam questions, and creative writing prompts) and two model families (gpt-5-mini and claude-haiku-4-5), a component ablation across 2-3 random seeds per method shows that DCE achieves 0.0 +/- 0.0% collapse versus 5.6 +/- 2.0% for naive prompting, while producing 17-18 HDBSCAN clusters per seed versus naive's volatile 2-17, indicating reliably richer conceptual structure. These results are validated with an independent embedding model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) and hold across sensitivity sweeps of the VTS threshold tau and dedup threshold delta. Deduplication and prompt evolution are individually insufficient but jointly effective, at approximately $0.50 per 1,000 candidates using only standard API calls, with no fine-tuning or custom architectures required.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and tackles cross-batch mode collapse in large language models, proposing Dynamic Context Evolution (DCE)—a lightweight, API-level framework that maintains output diversity across many generations.
AI.motivation: When large language models are prompted repeatedly in separate batches without shared context, they tend to converge on similar, repetitive answers, reducing usefulness in applications that need many diverse ideas or items (e.g., product concepts, exam questions, creative prompts). Current fixes like deduplication or random seed changes are ad hoc and lack a principled, generalizable method, motivating a systematic approach to preserving diversity across batches.
AI.method: The authors define cross-batch mode collapse and propose Dynamic Context Evolution (DCE), which has three components: (1) Verbalized Tail Sampling (VTS), where the model annotates each idea with how obvious it is and filters out highly obvious (high-probability) ones based on a threshold; (2) a semantic memory implemented as an embedding index that persists across batches and rejects new candidates that are near-duplicates of previous ones; and (3) adaptive prompt evolution, which rebuilds and varies the prompt each batch according to the evolving memory and diversity strategies. They test DCE versus naive prompting in three application domains and across two LLM families, using clustering (HDBSCAN) of embeddings to quantify conceptual diversity and performing ablations and sensitivity analyses on key thresholds.
AI.result: Across three domains (sustainable packaging, educational exam questions, creative writing prompts) and two LLMs (gpt-5-mini, claude-haiku-4-5), DCE eliminates measured collapse (0.0 ± 0.0% vs. 5.6 ± 2.0% for naive prompting) and yields consistently more and richer clusters (17–18 HDBSCAN clusters per seed vs. naive’s unstable 2–17). These gains persist under different settings of the VTS obviousness threshold (τ) and semantic deduplication threshold (δ). Ablation studies show that deduplication alone or prompt evolution alone cannot achieve the same robustness; both mechanisms are required. The method is cost-effective, at about $0.50 per 1,000 generated candidates, and uses only off-the-shelf APIs plus an external embedding model for evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic Context Evolution provides a practical, principled framework to maintain and enhance cross-batch diversity of LLM generations without modifying model weights. By combining self-assessed tail sampling, persistent semantic memory, and adaptive prompt evolution, it reliably prevents mode collapse, yields more stable and diverse conceptual structures, and remains inexpensive and easy to deploy with standard model APIs. This suggests that managing context and memory across batches can substantially improve generative diversity without architectural changes or fine-tuning.

title: Evaluating In-Context Translation with Synchronous Context-Free Grammar Transduction
summary: Low-resource languages pose a challenge for machine translation with large language models (LLMs), which require large amounts of training data. One potential way to circumvent this data dependence is to rely on LLMs' ability to use in-context descriptions of languages, like textbooks and dictionaries. To do so, LLMs must be able to infer the link between the languages' grammatical descriptions and the sentences in question. Here we isolate this skill using a formal analogue of the task: string transduction based on a formal grammar provided in-context. We construct synchronous context-free grammars which define pairs of formal languages designed to model particular aspects of natural language grammar, morphology, and written representation. Using these grammars, we measure how well LLMs can translate sentences from one formal language into another when given both the grammar and the source-language sentence. We vary the size of the grammar, the lengths of the sentences, the syntactic and morphological properties of the languages, and their written script. We note three key findings. First, LLMs' translation accuracy decreases markedly as a function of grammar size and sentence length. Second, differences in morphology and written representation between the source and target languages can strongly diminish model performance. Third, we examine the types of errors committed by models and find they are most prone to recall the wrong words from the target language vocabulary, hallucinate new words, or leave source-language words untranslated.

AI.tldr: The paper tests how well large language models can learn to translate between formal languages when given an explicit grammar in the prompt, as a proxy for using textbook-style descriptions to handle low-resource natural languages.
AI.motivation: Low-resource languages lack large parallel corpora needed to train high-performing machine translation systems. A possible workaround is to leverage LLMs' ability to reason over textual descriptions of grammar (like textbooks, dictionaries, or reference grammars) provided in-context, instead of relying solely on data-intensive training. To understand whether this is feasible, the authors need a controlled way to test if LLMs can interpret and apply explicit grammatical rules to perform translation, without the confounds of real-world language data.
AI.method: The authors construct synchronous context-free grammars (SCFGs) that define pairs of formal languages. Each pair is engineered to mimic specific aspects of natural language: syntax, morphology, and writing systems. Given such a grammar and a source-language string, an LLM is asked to output the corresponding target-language string.
